outputs\campaigns\CAMP_20260310T073230_fb78bf\WEB_20260310T073230_5da0fc.json:1695:      "source_content": "Susannah\nPowell\na,\nHongyi\nBan\nac,\nYi\nSang\na,\nDaewon\nKim\nb,\nPhillip J.\nMilner\nb,\nMatthew\nReid\na and\nDamian E.\nHelbling\n*a\naSchool of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Cornell University, Ithaca, NY 14853, USA. E-mail: damian.helbling@cornell.edu; Fax: +1 607 255 9004; Tel: +1 607 255 5146\nbDepartment of Chemistry and Chemical Biology, Cornell University, Ithaca, NY 14853, USA\ncDepartment of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Yale University, New Haven, CT 06511, USA\nFirst published on 16th December 2025\nThe goals of this study were to measure sorption kinetics, solid–water partitioning (logKd values) as a function of pH, and percent desorption for a diverse set of PFASs on four highly abundant soil and aquifer minerals. We found that PFAS sorption was relatively fast on all four minerals and that overall logKd values were higher for ferrihydrite and montmorillonite than for goethite and kaolinite, possibly driven by differences in surface area. We also found that logKd values on ferrihydrite and goethite were dependent on pH levels and the length of the perfluoroalkyl chain. Significant differences in logKd values between the iron oxide minerals were explained by differences in their respective point-of-zero-charge, and changes in PFAS speciation as a function of pH amplified those differences. Despite the relatively high logKd values of the iron oxide minerals reflecting relatively high affinity for PFASs, facile desorption from the iron oxides suggests that PFAS sorption is driven by relatively weak electrostatic interactions. The logKd values on montmorillonite and kaolinite were not significantly dependent on pH levels, but were dependent on the length of the perfluoroalkyl chain. Less facile desorption from the silica clay minerals suggests that PFAS sorption is driven by relatively strong hydrophobic and electrostatic interactions. Together, our data make practical contributions to support site characterization and remediation efforts, while also contributing key insights into the fundamental sorption processes.\nEnvironmental significanceWe quantified sorption kinetics, solid–water partitioning (logKd values) as a function of pH, and the extent of desorption for a diverse set of dozens of per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs) on two iron oxide minerals (ferrihydrite and goethite) and two silica clay minerals (montmorillonite and kaolinite). The results demonstrate that pH-dependent changes in the surface charge of the mineral and speciation of the PFASs play an important role in PFAS sorption and that iron oxide minerals exhibit relatively facile desorption when compared to silica clay minerals. Together, our data make practical contributions to support site characterization and remediation efforts, while also contributing key insights into the fundamental sorption processes. |\nOnce PFASs reach groundwater, their ultimate fate is determined by a number of co-occurring environmental processes. For example, PFASs can sorb to soils and aquifer minerals to varying extents based on the surface chemistry of the soils and aquifer minerals, the chemical nature of the individual PFAS, and local biogeochemical conditions including parameters such as pH, ionic strength, and the presence of other dissolved organic and inorganic substances.14–16 PFASs that sorb under prevailing conditions will be partially sequestered near the source zone and their transport away from the source zone will be retarded as a function of their solid–water partition coefficient (Kd).17 These types of PFASs can sustain a source zone for many years as they slowly leach away from the source zone. PFASs that do not sorb under prevailing conditions will be transported by means of advection, diffusion, and dispersion and will migrate greater distances over shorter periods of time. These types of PFASs can impact water quality in ground water resources at distances rather far from the source zone.18 To complicate matters further, some PFASs that sorb to soils or aquifer minerals can be (bio)transformed into PFASs that do not sorb as strongly and those PFASs can slowly leach away from the source zone for durations that are linked to the (bio)transformation kinetics.19\nIron oxides and silica clays are highly abundant soil and aquifer minerals that are increasingly recognized as subsurface features that strongly influence the transport and retardation of PFASs on the subsurface.20–22 Ferrihydrite is a poorly crystalline, amorphous iron oxide mineral.23 It is an effective sorbent due to its large surface area and mesoporous structure.24,25 Ferrihydrite has a point-of-zero-charge (PZC) that ranges between 7.3 and 8 and is consequently a particularly effective sorbent for anions in the environment.25,26 Goethite is a more stable, thermodynamically favorable product of ferrihydrite and other iron oxides.27 Goethite exhibits a well-ordered crystalline structure and often forms needle-like particles that can play a significant role in controlling the mobility and retention of contaminants on the subsurface.28 Goethite is likewise an effective sorbent but has a lower surface area and pore volume when compared to ferrihydrite. Variable coverage of surface hydroxyl groups on goethite can result in PZC values ranging between 7.5–9.5.29–31\nMontmorillonite is a smectite phyllosilicate group clay mineral with a structure that includes two O–Si–O tetrahedral sheets sandwiching an O–Al(Mg)–O octahedral sheet,32 with cations located in the interlayered space.33 The sheets are linked together by van der Waals and electrostatic forces and the basal surface of the siloxane tetrahedral sheets is capable of interacting with nonpolar chemicals by means of hydrophobic interactions.34,35 The average reported PZC for montmorillonite is 3.4 due to the presence of surface hydroxyl groups and isomorphic substitution.36 Kaolinite is a clay mineral which features a flat plate-like structure of two basal planes, a Si–O silica tetrahedral sheet, an Al–OH gibbsite facet sheet, and edge sites that contain hydroxyl groups that can be protonated below a pH of 6–7.37 The presence of hydroxyl groups at the basal surface of the Al–OH gibbsite facet sheet results in a hydrophilic character, while the basal surface of the Si–O silica tetrahedral sheet results in a hydrophobic character.38 Although the positively charged edge sites may allow for some electrostatic interactions with anions, these sites are limited and kaolinite has an overall PZC of less than 4. In this context, sorption of anions is likely dominated by hydrophobic interactions.39\nDue to the hydrophilic nature of ferrihydrite and goethite along with their reported PZC values in the range of 7.3–9.5, sorption of PFASs is expected to be driven by electrostatic interactions and H-bonding in a pH-dependent way.40,41 Previous studies with sets of anionic perfluoroalkyl acids (PFAAs) have demonstrated that sorption on ferrihydrite and goethite can be driven by various types of electrostatic interactions including the formation of outer-sphere and inner-sphere complexes.20,31,42 PFAS sorption on montmorillonite and kaolinite can be more complex.43 The relatively low PZC for montmorillonite and kaolinite makes electrostatic interactions with anionic PFASs unfavorable, although the positive surface charge on the edge sites of kaolinite can support point interactions with anionic PFASs. The basal surface of the Si–O silica tetrahedral sheet of kaolinite can also support hydrophobic interactions with PFASs.\nSorption to aquifer minerals is one of the most important factors for understanding PFAS fate and transport in subsurface environments.21 Although the sorption and desorption of certain PFASs on specific aquifer minerals including iron oxides, metal carbonates, silica clays, and organic-rich soils were previously reported, our current knowledge of PFAS sorption and desorption on aquifer minerals remains limited in scope (i.e., diverse types of PFASs) and depth (i.e., integrated studies of sorption and desorption as a function of pH).44,45 The goals of this study were to measure logKd values as a function of pH and percent desorption for a diverse set of up to 40 PFASs on four highly abundant aquifer minerals including ferrihydrite, goethite, montmorillonite, and kaolinite. The PFASs were selected to represent seven classes that were defined by their functional groups and their potential to be neutral, anionic, cationic, or zwitterionic as a function of pH. The model minerals were selected due to their relative abundance in subsurface environments and to represent sorbents that can participate in a variety of sorption mechanisms including electrostatic interactions resulting in the formation of outer-sphere or inner-sphere complexes, general hydrophobic interactions, H-bonding, and ligand exchange. By investigating the sorption of a broad range of PFASs across the four model minerals, we generated essential data to support fate and transport modelling of PFASs in subsurface environments while also contributing key insights into the fundamental sorption processes.\n| Fig. 1 Chemical structures of representative PFASs included in this study. Chemical structures are drawn as neutral molecules unless there is a cationic quaternary amine group in the structure. |\nWe selected equilibrium times of two days for ferrihydrite, four days for goethite, seven days for kaolinite, and seven days for montmorillonite (Table S8). Most PFASs exhibit significantly similar extents of sorption from day 1 to day 2, indicating that sorption equilibrium is reached within 48 hours for many PFASs on all four minerals (23 out of 29 for ferrihydrite, 24 out of 27 for goethite, 20 out of 31 for kaolinite, and 19 out of 32 for montmorillonite). However, some PFASs exhibit distinct rate-limiting behaviors that result in continued sorption over seven days. This phenomenon was observed for 6:2 FTCA and 6:2 FTS on goethite, 6:2 FTCA, 4:2 FTS, and 8:2 FTS",
outputs\campaigns\CAMP_20260310T073230_fb78bf\WEB_20260310T073230_5da0fc.json:2054:      "source_content": "Craig\nKlevan\n,\nOren\nVan Allen\n,\nKelly\nMukai\n,\nAndre\nGomes\n,\nShana\nXia\n,\nSeth\nCaines\n,\nMatthew J.\nWoodcock\nand\nKurt D.\nPennell\n*\nBrown University, School of Engineering, Providence, RI, USA. E-mail: kurt_pennell@brown.edu\nFirst published on 28th July 2025\nDue to the tendency for per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) to accumulate at the air–water interface, foam fractionation has gained attention as a cost-effective approach to treat PFAS-impacted water. Although foam fractionation is effective for long-chain length PFAS, short-chain PFAS are less surface active and can remain in solution even after extended treatment times. The objective of this study was to evaluate the ability of six cationic surfactants to remove both long- and short-chain length PFAS from groundwater by foam fractionation. Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) accumulated the most at the air–water interface and was subsequently evaluated in a foam fractionation system over a 4 order of magnitude range of PFAS concentrations, as well as 4 different CTAB mass delivery rates. For two natural groundwaters impacted by aqueous film forming foam (AFFF), total PFAS concentrations were reduced from ∼15000 ng L−1 to less than the detection limit of 8 ng L−1 in as short as 15 minutes. These findings demonstrate that the addition of CTAB, which exhibits a strong affinity for the air–water interface, to foam fractionation systems achieves rapid removal of both long- and short-chain PFAS from synthetic and natural groundwaters.\nWater impactPer- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) concentrations in groundwater may span orders of magnitude. This work exhibits the versatility of cationic surfactant-enhanced foam fractionation over a range of long-chain and short-chain PFAS concentrations in groundwater including water contaminated with aqueous film-forming foam (AFFF) and provides key insights into avoiding spreading contamination through aerosolization and minimizing residual water pollution with cationic surfactants used for treatment. |\nAn important property of PFAS is their amphiphilic structure, which arises from a hydrophobic carbon fluorine chain and a hydrophilic head group, most commonly a carboxylic acid or sulfonic acid. This structure leads to surface active behavior, including accumulation at the air–water interface where the hydrophobic chain is oriented toward the nonpolar air and the hydrophilic head group is oriented toward the polar water.8–10 The extent to which PFAS accumulate at interfaces is heavily dependent on the molecular structure of the individual PFAS compounds (e.g. head group, linear or branched), and for common anionic PFAS such of perfluoroalkyl carboxylic acids (PFCAs) and perfluoroalkyl sulfonic acids (PFSAs) is generally proportional to the length of the carbon–fluorine chain, with longer chain PFAS exhibiting greater hydrophobicity and greater accumulation at interfaces.11,12 Shorter chain-length PFAS tend to exhibit less adsorption at interfaces, and therefore are often more mobile in the environment.13,14\nDue to the tendency for many PFAS to accumulate at the air–water interface, foam fractionation has emerged as a promising method to remove PFAS from contaminated water. Foam fractionation involves the injection of air into the bottom of a vessel containing contaminated water, where PFAS then accumulate at the air–water interface of the air bubbles as they rise through the water column, with a goal of creating a PFAS-enriched foam at the top of the water column. Removal efficiencies for long-chain PFAS typically approach >90%, whereas short-chain PFAS may only achieve removal efficiencies on the order of 5–50% due to their lower affinity for the air–water interface.15–20 Foam fractionation operating conditions (e.g., air flow rate, bubble size) can strongly influence PFAS removal efficiencies.16,18,21,22 Likewise, the extent of PFAS accumulation at the air–water interface can be influenced by solution properties, such as ionic strength or competing species.15,16,23\nSince most PFAS possess a negative charge in aqueous solutions, cationic surfactants, such as cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), have been found to be effective in enhancing removal of short-chain PFAS.15,17 Complex solutions, such as landfill leachate, have been shown to reduce the ability of cationic co-surfactants to enhance the removal of short-chain PFAS.10,24–26 Due to these issues, it is often necessary to pilot test foam fractionation systems and adjust parameters until removal targets have been achieved. For example, the performance of CTAB with PFAS-impacted groundwater has not been extensively studied, nor has removal performance been demonstrated over a range of PFAS concentrations, in particular, dilute concentrations that are typically observed in groundwater plumes.16\nThus, the overall goal of this work was to evaluate the ability of six cationic co-surfactants (3 poly(diallyldimethylammonium chloride) (polyDADMAC) and 3 trimethylammonium compounds) to enhance the removal of both long- and short-chain PFAS during foam fractionation of synthetic and natural PFAS-impacted groundwaters. Surface tension data were measured for each co-surfactant, and this information was used to guide co-surfactant selection for foam fractionation tests with synthetic groundwater containing long- and short-chain PFAS over four orders of magnitude concentration range. Based on these results, foam fractionation with CTAB was evaluated using PFAS-impacted groundwater from two military installations, and at four mass flow rates to further explore system optimization.\nFoam fractionation experiments were carried out in semi-batch mode. For each experiment, the column was filled up to approximately 95% capacity (the true volume is recorded gravimetrically) with the synthetic groundwater containing mixtures or the AFFF impacted groundwater, after which an inverted funnel was quickly sealed to the top of the column to allow the column to operate under vacuum conditions. At the start of each experiment compressed air flow was initiated at the same time as the injection of a co-foaming agent if one was being used. The vacuum pump at this time was turned on to remove foam generated by the system. The time taken to add the solution to the system and turn on the equipment is less than 30 seconds. The experimental conditions, including air flow rate, air pressure, and vacuum pressure are summarized in the SI section SI: Experimental parameters. For each experiment, the foam fractionation system was operated for up to 2 hours, with ∼1 mL samples collected in triplicate from the bottom and upper sampling ports in the water column at 2, 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 75, 90, 105, and 120 minutes of elapsed operation time with higher resolution early in the experiment as preliminary studies suggest this is when removal is most rapid. Foam generation was monitored over the duration of the experiment. At the conclusion of the experiment, the foam collapsed with the addition of methanol and then was diluted for subsequent quantification. Initial PFAS concentrations are provided in the SI section SI: Experimental parameters. Characteristics of bubbles were determined through image analysis with ImageJ as described in SI section SIII; Additional bubble and foam analysis.29\n| (1) |\nIt is evident from Fig. 2 that long-chain PFAS such as PFOA (Fig. 2a) and PFOS (Fig. 2b) exhibit much greater accumulation to the air water interface than PFBS (Fig. 2c) and PFBA (Fig. 2d). Likewise, the sulfonic acid compounds (PFOS and PFBS) exhibit greater surface tension reduction relative to their analogous carboxylic acid compounds (PFOA and PFBA, respectively) at the same concentrations. The short-chain compounds only accumulate at the air–water interface enough to see a significant decrease in surface tension at concentrations several orders of magnitude higher than the concentration range relevant to the experiments in this study (Fig. S7). Furthermore, in mixtures, PFAS have been shown to exhibit competitive behavior at the air–water interface.35–37 For example, in a system with limited adsorption sites and both PFOS and PFOA present, PFOS having greater interfacial affinity will outcompete PFOA for adsorption sites and may kick PFOA molecules off of the air–water interface.32 Therefore, especially in mixtures with short-chain PFAS, these compounds may exhibit even less interfacial accumulation as the available interfacial area is preferentially occupied by long-chain PFAS.38,39\nThe observed differences in accumulation are important because cationic surfactants may interact with short-chain PFAS in the bulk aqueous phase and then accumulate as a pair to the air–water interface. Therefore, a cationic surfactant with high affinity for the air–water interface is likely to be more effective in removing short-chain PFAS during foam fractionation, compared to a cationic surfactant that exhibits lower surface activity.17,40 As can been seen in Fig. 3, CTAB exhibits far greater surface activity than any of the other co-surfactants tested. Additionally, co-surfactants that strongly accumulate at air–water interface also support the formation of stable foams.46 Foamability, the foam generation capacity, and foam stability, the lifetime of the foam, must be considered during foam fractionation. Likewise foam properties such as liquid content are important to consider as foam drainage may reintroduce PFAS back into the bulk liquid.47–49 Foam consistency is explored further in the SI section: SIII: Additional bubble and foam analysis.\nThe small rises and falls in concentration of PFOS and PFOA resulting in a relatively flat portion of the curve after the first several minutes is likely due to the recycling of the PFAS in the system as the aerosolized droplets make their way back into the solution for the PFAS to then be removed again with the bubbles. In a similar nonfoaming system it was observed that",

outputs\campaigns\CAMP_20260310T073230_fb78bf\WEB_20260310T073230_5da0fc.json:2186:      "source_content": "Abstract\nDetermining the risk posed by PFAS leaching from soil to groundwater requires quantification of the magnitude and temporal/spatial variability of PFAS mass discharge from the vadose zone, which is governed in part by the concentrations of PFAS in soil porewater. Porewater concentrations are impacted and mediated by the properties of the PFAS and soil, multiple transport and fate processes, and site conditions. The objective of this research was to delineate the relationship between soil porewater concentrations and soil concentrations, based on a comprehensive model of PFAS mass distribution within a soil sample volume. Measured parameters representing solid-phase sorption and air-water interfacial adsorption are used to illustrate the impact of soil and PFAS properties on the distribution of representative PFAS between soil and soil porewater. Literature data reported for soil and soil porewater concentrations of several PFAS obtained from outdoor lysimeter experiments are used to test the distribution model. Soil-to-porewater concentration ratios predicted with the model compared very well to the measured concentration ratios. The nondimensional distribution coefficient that describes the distribution of PFAS mass amongst all domains within a soil sample was observed to be a function of PFAS molecular size. Numerical simulations conducted for a model fire-training source area were used to illustrate the ranges in magnitude of soil versus porewater concentrations for representative field conditions. The results of the measured and simulated data sets demonstrated the importance of air-water interfacial adsorption for the distribution of the longer-chain PFAS within soil samples. PFAS soil porewater concentrations are anticipated to range from ng/L to mg/L depending upon soil concentrations, which in turn depend upon the nature of the site.\nKeywords: PFOS, PFOA, Perfluorinated, Sorption, Air-water Interfacial adsorption, Leaching\n1. Introduction\nPer and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) have been demonstrated to be widespread in soils across the globe (Brusseau et al., 2020). PFAS concentrations are often, but not always, observed to be highest near the surface and to diminish with depth. This is particularly the case for longer-chain PFAS. This distribution has been observed for different types of source sites, including sites impacted by fire-fighting foam applications (Filipovic et al., 2015; Baduel et al., 2017; Dauchy et al., 2019; Brusseau et al., 2020), manufacturing facilities (Davis et al. 2007), and land application of biosolids (Washington et al., 2010; Sepulvado et al. 2011; Pepper et al., 2021; Johnson, 2022). In addition, the concentrations of PFAS in soils are typically significantly higher than those in underlying groundwater, often by orders of magnitude (Anderson et al., 2019; Brusseau et al., 2020). These observations demonstrate that soils are a primary reservoir of PFAS at numerous sites. A critical concern for these sites is the potential for leaching of PFAS from soil, through the vadose zone, to groundwater, as illustrated by field-site investigations (Xiao et al., 2015; Baduel et al., 2017; Weber et al., 2017; Hoisaeter et al., 2019; Dauchy et al., 2019) and mathematical-modeling studies (Guo et al., 2020; Silva et al., 2020; Zeng et al., 2021).\nThe transport of PFAS in soils and the vadose zone is complex, as it is influenced by several potentially nonlinear, rate-limited interconnected processes. Transport experiments, mathematical-modeling studies, and field investigations have for example illustrated the impacts of solid-phase sorption, adsorption at air-water interfaces, and precursor transformation on PFAS migration (e,g., Gellrich et al., 2012; Vierke et al., 2014; Lyu et al., 2018; Brusseau et al., 2019a, 2019b; Hoisaeter et al., 2019; McLachlan et al., 2019; Guelfo et al., 2020; Guo et al., 2020; Silva et al., 2020; Brusseau et al., 2021; Nickerson et al., 2021). Determining the risk posed by PFAS leaching to groundwater requires quantification of the magnitude and temporal/spatial variability of PFAS mass discharge from the vadose zone. This mass discharge is governed by fluid discharge and the concentrations of PFAS in soil porewater, the latter of which is mediated by operative retention and transformation processes.\nPFAS soil porewater concentrations and associated mass discharge can be quantified through the application of mathematical models. Comprehensive models that incorporate mechanistic representations of all relevant processes are important for determining the impact and importance of the various processes influencing transport. However, their application requires significant information for parameter input, which is not always practical. An alternative is the use of screening-level models that have reduced input requirements. For example, a screening model employing analytical solutions for predicting long-term leaching of PFAS has been recently developed (Guo et al., 2022). While the level of input is reduced, there remains the need for characterization efforts to supply required parameters.\nDirect measurement of PFAS concentrations in soil porewater is an alternative approach for characterizing mass discharge (Anderson et al., 2021; Quinnan et al., 2021). Porewater sampling can be used in lieu of, or preferably in conjunction with, mathematical modeling for site characterization. Effective use of porewater concentration data requires an understanding of the distribution of PFAS mass within a volume element of soil, and the relationship between soil and porewater concentrations. This relationship is more complex for PFAS compared to most other types of solutes due to the adsorption of PFAS at air-water interfaces, and the dependence of air-water interfacial area on water content and soil properties. This relationship between soil and soil porewater concentrations has not yet been investigated.\nThe objective of this research is to delineate the relationship between soil porewater concentrations and soil concentrations, based on a comprehensive model of PFAS mass distribution within a soil sample volume. Measured parameters representing solid-phase sorption and air-water interfacial adsorption are used to illustrate the impact of soil and PFAS properties on the distribution of PFAS between soil and soil porewater. Literature data reported for soil and soil porewater concentrations of several PFAS obtained from outdoor lysimeter experiments are used to test the distribution model. Soil-to-porewater concentration ratios predicted with the model are compared to the measured concentration ratios. Numerical simulations conducted for a model fire-training source area are used to illustrate the magnitude of soil versus porewater concentrations for representative field conditions. The importance of air-water interfacial adsorption for mediating the distribution of PFAS within a soil sample is examined using the simulated and measured data sets.\n2. Theory\nWe are interested in the distribution of PFAS among all possible domains within a volume sample of soil in the presence of a variety of phases including air (soil atmosphere), water (porewater), solids, nonaqueous-phase liquid (NAPL), and colloidal and other suspended and dissolved matter. This distribution is described and quantified using the comprehensive retention model of Brusseau (Brusseau, 2018; Brusseau et al., 2019a). The total mass of a select PFAS constituent in a volume of soil is given by:\n| [1] |\nwhere Mt is total mass in the volume of sample (M), Cp is the porewater concentration (M/L3), Cs represents the mass of PFAS sorbed by the soil solids (soil-solids concentration, M/M), Ca is the concentration in the soil atmosphere (M/L3), Cn is the concentration in NAPL present in the sample (M/L3), Caw represents the mass adsorbed at the air-water interface (air-water interfacial concentration (M/L2), Cnw represents the mass adsorbed at the NAPL-water interface (NAPL-water interfacial concentration (M/L2), Can represents the mass adsorbed at the air-NAPL interface (air-NAPL interfacial concentration (M/L2), Cc represents the mass associated with colloidal and other suspended and dissolved matter in solution (M/M), Aaw is the total air-water interfacial area (L2), Anw is the total NAPL-water interfacial area (L2), Aan is the total air-NAPL interfacial area (L2), Vw is the volume of porewater (L3), Va is the volume of air (L3), Vn is the volume of NAPL (L3), Ms is the mass of soil solids (M), and Mc is the mass of colloidal material (M).\nNormalizing the phase volumes, phase masses, and interfacial areas by the total sample volume, Vt, and introducing the equilibrium distribution coefficients results in transformation of equation 1 to:\n| [2] |\nwhere Kd* is the nonlinear solid-phase adsorption coefficient (cm3/g), Kn is the NAPL-water partition coefficient (−), Ka is the air-water partition coefficient (Henry’s coefficient, −), Kaw* is the nonlinear air-water interfacial adsorption coefficient (cm3/cm2), Knw* is the nonlinear NAPL-water interfacial adsorption coefficient (cm3/cm2), Kan* is the nonlinear air-NAPL interfacial adsorption coefficient (cm3/cm2), Kc* is the nonlinear distribution coefficient for sorption by the colloids (cm3/g), aaw is the specific air-water interfacial area (cm2/cm3), anw is the specific NAPL-water interfacial area (cm2/cm3), aan is the specific air-NAPL interfacial area (cm2/cm3), ρb is porous-medium bulk density (g/cm3), θa is volumetric air content (cm3/cm3), θn is volumetric NAPL content (cm3/cm3), and θw is volumetric water content (cm3/cm3). outputs\campaigns\CAMP_20260310T073230_fb78bf\WEB_20260310T073230_5da0fc.json:2772:      "content": "Regulatory classification of PFAS by chain length establishes foundational boundaries that directly govern how environmental fate and transport assessments are conducted in aquifer systems [1]. In addition, the classification hinges on bioaccumulation potential rather than chemical functionality alone [1]. In addition, this bioaccumulation-centered framework creates consequential implications for leaching assessments, as compounds with greater bioaccumulative capacity exhibit fundamentally different subsurface mobility profiles [1].\n\nThe fluorinated carbon count serves as the primary quantitative delimiter between regulatory categories [2]. Perfluorocarboxylic acids (PFCAs) containing fewer than seven fluorinated carbon atoms and perfluoroalkane sulfonic acids (PFSAs) with fewer than six fluorinated carbon atoms receive short-chain designation [2]. In addition, these numerical thresholds—six and seven carbons—emerged from empirical observations correlating molecular size with biological uptake efficiency [1]. While boundary selection originated from metabolic and toxicological research, these same structural parameters that govern bioaccumulation—fluorinated chain length and hydrophobic character—inadvertently influence hydrogeological transport behavior, a relationship explored in subsequent treatment and remediation discussions.\n\nThis classification architecture produces asymmetric regulatory coverage that affects aquifer monitoring priorities. Moreover, long-chain compounds, by virtue of their classification, trigger stricter regulatory thresholds and mandatory detection protocols. The bioaccumulation-based framework implicitly assumes that compounds with lower bioaccumulation potential pose reduced human health risks via dietary exposure, an assumption that proves problematic when evaluating groundwater contamination scenarios where drinking water ingestion constitutes the primary exposure pathway. However, short-chain PFAS demonstrate elevated aqueous mobility and diminished sorption affinity, properties that facilitate rapid vertical migration through unsaturated zones into saturated aquifer materials, as detailed in Sediment Contamination Hotspots regarding their preferential distribution in distal groundwater positions.\n\nThe structural determinants of bioaccumulation parallel—but do not perfectly align with—the structural controls on subsurface transport. Nevertheless, both phenomena respond to fluorinated chain length, yet the optimal chain length for biological membrane partitioning differs from that governing mineral surface interactions. These divergent structure-activity relationships between biological and geochemical systems create treatment efficiency disparities that complicate remediation design, as elaborated in Treatment Efficiency Disparities.\n\nRegulatory thresholds established under this framework may underestimate the aquifer contamination risk posed by short-chain compounds. In addition, when monitoring programs prioritize long-chain PFAS detection based on bioaccumulation classifications, they potentially overlook the primary mobile contaminants driving plume expansion. The classification system's origin in toxicological rather than hydrogeological science creates methodological tensions that persist throughout environmental assessment protocols.\n\nThe six-carbon and seven-carbon boundaries also influence how precursor compounds are evaluated [2]. Nevertheless, polyfluorinated substances with chain lengths below these thresholds may transform into short-chain perfluorinated products, yet regulatory frameworks often treat precursors separately from their terminal degradation products. This fragmentation complicates source attribution in aquifer systems where biotic or abiotic transformation generates short-chain PFAS from longer-chain parent compounds.\n\nInternational regulatory divergence further complicates classification-based assessment. Moreover, while the six- and seven-carbon thresholds predominate in North American frameworks, European jurisdictions have adopted alternative boundary definitions or compound-specific approaches. Additionally, these disparities generate inconsistent monitoring datasets that hinder comparative leaching potential analysis across hydrogeological settings. Standardized classification would facilitate more robust cross-system transport modeling, though consensus remains elusive given competing scientific and economic interests.\n\nThe relationship between molecular structure and regulatory classification extends beyond simple carbon counting to encompass branching patterns, ether linkages, and terminal functional group variations. Furthermore, these structural modifications alter bioaccumulation potential without necessarily changing the nominal chain length, creating classification ambiguities that propagate into environmental monitoring requirements. Aquifer systems receiving mixed industrial discharges thus may contain PFAS species whose regulatory status remains uncertain, complicating compliance determination and remedial prioritization.\n\nAs discussed in Chemical Persistence and Environmental Stability, the classification framework's emphasis on bioaccumulation rather than persistence creates additional assessment challenges. In contrast, the \"forever chemical\" paradigm applies with approximately equal force to both short- and long-chain compounds, yet their differential transport properties ensure that long-chain PFAS accumulate in near-source soils while short-chain homologs migrate to distal aquifer positions. This spatial separation means that bioaccumulation-based regulatory thresholds may adequately protect ecological receptors at the surface while failing to safeguard deeper groundwater resources.\n\nThe classification boundaries established by regulatory frameworks require careful contextual interpretation when applied to aquifer vulnerability assessment. Moreover, the six- and seven-carbon thresholds represent biologically derived conventions that must be supplemented with hydrogeological transport parameters to accurately predict leaching potential. In addition, subsequent sections examine how these structural classifications translate into measurable differences in environmental partitioning, surface activity, and ultimately treatment efficacy.\n\nThe regulatory fixation on carbon-count thresholds overlooks critical molecular geometry parameters that modulate both bioaccumulation and transport behavior. Linear perfluorinated chains exhibit stronger hydrophobic cohesion and membrane partitioning efficiency compared to branched isomers of identical carbon number, yet regulatory frameworks typically do not discriminate by structural isomerism. Moreover, this omission creates classification paradoxes where branched long-chain PFAS may demonstrate reduced bioaccumulation while retaining substantial sorption affinity, whereas linear short-chain compounds show the inverse pattern. The failure to incorporate three-dimensional molecular configuration into classification schemes introduces systematic uncertainty into aquifer vulnerability assessments.\n\nEther-linked perfluoropolyethers present additional classification challenges that regulatory boundaries struggle to accommodate. Additionally, these compounds incorporate oxygen atoms within the fluorinated backbone, effectively interrupting the continuous carbon chain that carbon-counting methodologies presume. Regulatory frameworks diverge in their treatment of such heteroatomic insertions—some jurisdictions count only contiguous perfluorinated segments, while others tally total fluorinated carbons regardless of intervening heteroatoms. In addition, this inconsistency produces compound-specific regulatory status variations that complicate multinational facility compliance and obscure comparative leaching studies across international aquifer systems. The bioaccumulation potential of ether-linked structures deviates unpredictably from their carbon-count analogs due to altered membrane permeability and metabolic transformation pathways.\n\nTemporal dynamics of precursor transformation introduce classification instability that static regulatory thresholds cannot capture. Moreover, polyfluorinated precursors classified separately from their perfluorinated degradation products persist in aquifer systems for years before abiotic or biotic conversion yields short-chain terminal products [2]. Regulatory frameworks that assess precursors and products independently fail to account for this temporal cascade, resulting in monitoring data that misidentifies the true contaminant mass balance. Moreover, the six- and seven-carbon boundaries, derived from steady-state toxicological assessments, prove poorly suited to dynamic systems where precursor loading continuously replenishes short-chain PFAS concentrations.\n\nThe bioaccumulation-centered classification framework neglects groundwater-specific exposure dynamics that diverge markedly from surface water and dietary pathways. In addition, aquifer contamination scenarios emphasize direct ingestion and inhalation of volatilized compounds during showering—routes where short-chain PFAS bioaccumulation irrelevance provides no risk reduction. Regulatory thresholds calibrated to dietary bioaccumulation factors misalign with the actual exposure profiles determining human health outcomes in groundwater-dependent populations. This pathway-specific mismatch represents a fundamental structural limitation of classification systems developed primarily for ecological risk assessment.\n\nEmerging perfluoroalkyl ether carboxylic acids and sulfonic acids—replacement compounds developed under regulatory pressure—exemplify these classification inadequacies. Additionally, these substances often straddle carbon-count boundaries or incorporate novel structural motifs that challenge existing categorization schemes. In contrast, their bioaccumulation profiles remain incompletely characterized, yet their production volumes and environmental releases continue expanding. Regulatory frameworks thus face persistent classification lag relative to industrial chemical innovation, with aquifer systems serving as unintended receptors for substances whose transport behavior and health significance remain scientifically unresolved.",
outputs\campaigns\CAMP_20260310T073230_fb78bf\WEB_20260310T073230_5da0fc.json:2877:      "content": "The vadose zone functions as the critical interface determining PFAS fate prior to groundwater contamination, with chain-length-dependent partitioning governing whether compounds remain sequestered in soil or migrate downward. Moreover, this decoupling between total soil burden and leachable fraction creates fundamental uncertainty in risk assessment methodologies that rely on bulk soil measurements. Moreover, while interfacial sequestration at air-water boundaries temporarily retards vertical migration of long-chain compounds, this same mechanism creates a reservoir subject to mobilization upon water table fluctuations or infiltration events —a dynamic discussed in relation to secondary source formation in Sediment Contamination Hotspots: Formation and Secondary Release Dynamics. However, short-chain compounds, lacking comparable interfacial affinity, maintain higher effective mobility through the vadose profile despite lower individual sorption affinities per unit surface area. Sorption-desorption kinetics display pronounced hysteresis, with desorption rates systematically slower than sorption rates for both chain-length classes. Significantly, the magnitude of this kinetic asymmetry increases with perfluorocarbon chain length, reflecting stronger van der Waals interactions between fluorinated tails and soil surfaces —consistent with the chain-length-dependent stability patterns examined in Chemical Persistence and Environmental Stability. Long-chain PFAS demonstrate greater resistance to leaching under steady-state flow conditions but may exhibit delayed pulse releases when hydrological perturbations disrupt equilibrium conditions. Furthermore, this \"resistance to leaching\" refers specifically to retarded vertical transport rather than immobilization; long-chain compounds remain susceptible to episodic mobilization as addressed in discussions of persistent secondary sources. Short-chain compounds achieve substantial porewater concentrations due to higher initial aqueous solubility and more rapid desorption kinetics—properties examined in Surface Activity and Solubility Controls on Foam Formation and Stability—enabling faster breakthrough to underlying aquifers. The relationship between soil organic carbon content and PFAS retention follows modified partitioning models accounting for the amphiphilic nature of these contaminants. Moreover, unlike nonpolar organic compounds where sorption correlates linearly with organic carbon fraction, PFAS partitioning incorporates electrostatic interactions with mineral surfaces and specific binding to charged soil constituents. Long-chain perfluoroalkyl acids particularly exhibit this dual-mode sorption behavior, partitioning into organic carbon domains while simultaneously coordinating with metal oxide surfaces through their charged headgroups. In addition, short-chain analogs participate less effectively in hydrophobic partitioning, rendering their transport more sensitive to pH-dependent surface charge variations and ionic strength effects. Vadose zone processes preceding aquifer contamination include multiple retention mechanisms operating at distinct temporal scales. Immediate sorption to mineral surfaces occurs within hours, followed by slower diffusion into soil organic matter and intraparticle micropores over days to weeks. Consequently, long-chain PFAS penetrate these immobile domains more extensively due to favorable free energy of transfer, effectively removing them from mobile porewater fractions on timescales relevant to episodic infiltration events. Short-chain compounds remain predominantly in rapidly exchanging pools, maintaining higher effective mobility despite lower affinity per sorption site. Nevertheless, sites with historical long-chain PFAS releases may exhibit delayed groundwater impacts relative to short-chain contamination, not due to absence of source term but due to vadose zone retention capacity. Short-chain replacement compounds introduced after regulatory phase-outs demonstrate more rapid breakthrough to groundwater, potentially underestimating transport velocities calculated from conservative tracer behavior. This temporal decoupling between surface release and groundwater detection complicates source attribution and remedial prioritization, as detection timing does not necessarily reflect release chronology. In addition, treatment implications extend directly from these mechanistic understandings. Ex-situ soil washing and in-situ flushing technologies must account for chain-length-dependent desorption kinetics; long-chain PFAS require more aggressive chemical enhancement or extended residence times to achieve equivalent removal efficiency. Furthermore, the mobile porewater fraction—directly available for groundwater migration—contains disproportionate short-chain enrichment relative to total soil composition, suggesting that monitoring focused on leachable rather than total concentrations would better predict aquifer contamination risk. Remediation strategies targeting the vadose zone must incorporate chain-length-specific extraction protocols rather than assuming uniform contaminant behavior across PFAS classes. The capillary fringe region warrants particular attention as the transition zone where vadose zone processes intersect saturated groundwater systems. Moreover, long-chain PFAS accumulating at this interface through interfacial trapping mechanisms create concentrated transition zones that may serve as persistent source terms during water table rise events. Short-chain compounds, transit this boundary more readily due to limited retention capacity in the capillary fringe, establishing direct hydraulic connectivity between surface contamination and aquifer receptors with minimal attenuation. Additionally, soil moisture content emerges as a critical variable modulating effective partitioning coefficients across the unsaturated profile. At intermediate saturations, the competing effects of reduced aqueous phase volume and enhanced air-water interfacial area generate non-monotonic relationships between moisture state and PFAS mobility. Long-chain compounds exhibit maximum retention under partially saturated conditions where interfacial area is maximized, whereas complete saturation or near-dry conditions diminish this retention mechanism. Moreover, short-chain PFAS display comparatively muted moisture dependence, reflecting their dominant partitioning into the aqueous phase regardless of saturation state. The role of soil structure and aggregation in regulating pore-scale transport pathways remains underappreciated in conventional partitioning models. Furthermore, intra-aggregate porosity creates diffusion-limited domains where long-chain PFAS may become sequestered within soil structural units, physically isolating contaminants from advective flow paths. Short-chain compounds, with higher effective diffusion coefficients, more readily equilibrate between inter-aggregate and intra-aggregate porosity, maintaining concentration continuity across pore domains. This structural sequestration contributes to the apparent kinetic hysteresis observed in batch desorption experiments, conflating true chemical desorption resistance with physical transport limitations. Additionally, co-solute effects and competitive displacement phenomena introduce additional complexity in multi-component PFAS mixtures characteristic of AFFF formulations. The presence of short-chain compounds may indirectly facilitate long-chain mobilization through competitive displacement from high-affinity sorption sites, particularly under dynamic flow conditions where local equilibrium assumptions fail. Furthermore, long-chain PFAS at elevated concentrations may modify air-water interfacial properties, potentially altering the effective retention capacity for co-occurring short-chain analogs. These mixture-specific behaviors challenge the predictive capacity of single-component partitioning parameters applied to complex environmental systems. Temperature fluctuations through seasonal cycles or climate variability introduce thermodynamic perturbations to equilibrium partitioning relationships. Nevertheless, the exothermic nature of hydrophobic partitioning interactions implies enhanced retention at lower temperatures, with long-chain PFAS exhibiting greater temperature sensitivity due to larger enthalpic contributions from perfluorocarbon tail interactions. Short-chain compounds, with weaker overall sorption enthalpies, demonstrate more stable partitioning behavior across temperature ranges relevant to vadose zone environments. Additionally, seasonal groundwater recharge events coinciding with temperature minima may therefore mobilize differentially enriched PFAS signatures compared to warm-season infiltration. Conventional leaching tests performed under standard temperature and moisture conditions may systematically misrepresent field-scale mobilization potential, particularly for long-chain compounds where retention capacity varies nonlinearly with environmental state variables. Monitoring strategies must incorporate temporal dynamics of infiltration events rather than relying on steady-state assumptions, as the episodic nature of vadose zone transport governs actual contaminant delivery to underlying aquifers. Additionally, the air-water interfacial partitioning behavior of PFAS compounds represents a critical mechanism distinguishing long-chain from short-chain transport dynamics in unsaturated soils. This differential interfacial affinity fundamentally governs how these compound classes distribute within soil pore networks and determines their relative mobility toward underlying aquifers. However, the pronounced accumulation of longer-chain compounds at air-water boundaries effectively sequesters these contaminants within the vadose zone, creating a temporary—but potentially reversible—retardation mechanism that does not operate equivalently for short-chain replacements. The relationship between soil-bound PFAS and mobile porewater concentrations remains inadequately characterized for predictive modeling purposes. This measurement gap introduces substantial uncertainty when extrapolating from soil contamination data to groundwater risk assessments, particularly given the chain-length-dependent decoupling between total soil burden and leachable fraction. Specifically, this finding carries significant implications for remediation technology selection: treatments exploiting interfacial phenomena, such as surfactant-enhanced foam fractionation, achieve rapid removal of both compound classes from groundwater—reducing total PFAS concentrations from approximately 15,000 ng L⁻¹ to below 8 ng L⁻¹ detection limits within 15 minutes. The addition of cationic surfactants like CTAB, which exhibit strong air-water interface affinity, enables this rapid extraction from both synthetic and natural groundwater matrices. Furthermore, this disconnect between environmental fate drivers and regulatory classification schemes complicates risk-based prioritization of contaminated sites. The persistence of PFAS compounds—their characterization as \"forever chemicals\" reflecting resistance to natural degradation processes —ensures that vadose zone reservoirs established through historical releases remain active source terms indefinitely, with chain-length-dependent release kinetics governing the temporal pattern of groundwater contamination. The fundamental distinction between chain-length classes in air-water interfacial partitioning has direct implications for how regulatory frameworks classify and manage these contaminants. In addition, current classification schemes for short- and long-chain PFAS derive primarily from bioaccumulation potential rather than subsurface transport behavior, creating a disconnect between environmental fate drivers and risk-based prioritization criteria. This misalignment proves particularly problematic when historical contamination scenarios involve complex AFFF mixtures where transport predictions based solely on bioaccumulation metrics fail to capture vadose zone mobility patterns. Additionally, the environmental ubiquity of PFAS compounds—detected across water, air, soil, fish, wildlife, and human populations nationwide —underscores the necessity of understanding chain-length-specific partitioning for predictive exposure assessment. The characterization of these substances as \"forever chemicals\" reflects their stability and persistence in environmental systems and biological tissues, indicating they do not readily degrade and accumulate over time. This intrinsic persistence ensures that vadose zone reservoirs function as indefinite source terms, with the temporal pattern of groundwater contamination governed by chain-length-dependent release kinetics rather than attenuation through natural degradation processes. Furthermore, the practical implications of these mechanistic understandings extend to treatment technology development. The efficiency of such ex-situ treatments contrasts with the challenge of in-situ vadose zone remediation, where the same interfacial phenomena that enable foam fractionation simultaneously retard natural flushing of long-chain compounds from soil matrices.",

Nevertheless, this spread reflects heterogeneous source distributions, variable transport distances, and differential attenuation processes. Long-chain PFAS exhibit stronger hydrophobic interactions and sorptive affinity to solid phases, theoretically producing attenuated transport distances and elevated concentrations near source zones. Establishing true background contamination levels presents methodological challenges. The ubiquity of PFAS in contemporary environmental systems, including atmospheric deposition and agricultural amendments, complicates discrimination between natural background and diffuse anthropogenic inputs. In addition, the lower concentration bound of 22 ng L⁻¹ likely represents a practical detection limit for composite PFAS analysis rather than a genuine geochemical baseline, indicating that even minimally impacted groundwater systems exhibit measurable PFAS burdens. Furthermore, point-source scenarios, typical of industrial facilities and firefighting training areas, generate steep concentration gradients where long-chain PFAS accumulate near source zones, while short-chain compounds penetrate deeper into aquifer systems. This differential penetration creates characteristic concentration profiles that invert with transport distance—long-chain species declining precipitously while short-chain concentrations persist, ultimately establishing mobile contamination fronts dominated by short-chain homologs. This regulatory mismatch between measured concentrations and controlled compounds complicates risk communication and remediation prioritization based on baseline data alone. outputs\campaigns\CAMP_20260310T073230_fb78bf\WEB_20260310T073230_5da0fc.json:2908:      "content": "The groundwater-surface water interface constitutes the critical boundary where subsurface PFAS plumes encounter hydrologically connected receptors, yet quantitative characterization of mass transfer across this transition remains substantially incomplete. Additionally, this methodological limitation constrains predictive capabilities for contaminant fate modeling, as field-validated mass transfer coefficients for PFAS compounds remain scarce. In addition, the hyporheic zone serves as the primary locus for bidirectional PFAS flux between groundwater and surface water systems. Hydrological gradients drive advective transport across this interface, while concentration gradients established through differential partitioning mechanisms govern diffusive components. The relative dominance of advection versus diffusion depends on aquifer hydraulic conductivity, stream stage variability, and head differentials between surface and subsurface systems. Moreover, long-chain compounds exhibit pronounced affinity for sedimentary organic carbon, creating concentration discontinuities at the groundwater-surface water transition. Sorptive retention dampens instantaneous flux into surface waters but establishes sustained release conditions where contaminated groundwater discharge occurs over extended timescales, a dynamic elaborated in Section [X.X] regarding soil-porewater partitioning mechanisms in unsaturated zones. Moreover, short-chain PFAS demonstrate weaker sediment binding, facilitating more rapid equilibration across the interface. This reduced retardation enables higher instantaneous mass flux rates per unit concentration gradient, fundamentally altering the temporal dynamics of contaminant delivery to surface receptors. For nationwide distribution patterns of short-chain versus long-chain compound mobility, see Section [X.X]. In addition, temperature and pH fluctuations at the interface modulate sorption equilibrium and transient mass transfer rates, as detailed in Section [X.X] regarding soil-porewater partitioning mechanisms. Seasonal groundwater temperature variations alter PFAS aqueous solubility and sorption partition coefficients, introducing temporal dynamics that complicate steady-state mass balance calculations. Moreover, episodic precipitation events generate hydraulic transients that temporarily reverse flow directions across the hyporheic zone, inducing bidirectional contaminant migration that challenges unidirectional transport assumptions in standard exposure models. Surface water discharge fundamentally transforms contaminant fate pathways. Mass entering surface waters becomes subject to more rapid advective distribution across receptor exposure zones compared to groundwater-contained mass. Furthermore, photolytic and biotic transformation processes absent in groundwater may alter PFAS profiles, with the relative persistence of long-chain versus short-chain compounds determining whether surface water discharge represents a terminal sink or temporary transfer mechanism. Treatment system design requires explicit consideration of chain-length-dependent mass transfer kinetics. Additionally, extraction well networks positioned to capture groundwater before surface water discharge must account for the enhanced mobility of short-chain PFAS, which may bypass capture zones designed for strongly retarded long-chain compounds. In-situ treatment barriers face kinetic limitations when short-chain PFAS transit times through reactive media prove insufficient for complete degradation or sorption. The bidirectional nature of hyporheic exchange carries additional complexity for remedial decision-making. In addition, surface water infiltration during high-flow conditions transports PFAS into shallow aquifer zones, effectively expanding the subsurface contamination footprint beyond original source delineations. Coupled with sediment re-suspension dynamics, this creates feedback loops where surface water becomes a secondary source for groundwater contamination rather than solely a receptor. Additionally, quantifying these exchange dynamics through field instrumentation remains essential for parameterizing predictive models. Nested piezometer arrays, seepage meters, and differential flow gauging constitute critical measurement approaches. Current reliance on laboratory-derived partition coefficients and numerical approximations for dispersion characteristics introduces uncertainty that propagates through risk assessment calculations. Additionally, enhanced field characterization targeting chain-length-specific mass transfer rates would substantially improve confidence in exposure forecasts and remedial performance predictions for groundwater-surface water systems impacted by PFAS contamination. Field-based quantification of PFAS mass transfer coefficients remains notably scarce, representing a persistent methodological gap that constrains model validation efforts. In addition, laboratory-derived sorption parameters frequently fail to capture the heterogeneity of natural hyporheic sediments, where organic carbon content varies spatially over orders of magnitude and mineral surface chemistry differs substantially from simplified experimental matrices. Mass transfer rates estimated from batch experiments may deviate from field-scale behavior by factors that remain uncharacterized for most PFAS compounds. The manufacturing facility context examined in recent field investigations demonstrates how source-area hydrogeology governs plume discharge characteristics to receiving waters. Furthermore, high-resolution temporal monitoring reveals that PFAS concentration profiles in discharging groundwater do not mirror static source compositions, indicating that subsurface transformation and differential transport fractionation occur prior to surface water entry. This finding carries significant implications for receptor exposure assessment, as surface water concentrations may poorly reflect original source signatures. Moreover, chain-length-dependent discharge patterns emerge distinctly under field conditions. Long-chain PFAS exhibit pronounced concentration attenuation across the hyporheic zone due to combined sorptive and diffusive limitations, whereas short-chain homologs demonstrate more conservative transport with mass flux rates correlating strongly with groundwater discharge velocity. The resulting compositional shift between groundwater and surface water end-members necessitates separate analytical frameworks for exposure modeling rather than simple dilution calculations. Moreover, seasonal hydrological variability introduces additional complexity for mass transfer quantification. Baseflow conditions favor sustained, low-concentration discharge of long-chain PFAS from sediment storage reservoirs, while stormflow events mobilize short-chain compounds through enhanced groundwater-surface water connectivity and reduced hydraulic residence times. Moreover, this temporal partitioning means that annual mass loading estimates based on single-season sampling campaigns risk substantial bias depending on which hydrological regime dominates the monitoring period. The bidirectional flux characterization presents particular challenges for remedial endpoint establishment. Surface water quality criteria typically assume continuous discharge scenarios, yet episodic infiltration during flood events can reverse contaminant migration directions and expand the effective source zone. Furthermore, remedial systems designed solely for groundwater capture may prove inadequate when surface water constitutes a secondary contamination pathway, requiring integrated monitoring approaches that track mass movement across both domains simultaneously. Analytical detection capabilities further constrain field-based mass transfer studies. In addition, the ultra-trace concentrations characteristic of PFAS in environmental matrices demand specialized extraction and quantification methods that introduce systematic biases between groundwater and surface water sample types. Method reporting limits that differ between matrices can create apparent concentration discontinuities that reflect analytical artifacts rather than true mass transfer behavior, complicating coefficient derivation from paired measurements. Future field investigation priorities should emphasize direct measurement of interfacial fluxes through nested instrumentation capable of resolving concentration gradients at centimeter scales within the hyporheic zone. Additionally, such approaches would enable validation of numerical model formulations currently reliant on assumed equilibrium conditions that may not prevail under dynamic field conditions. Air-water interfacial phenomena introduce additional complexity to PFAS mass transfer dynamics at the groundwater-surface water interface. In addition, this differential interfacial adsorption capacity fundamentally alters transport behavior in systems where entrapped air or fluctuating water tables create extensive air-water boundaries. Long-chain compounds may experience enhanced retardation in unsaturated zone transitions preceding surface water discharge, effectively decoupling their arrival times from groundwater flow velocities. The significance of air-water interfacial adsorption extends beyond unsaturated zone processes. In contrast, recent investigations demonstrated that this mechanism governs the distribution of longer-chain PFAS within soil samples, creating concentration profiles that deviate substantially from predictions based solely on solid-phase sorption. For mass transfer modeling across the groundwater-surface water interface, this implies that long-chain PFAS may exhibit dual-retardation behavior—sorptive partitioning to organic carbon combined with interfacial accumulation—that short-chain compounds largely bypass. Moreover, bioaccumulation potential serves as the definitive criterion for classifying chain-length categories, establishing a functional rather than purely structural distinction between PFAS groups. This classification framework carries direct implications for interfacial mass transfer, as compounds with elevated bioaccumulation potential simultaneously demonstrate stronger interfacial activity and sediment affinity. The convergence of these properties means that long-chain PFAS encounter multiple sequential barriers to surface water discharge, whereas short-chain compounds face fewer attenuation mechanisms. Moreover, field-based quantification efforts remain essential for resolving these complex behaviors. Such empirical approaches are necessary because laboratory-derived parameters fail to capture the coupled physicochemical processes operating at environmental interfaces. Furthermore, the scarcity of validated field measurements for chain-length-specific mass transfer rates continues to limit predictive model confidence, particularly for scenarios involving dynamic hydrological conditions where equilibrium assumptions prove inadequate. This classification framework carries mechanistic significance for groundwater-surface water exchange, as compounds with elevated bioaccumulation potential simultaneously exhibit enhanced air-water interfacial activity and stronger sediment affinity. The resulting dual-retardation behavior—combining organic carbon partitioning with interfacial accumulation—generates pronounced arrival time decoupling from groundwater flow velocities for long-chain species. Additionally, this mechanism assumes particular importance in unsaturated zone transitions preceding surface water discharge, where entrapped air or fluctuating water tables create extensive interfacial boundaries. The convergence of bioaccumulation potential, interfacial activity, and sediment affinity creates sequential attenuation barriers that fundamentally differentiate chain-length-dependent mass transfer. Furthermore, long-chain PFAS encounter multiple retention mechanisms—organic carbon sorption, air-water interfacial adsorption, and diffusion limitations—whereas short-chain compounds experience reduced retardation and more conservative transport.
