outputs\campaigns\CAMP_20260310T073230_fb78bf\WEB_20260310T073230_5da0fc.json:2916:      "content": "Additionally, this differential surface activity originates from the pronounced hydrophobic character conferred by extended fluorinated chains, which drives preferential orientation and concentration at phase boundaries. The technological implementation of this principle has been systematically evaluated through examination of six distinct cationic surfactant formulations. For instance, these surfactants function by stabilizing foam structures while enhancing PFAS partitioning to the interface, with molecular design parameters—including hydrocarbon tail length, charge density, and counterion composition—modulating both foam stability and PFAS-enrichment capacity. Additionally, surfactant selection becomes particularly consequential when addressing mixed-chain-length contamination, as competitive interfacial partitioning may favor long-chain species and leave short-chain counterparts inadequately extracted in treated effluent —a limitation examined in detail in the Treatment Efficiency Disparities section regarding short-chain remediation challenges. Empirical validation demonstrates substantial removal capabilities under optimized conditions, though with important chain-length-dependent qualifications. However, this aggregate performance metric masks substantial disparities in individual species removal: while long-chain PFAS achieve near-complete extraction, short-chain analogs demonstrate markedly diminished removal efficiencies, reflecting the underlying surface-activity differential. The optimistic framing of total PFAS reduction must be tempered by recognition that short-chain species—increasingly prevalent in contemporary AFFF formulations and environmental degradation products—may persist at 50% or greater residual concentrations despite aggressive treatment parameters. Operational constraints fundamentally shape aquifer remediation applicability. In addition, the technology requires ex situ implementation, necessitating groundwater extraction and above-ground treatment before potential reinjection or discharge. This engineering configuration limits deployment for deep confined aquifers or regions where water table depression is environmentally or economically prohibitive, constraints that intersect with groundwater-surface water exchange dynamics discussed in the corresponding hydrological section. Moreover, for accessible unconfined aquifers, the method offers distinct waste management advantages over activated carbon or ion exchange: concentrated PFAS-laden foam fractions amenable to subsequent destruction technologies replace spent solid waste requiring landfill disposal —a characteristic relevant to sediment contamination hotspot management where concentrated waste streams require controlled destruction. Treatment system design must accommodate chain-length-specific performance characteristics. Long-chain PFAS removal achieves satisfactory efficiencies with standard cationic surfactant formulations and conventional bubble generation parameters. Indeed, short-chain PFAS remediation demands substantially modified approaches—increased surfactant concentrations, reduced bubble sizes to enhance interfacial area, or sequential treatment stages—that escalate operational costs and energy requirements. These economic and engineering trade-offs reflect a broader technological limitation: surface-activity-based separation methods face fundamental constraints with minimally hydrophobic short-chain perfluorinated compounds. Furthermore, the environmental significance of chain-length transformations—whether through natural precursor degradation or engineered treatment—warrants careful consideration. Conversion of more extractable long-chain species to recalcitrant short-chain forms may inadvertently degrade remediation effectiveness, as the latter demonstrate markedly diminished foam fractionation amenability. This risk reinforces the necessity for treatment trains that address both chain length categories rather than relying on single-technology solutions. Furthermore, analytical considerations extend to surfactant residual management. Cationic surfactants introduced to enhance PFAS removal may persist in treated water, potentially affecting downstream biological processes or requiring additional treatment stages. In addition, the environmental fate of these auxiliary chemicals in aquifer systems—should treated water be reinjected—must be evaluated against PFAS concentration reduction benefits. Source zone characteristics further modulate implementation: high-concentration zones may overwhelm surfactant binding capacity, necessitating pretreatment or staged application, while dilute plumes permit reduced surfactant loading with improved process economics. Current technological development reflects recognition that conventional PFAS remediation approaches exhibit chain-length-dependent performance limitations. In addition, the six-surfactant evaluation establishes foundational understanding toward expanding effective treatment range, though substantial performance gaps remain for short-chain species. Future advancement toward multifunctional formulations capable of engaging short-chain PFAS through alternative binding mechanisms beyond hydrophobic interaction alone would address this fundamental constraint and support more comprehensive aquifer restoration objectives. Foam fractionation operational parameters exhibit pronounced sensitivity to groundwater geochemistry, with ionic strength and dissolved organic matter content modulating surfactant-PFAS complex stability and interfacial partitioning dynamics. Elevated concentrations of competing anions, particularly sulfate and bicarbonate, can destabilize cationic surfactant foams through charge screening effects, necessitating formulation adjustments that increase surfactant dosage requirements and associated treatment costs. In addition, the six-surfactant evaluation systematically examined these matrix effects across representative groundwater compositions, establishing operational envelopes within which chain-length-dependent removal efficiencies remain achievable. Temperature fluctuations present additional engineering considerations. Reduced groundwater temperatures, characteristic of deeper aquifer systems, diminish surfactant solubility and increase solution viscosity, impairing bubble rise velocities and foam drainage rates. Nevertheless, elevated temperatures enhance surfactant mobility but accelerate foam collapse, reducing PFAS enrichment factors. The optimal temperature window for cationic surfactant foam fractionation spans approximately, conditions most readily achievable in temperate unconfined aquifer applications where groundwater extraction temperatures approximate ambient surface conditions. In addition, the six distinct surfactant formulations evaluated encompassed structural variations designed to probe structure-performance relationships: alkyltrimethylammonium species with C12–C18 hydrocarbon tails, dialkyldimethylammonium compounds, and benzalkonium-derived structures incorporating aromatic moieties. These molecular architectures generate divergent interfacial film properties—elasticity, surface viscosity, and electrostatic characteristics—that differentially accommodate PFAS partitioning. Notably, surfactants with intermediate tail lengths (C14–C16) demonstrated superior performance across the chain-length spectrum, achieving the most favorable compromise between foam stability and short-chain PFAS accessibility to the air-water interface. Comparative assessment against alternative surfactant classes—anionic, nonionic, and zwitterionic—establishes cationic formulations as uniquely advantageous for PFAS foam fractionation. Anionic surfactants generate electrostatic repulsion with anionic PFAS species, severely limiting interfacial co-adsorption. Furthermore, nonionic surfactants lack the electrostatic attraction mechanism that enhances PFAS partitioning in cationic systems. Zwitterionic compounds exhibit pH-dependent charge characteristics that introduce operational complexity inconsistent with robust field deployment. Process intensification strategies examined in conjunction with the six-surfactant evaluation include foam reflux operation, wherein collapsed foam liquid is recycled to the feed stream to enhance PFAS concentration factors. Nevertheless, this modification improves overall system efficiency but introduces surfactant accumulation risks requiring careful mass balance management. The concentrated foamate produced—containing PFAS enriched by factors of 10²–10³ relative to influent concentrations—demands subsequent treatment, with thermal destruction or supercritical water oxidation representing viable destruction pathways that complete the remediation chain. In addition, scale-up considerations from laboratory evaluation to field implementation necessitate recognition of hydrodynamic non-idealities. Column diameter effects on bubble size distribution, gas distributor fouling under prolonged operation, and surfactant degradation through microbial or oxidative pathways in recycled systems all introduce performance uncertainties not fully captured in controlled experimental conditions. Nevertheless, the systematic six-surfactant evaluation provides essential foundational data for predictive model development and process optimization, establishing quantitative relationships between surfactant molecular parameters and PFAS removal outcomes that inform technology selection for site-specific aquifer remediation challenges. The interfacial partitioning behavior of PFAS species exhibits direct correspondence with their environmental mobility profiles in aquifer systems. Long-chain PFAS such as PFOA and PFOS demonstrate substantially greater accumulation at the air-water interface compared to short-chain analogs including PFBS and PFBA, a differential that simultaneously enables efficient foam fractionation extraction while indicating their relative retention in vadose zone environments through capillary-facilitated interfacial adsorption. Indeed, this surface-activity gradient carries significant implications for leaching potential assessment: compounds most amenable to surfactant-enhanced removal are conversely those most prone to attenuation in unsaturated soil profiles through air-water interfacial trapping mechanisms. The measured and simulated data sets demonstrate that longer-chain PFAS distribution within soil samples depends critically on air-water interfacial adsorption processes, suggesting that high foam fractionation efficiency for these species correlates with reduced vertical migration risk in typical aquifer overburden materials. Short-chain PFAS present inverse characteristics—diminished foam fractionation amenability yet enhanced subsurface mobility due to weaker interfacial partitioning and greater aqueous solubility. In addition, this creates a fundamental remediation paradox: the species most likely to reach groundwater through rapid leaching are precisely those most resistant to surfactant-based extraction technologies. The classification of short- and long-chain PFAS based on bioaccumulation potential further complicates risk prioritization, as regulatory frameworks emphasizing bioaccumulation may underweight the hydrological hazards posed by mobile short-chain compounds that bypass natural attenuation mechanisms. In addition, empirical validation of cationic surfactant foam fractionation under realistic groundwater conditions demonstrates substantial treatment capabilities that must be interpreted through chain-length-specific lenses. However, this aggregate performance obscures species-dependent extraction efficiencies: the near-complete elimination of total PFAS concentrations masks persistent short-chain residuals that may constitute environmentally significant fractions when initial plume compositions reflect contemporary AFFF formulations or precursor degradation products. The six-surfactant evaluation framework provides systematic comparison of molecular design parameters, yet acknowledges that short-chain PFAS removal requires substantially modified operational approaches that escalate energy and chemical inputs. Groundwater-surface water exchange dynamics introduce additional complexity for aquifer remediation design. Field-based quantification of PFAS mass transfer between groundwater and surface water reveals transport pathways that bypass conventional extraction well capture zones, suggesting that foam fractionation implementation must account for hydrological connectivity that may deliver short-chain PFAS to receiving waters before treatment system intervention. Additionally, the persistence characteristics of PFAS as \"forever chemicals\" that resist environmental degradation amplify the significance of chain-length-dependent removal efficiency gaps. Unlike biodegradable contaminants where partial extraction may suffice for natural attenuation completion, PFAS species escaping foam fractionation retain indefinite aquifer residence potential. This necessitates treatment train approaches that combine surfactant-enhanced extraction with destructive technologies capable of addressing concentrated short-chain residuals in foamate streams, rather than reliance on single-technology solutions with inherent chain-length selectivity limitations. Specifically, the six-surfactant evaluation framework specifically targeted cationic formulations including CTAB (cetyltrimethylammonium bromide) to probe structure-performance relationships across chain-length variants. CTAB-enhanced systems demonstrated exceptional aggregate performance, reducing total PFAS concentrations from approximately 15,000 ng L⁻¹ to below 8 ng L⁻¹ detection limits within 15 minutes under optimized conditions. However, the substantial aggregate removal efficiency must be interpreted cautiously: the detection limit threshold masks differential species persistence, with short-chain residuals potentially representing environmentally significant fractions when contemporary AFFF formulations dominate source composition. The air-water interfacial accumulation differential between chain-length categories carries direct implications for vadose zone attenuation mechanisms. Long-chain PFAS including PFOA and PFOS demonstrate markedly greater interfacial accumulation than short-chain analogs PFBS and PFBA, a characteristic that simultaneously enables efficient foam fractionation while indicating enhanced retention in unsaturated soil profiles through capillary-facilitated interfacial trapping. However, research examining PFAS distribution in unsaturated soils confirms that air-water interfacial adsorption governs longer-chain PFAS retention within soil samples, establishing an inverse relationship between foam fractionation amenability and leaching potential. This correlation between surface activity and subsurface mobility generates divergent risk profiles: compounds most resistant to groundwater penetration are paradoxically those most amenable to surfactant-based extraction. Furthermore, research exploring soil-porewater concentration relationships demonstrates that short-chain species maintain elevated mobility in porewater fractions capable of migrating to underlying aquifers. This creates a fundamental treatment paradox where the PFAS most likely to reach groundwater through rapid leaching are precisely those most resistant to cationic surfactant extraction technologies. The environmental persistence of PFAS as \"forever chemicals\" that resist natural degradation amplifies the significance of chain-length-dependent removal gaps. Moreover, unlike biodegradable contaminants where partial extraction suffice for attenuation completion, PFAS species escaping foam fractionation retain indefinite aquifer residence potential. This necessitates treatment train approaches combining surfactant-enhanced extraction with destructive technologies rather than reliance on single-technology solutions with inherent chain-length selectivity limitations.",

Furthermore, this differential treatment response amplifies the environmental mobility challenges identified throughout this analysis: compounds exhibiting greater subsurface mobility simultaneously resist capture by conventional and advanced treatment technologies, creating a compounding effect with the transport dynamics discussed in Section 'Sediment Contamination Hotspots: Formation and Secondary Release' [11]. The technological gap means that short-chain PFAS, already predisposed to extensive aquifer transport due to weaker soil sorption characteristics as detailed in that same section, encounter fewer effective barriers to long-range migration. The mechanistic basis for this disparity in unenhanced systems resides in surface-active properties governing foam fractionation efficacy. Additionally, as established in Section 'Air-Water Interfacial Dynamics: Different', long-chain PFAS exhibit substantially greater accumulation at air-water interfaces than short-chain analogs, a differential that directly translates to separation performance. Short-chain compounds exhibit markedly diminished surface activity, limiting their transfer to foam fractions and necessitating substantially longer residence times or modified operational parameters to achieve comparable removal [11]. Furthermore, this physicochemical constraint represents a fundamental technological boundary for conventional foam fractionation rather than an engineering optimization challenge. Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) enhancement demonstrates potential pathways for addressing this disparity, as examined in Section 'Remediation Methodologies: Cationic Surfactants and PFAS'. The cationic surfactant operates through dual mechanisms: direct competition for air-water interface occupancy that displaces PFAS molecules, and complexation with anionic PFAS species that modifies their partitioning behavior. Furthermore, this approach acknowledges that short-chain removal requires fundamental modification of separation chemistry rather than incremental parameter adjustment. Field validation in AFFF-impacted natural groundwaters illustrates the performance characteristics of enhanced treatment. In addition, this exceptional bulk reduction performance demonstrates the efficacy of surfactant enhancement for achieving low total concentrations, though compound-specific removal kinetics may vary. The 15-minute timeframe represents optimal laboratory conditions; practical full-scale applications face constraints from groundwater geochemistry variability, competing organic matter, and surfactant dosage optimization that may differentially affect short-chain removal sustainability. Treatment efficiency disparities carry profound implications for aquifer management strategies. Moreover, remediation systems designed around long-chain PFAS behavior will systematically underperform for short-chain contamination, potentially creating false confidence in protective efficacy while mobile short-chain plumes continue expanding. This technological blind spot necessitates compound-specific performance validation rather than reliance on bulk PFAS metrics that obscure differential removal. Additionally, monitoring frameworks must explicitly quantify short-chain species to verify treatment effectiveness, as total PFAS reduction may primarily reflect long-chain capture while short-chain concentrations persist above regulatory thresholds. The convergence of transport and treatment disadvantages for short-chain PFAS demands integrated assessment of leaching potential. Compounds exhibiting 50% or lower removal efficiency enter aquifer systems with reduced attenuation capacity [11], while their intrinsic mobility characteristics enable faster and more extensive plume migration. In addition, this dual disadvantage suggests that short-chain PFAS contamination may represent the dominant long-term groundwater quality challenge even where total contamination burdens are lower than historical long-chain releases. Notably, primary treatment targeting long-chain PFAS removal should be coupled with secondary processes specifically engineered for short-chain capture, potentially including advanced oxidation, ion exchange resins with tailored selectivity, or membrane separations with appropriate molecular weight cutoffs. The economic and operational complexity of such multi-barrier systems underscores the prevention imperative: controlling short-chain PFAS source terms offers greater protective efficacy than reliance on attenuation technologies with inherent performance limitations for mobile compounds. The differential treatment response ultimately reinforces the central thesis regarding differential leaching potential. Moreover, short-chain PFAS combine environmental persistence, subsurface mobility, and technological resistance in a manner that challenges conventional groundwater protection paradigms developed around less mobile contaminants. Effective aquifer management requires explicit recognition that the compounds most difficult to remove from water are simultaneously those most likely to migrate extensively within aquifer systems, necessitating protective strategies that prioritize source control and early interception over reliance on in situ or ex situ treatment efficacy. Moreover, the CTAB enhancement mechanism operates through rapid kinetic partitioning that overcomes the surface activity limitations constraining unenhanced systems. This performance validates that surfactant enhancement fundamentally alters separation chemistry rather than merely optimizing existing parameters. Moreover, the dual mechanism—interface competition and anionic complexation—enables simultaneous capture of both long- and short-chain species that unenhanced systems cannot achieve. However, the 15-minute treatment timeframe reflects controlled laboratory conditions with optimized CTAB dosages and minimal competing matrix constituents. Practical deployment faces substantial constraints from variable groundwater geochemistry, including dissolved organic carbon interference, ionic strength fluctuations, and pH-dependent surfactant speciation that may differentially compromise short-chain removal sustainability. The concentration reduction from 15,000 ng L⁻¹ to <8 ng L⁻¹, while demonstrating exceptional bulk performance, does not guarantee equivalent removal efficiency for individual short-chain compounds whose partitioning behavior varies with perfluorinated chain length and headgroup chemistry. The technological implications extend beyond single-process optimization to systemic treatment architecture. Significantly, the 50% or lower unenhanced removal efficiency for short-chain PFAS establishes a performance floor that conventional engineering cannot breach without chemical enhancement. This constraint positions CTAB-enhanced foam fractionation as a potential primary treatment component, though its effectiveness as a standalone solution remains contingent on sustained surfactant dosing and matrix-specific optimization. In addition, the rapid kinetic achievement of bulk reduction suggests that enhanced foam fractionation may serve effectively as a pretreatment stage concentrating PFAS for subsequent destruction technologies, thereby addressing the volume reduction challenge that limits advanced oxidation process economic viability. Compound-specific removal validation becomes critical given that bulk metrics may obscure persistent short-chain contamination. However, the reduction to <8 ng L⁻¹ total PFAS does not preclude residual short-chain concentrations above regulatory thresholds, as these compounds contribute disproportionately less to total mass while presenting disproportionately greater mobility risk. Additionally, monitoring frameworks must therefore implement speciated analysis rather than total PFAS quantification to verify treatment effectiveness against compound-specific standards. The convergence of rapid enhanced treatment kinetics with inherent unenhanced system limitations underscores the compound-specific nature of technological solutions. Furthermore, the transition from long-chain to short-chain PFAS in industrial formulations, driven by bioaccumulation-based regulatory pressure, has transferred environmental risk to compounds that combine maximum subsurface mobility with minimum technological capturability—characteristics that challenge legacy groundwater protection frameworks developed around less mobile, more treatable contaminants. The evaluation of six cationic surfactants for PFAS removal from groundwater demonstrates that CTAB represents one of multiple chemical enhancement pathways, though its specific performance characteristics relative to alternative surfactants remain subject to comparative validation. This broader surfactant screening suggests that the enhancement approach itself—rather than any single compound—constitutes the critical innovation for addressing short-chain removal limitations. The mechanistic versatility of cationic surfactants, operating through both interface competition and electrostatic complexation, provides a template for future optimization that may yield superior cost-effectiveness or reduced environmental persistence compared to CTAB specifically. The relationship between soil-phase PFAS and mobile porewater concentrations directly informs treatment efficiency disparities at the source-pathway interface. Additionally, research quantifying PFAS mass transfer between soil and porewater reveals that unsaturated zone processes govern the contaminant loading delivered to groundwater systems. Treatment technologies encounter not merely dissolved-phase contamination but dynamically equilibrating systems where soil reservoirs continuously replenish groundwater concentrations. Additionally, this has particular significance for short-chain PFAS: their weaker soil sorption drives higher porewater fractions, increasing the mass flux requiring treatment while simultaneously reducing the efficacy of technologies dependent on interfacial phenomena. The compounding effect intensifies the challenge—greater mobile mass combined with inferior removal efficiency creates a treatment demand that unenhanced systems cannot satisfy. Field-scale groundwater-to-surface water transport studies provide essential context for interpreting treatment limitations within broader hydrological systems. Moreover, quantification of PFAS mass transfer between groundwater and surface water using field data demonstrates that aquifer contamination represents a dynamic source term with continuous export potential. Treatment systems therefore operate against a background of ongoing source contribution rather than static plume conditions. Indeed, this reality underscores the inadequacy of partial removal: 50% short-chain efficiency leaves substantial mobile contamination capable of sustained propagation through aquifer systems and subsequent discharge to surface receptors. The technological gap thus translates directly to persistent environmental exposure risk that end-of-pipe treatment cannot fully mitigate. The regulatory classification basis for chain-length differentiation—bioaccumulation potential rather than groundwater mobility—reveals a fundamental mismatch between environmental protection frameworks and emerging contamination profiles. Moreover, regulatory pressure has driven industrial substitution toward short-chain formulations that evade bioaccumulation-based restrictions while exhibiting maximal subsurface transport potential. This substitution effect positions short-chain PFAS as the dominant groundwater quality concern of coming decades: compounds selected specifically for their environmental mobility encounter treatment technologies structurally incapable of efficient capture. Nevertheless, the temporal dimension proves critical—long-chain PFAS may dominate current contamination burdens, but short-chain compounds will persist longer in aquifer systems due to treatment bypass and sustained source release, ultimately representing the greater long-term management challenge. The environmental persistence of PFAS compounds fundamentally constrains remediation timelines and treatment system design. This intrinsic stability ensures that short-chain PFAS entering aquifer systems through treatment bypass or direct release will persist indefinitely, accumulating management liability across generational timescales. Consequently, the 50% or lower removal efficiency for unenhanced short-chain treatment translates not merely to partial capture but to permanent environmental loading when residual concentrations exceed regulatory thresholds. The national distribution of PFAS contamination underscores the systemic scale of this challenge. This pervasive presence indicates that aquifer systems nationwide function as receiving waters for continuous PFAS inputs, with short-chain compounds particularly favored for long-range transport due to their combination of persistence and mobility. Moreover, air-water interfacial dynamics in unsaturated zones further illuminate the source-pathway mechanisms governing treatment system loading. This differential interfacial partitioning means that long-chain PFAS retain greater soil-phase residence, reducing the mass flux delivered to groundwater and treatment systems. In contrast, short-chain PFAS exhibit diminished interfacial accumulation, accelerating their transfer to mobile porewater and increasing treatment demand precisely where technological efficacy is lowest. Standard leaching methods in EPA SW-846, known as the Leaching Environmental Assessment Framework (LEAF; EPA Methods 1313–1316), were originally developed for inorganic constituents and need to be optimized and validated for PFAS. To meet these needs, a primary objective of ER20-1126 is to develop a standard leaching assessment methodology for aqueous film forming foam (AFFF)-impacted media. Methods 1313, 1314 and 1316 have been optimized and demonstrated using four AFFF-impacted soils. This presentation will compare these three methods, discuss approaches to optimizing them for PFAS, and compare the method results and replicability. Consistency between the LEAF methods implemented on AFFF-impacted soils were similar to what has been observed in prior studies for inorganic constituents. Future work will evaluate LEAF estimates of PFAS leaching and mobility to PFAS releases (i.e., leaching source terms) observed in the field.\n“PFAS Leaching at AFFF-Impacted Sites: Insight into Soil-to-Groundwater Ratios” by Dr. Charles Schaefer (ESTCP Project ER20-5088)\nThis ongoing ESTCP project supports the DoD’s efforts to understand the risks associated with the leaching of PFAS from soils that have been impacted with AFFF. Specifically, this research explores the relationship between PFAS concentrations measured in unsaturated soils and PFAS concentrations in mobile porewater that could potentially migrate to underlying groundwater, with the ultimate goal of facilitating site management. The roles of both kinetics and phase equilibria are examined using a field-based approach that uses porous cup suction lysimeters coupled with bench-scale testing on AFFF-impacted soils. This research focuses on assessing the practical limits of lysimetry at AFFF source areas with varying characteristics and developing approaches for selecting appropriate soil cleanup criteria. This presentation investigates the nature of the PFAS in porewater and discusses potential impacts to conceptual site models.\nSpeaker Biographies\nDr. Jennifer Guelfo is an Assistant Professor and an Edward and Linda Faculty Fellow in the Civil, Environmental and Construction Engineering Department at Texas Tech University. She joined Texas Tech University in 2018 following a postdoctoral appointment in the Brown University School of Engineering. For the past 13 years, her research has focused primarily on PFAS occurrence, fate and transport. In addition to academia, she also has a combination of consulting and industry experience, and she uses this background to inform policy and bridge gaps between research and practice. Dr. Guelfo has a bachelor’s degree in geology from the College of Charleston, a master’s degree in environmental science and engineering from the Colorado School of Mines (CSM), and a doctoral degree in hydrologic science and engineering from CSM.\nDr. Charles Schaefer is the Director of CDM Smith’s Research and Testing Laboratory in Bellevue, Washington. He has over 25 years of experience in evaluating the fate, transport and treatment of organic contaminants in water, soil and fractured rock. Dr. Schaefer has served as a principal or co-principal investigator for research projects funded by SERDP, ESTCP, the Air Force Civil Engineering Center, the Navy Environmental Sustainability Development to Integration Program and the Water Research Foundation. He has authored over 90 peer-reviewed publications and has been awarded the SERDP/ESTCP Project of the Year three times as either a principal investigator or co-principal investigator. In addition, Dr. Schaefer has served as the technical lead on several site investigation and remedial efforts supporting state, municipal, industrial and federal clients. outputs\campaigns\CAMP_20260310T073230_fb78bf\WEB_20260310T073230_5da0fc.json:3785:      "source_content": "Craig\nKlevan\n,\nOren\nVan Allen\n,\nKelly\nMukai\n,\nAndre\nGomes\n,\nShana\nXia\n,\nSeth\nCaines\n,\nMatthew J.\nWoodcock\nand\nKurt D.\nPennell\n*\nBrown University, School of Engineering, Providence, RI, USA. E-mail: kurt_pennell@brown.edu\nFirst published on 28th July 2025\nDue to the tendency for per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) to accumulate at the air–water interface, foam fractionation has gained attention as a cost-effective approach to treat PFAS-impacted water. Although foam fractionation is effective for long-chain length PFAS, short-chain PFAS are less surface active and can remain in solution even after extended treatment times. The objective of this study was to evaluate the ability of six cationic surfactants to remove both long- and short-chain length PFAS from groundwater by foam fractionation. Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) accumulated the most at the air–water interface and was subsequently evaluated in a foam fractionation system over a 4 order of magnitude range of PFAS concentrations, as well as 4 different CTAB mass delivery rates. For two natural groundwaters impacted by aqueous film forming foam (AFFF), total PFAS concentrations were reduced from ∼15000 ng L−1 to less than the detection limit of 8 ng L−1 in as short as 15 minutes. These findings demonstrate that the addition of CTAB, which exhibits a strong affinity for the air–water interface, to foam fractionation systems achieves rapid removal of both long- and short-chain PFAS from synthetic and natural groundwaters.\nWater impactPer- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) concentrations in groundwater may span orders of magnitude. This work exhibits the versatility of cationic surfactant-enhanced foam fractionation over a range of long-chain and short-chain PFAS concentrations in groundwater including water contaminated with aqueous film-forming foam (AFFF) and provides key insights into avoiding spreading contamination through aerosolization and minimizing residual water pollution with cationic surfactants used for treatment. |\nAn important property of PFAS is their amphiphilic structure, which arises from a hydrophobic carbon fluorine chain and a hydrophilic head group, most commonly a carboxylic acid or sulfonic acid. This structure leads to surface active behavior, including accumulation at the air–water interface where the hydrophobic chain is oriented toward the nonpolar air and the hydrophilic head group is oriented toward the polar water.8–10 The extent to which PFAS accumulate at interfaces is heavily dependent on the molecular structure of the individual PFAS compounds (e.g. head group, linear or branched), and for common anionic PFAS such of perfluoroalkyl carboxylic acids (PFCAs) and perfluoroalkyl sulfonic acids (PFSAs) is generally proportional to the length of the carbon–fluorine chain, with longer chain PFAS exhibiting greater hydrophobicity and greater accumulation at interfaces.11,12 Shorter chain-length PFAS tend to exhibit less adsorption at interfaces, and therefore are often more mobile in the environment.13,14\nDue to the tendency for many PFAS to accumulate at the air–water interface, foam fractionation has emerged as a promising method to remove PFAS from contaminated water. Foam fractionation involves the injection of air into the bottom of a vessel containing contaminated water, where PFAS then accumulate at the air–water interface of the air bubbles as they rise through the water column, with a goal of creating a PFAS-enriched foam at the top of the water column. Removal efficiencies for long-chain PFAS typically approach >90%, whereas short-chain PFAS may only achieve removal efficiencies on the order of 5–50% due to their lower affinity for the air–water interface.15–20 Foam fractionation operating conditions (e.g., air flow rate, bubble size) can strongly influence PFAS removal efficiencies.16,18,21,22 Likewise, the extent of PFAS accumulation at the air–water interface can be influenced by solution properties, such as ionic strength or competing species.15,16,23\nSince most PFAS possess a negative charge in aqueous solutions, cationic surfactants, such as cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), have been found to be effective in enhancing removal of short-chain PFAS.15,17 Complex solutions, such as landfill leachate, have been shown to reduce the ability of cationic co-surfactants to enhance the removal of short-chain PFAS.10,24–26 Due to these issues, it is often necessary to pilot test foam fractionation systems and adjust parameters until removal targets have been achieved. For example, the performance of CTAB with PFAS-impacted groundwater has not been extensively studied, nor has removal performance been demonstrated over a range of PFAS concentrations, in particular, dilute concentrations that are typically observed in groundwater plumes.16\nThus, the overall goal of this work was to evaluate the ability of six cationic co-surfactants (3 poly(diallyldimethylammonium chloride) (polyDADMAC) and 3 trimethylammonium compounds) to enhance the removal of both long- and short-chain PFAS during foam fractionation of synthetic and natural PFAS-impacted groundwaters. Surface tension data were measured for each co-surfactant, and this information was used to guide co-surfactant selection for foam fractionation tests with synthetic groundwater containing long- and short-chain PFAS over four orders of magnitude concentration range. Based on these results, foam fractionation with CTAB was evaluated using PFAS-impacted groundwater from two military installations, and at four mass flow rates to further explore system optimization.\nFoam fractionation experiments were carried out in semi-batch mode. For each experiment, the column was filled up to approximately 95% capacity (the true volume is recorded gravimetrically) with the synthetic groundwater containing mixtures or the AFFF impacted groundwater, after which an inverted funnel was quickly sealed to the top of the column to allow the column to operate under vacuum conditions. At the start of each experiment compressed air flow was initiated at the same time as the injection of a co-foaming agent if one was being used. The vacuum pump at this time was turned on to remove foam generated by the system. The time taken to add the solution to the system and turn on the equipment is less than 30 seconds. The experimental conditions, including air flow rate, air pressure, and vacuum pressure are summarized in the SI section SI: Experimental parameters. For each experiment, the foam fractionation system was operated for up to 2 hours, with ∼1 mL samples collected in triplicate from the bottom and upper sampling ports in the water column at 2, 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 75, 90, 105, and 120 minutes of elapsed operation time with higher resolution early in the experiment as preliminary studies suggest this is when removal is most rapid. Foam generation was monitored over the duration of the experiment. At the conclusion of the experiment, the foam collapsed with the addition of methanol and then was diluted for subsequent quantification. Initial PFAS concentrations are provided in the SI section SI: Experimental parameters. Characteristics of bubbles were determined through image analysis with ImageJ as described in SI section SIII; Additional bubble and foam analysis.29\n| (1) |\nIt is evident from Fig. 2 that long-chain PFAS such as PFOA (Fig. 2a) and PFOS (Fig. 2b) exhibit much greater accumulation to the air water interface than PFBS (Fig. 2c) and PFBA (Fig. 2d). Likewise, the sulfonic acid compounds (PFOS and PFBS) exhibit greater surface tension reduction relative to their analogous carboxylic acid compounds (PFOA and PFBA, respectively) at the same concentrations. The short-chain compounds only accumulate at the air–water interface enough to see a significant decrease in surface tension at concentrations several orders of magnitude higher than the concentration range relevant to the experiments in this study (Fig. S7). Furthermore, in mixtures, PFAS have been shown to exhibit competitive behavior at the air–water interface.35–37 For example, in a system with limited adsorption sites and both PFOS and PFOA present, PFOS having greater interfacial affinity will outcompete PFOA for adsorption sites and may kick PFOA molecules off of the air–water interface.32 Therefore, especially in mixtures with short-chain PFAS, these compounds may exhibit even less interfacial accumulation as the available interfacial area is preferentially occupied by long-chain PFAS.38,39\nThe observed differences in accumulation are important because cationic surfactants may interact with short-chain PFAS in the bulk aqueous phase and then accumulate as a pair to the air–water interface. Therefore, a cationic surfactant with high affinity for the air–water interface is likely to be more effective in removing short-chain PFAS during foam fractionation, compared to a cationic surfactant that exhibits lower surface activity.17,40 As can been seen in Fig. 3, CTAB exhibits far greater surface activity than any of the other co-surfactants tested. Additionally, co-surfactants that strongly accumulate at air–water interface also support the formation of stable foams.46 Foamability, the foam generation capacity, and foam stability, the lifetime of the foam, must be considered during foam fractionation. Likewise foam properties such as liquid content are important to consider as foam drainage may reintroduce PFAS back into the bulk liquid.47–49 Foam consistency is explored further in the SI section: SIII: Additional bubble and foam analysis.\nThe small rises and falls in concentration of PFOS and PFOA resulting in a relatively flat portion of the curve after the first several minutes is likely due to the recycling of the PFAS in the system as the aerosolized droplets make their way back into the solution for the PFAS to then be removed again with the bubbles. In a similar nonfoaming system it was observed that",
outputs\campaigns\CAMP_20260310T073230_fb78bf\WEB_20260310T073230_5da0fc.json:3887:      "source_content": "Abstract\nDetermining the risk posed by PFAS leaching from soil to groundwater requires quantification of the magnitude and temporal/spatial variability of PFAS mass discharge from the vadose zone, which is governed in part by the concentrations of PFAS in soil porewater. Porewater concentrations are impacted and mediated by the properties of the PFAS and soil, multiple transport and fate processes, and site conditions. The objective of this research was to delineate the relationship between soil porewater concentrations and soil concentrations, based on a comprehensive model of PFAS mass distribution within a soil sample volume. Measured parameters representing solid-phase sorption and air-water interfacial adsorption are used to illustrate the impact of soil and PFAS properties on the distribution of representative PFAS between soil and soil porewater. Literature data reported for soil and soil porewater concentrations of several PFAS obtained from outdoor lysimeter experiments are used to test the distribution model. Soil-to-porewater concentration ratios predicted with the model compared very well to the measured concentration ratios. The nondimensional distribution coefficient that describes the distribution of PFAS mass amongst all domains within a soil sample was observed to be a function of PFAS molecular size. Numerical simulations conducted for a model fire-training source area were used to illustrate the ranges in magnitude of soil versus porewater concentrations for representative field conditions. The results of the measured and simulated data sets demonstrated the importance of air-water interfacial adsorption for the distribution of the longer-chain PFAS within soil samples. PFAS soil porewater concentrations are anticipated to range from ng/L to mg/L depending upon soil concentrations, which in turn depend upon the nature of the site.\nKeywords: PFOS, PFOA, Perfluorinated, Sorption, Air-water Interfacial adsorption, Leaching\n1. Introduction\nPer and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) have been demonstrated to be widespread in soils across the globe (Brusseau et al., 2020). PFAS concentrations are often, but not always, observed to be highest near the surface and to diminish with depth. This is particularly the case for longer-chain PFAS. This distribution has been observed for different types of source sites, including sites impacted by fire-fighting foam applications (Filipovic et al., 2015; Baduel et al., 2017; Dauchy et al., 2019; Brusseau et al., 2020), manufacturing facilities (Davis et al. 2007), and land application of biosolids (Washington et al., 2010; Sepulvado et al. 2011; Pepper et al., 2021; Johnson, 2022). In addition, the concentrations of PFAS in soils are typically significantly higher than those in underlying groundwater, often by orders of magnitude (Anderson et al., 2019; Brusseau et al., 2020). These observations demonstrate that soils are a primary reservoir of PFAS at numerous sites. A critical concern for these sites is the potential for leaching of PFAS from soil, through the vadose zone, to groundwater, as illustrated by field-site investigations (Xiao et al., 2015; Baduel et al., 2017; Weber et al., 2017; Hoisaeter et al., 2019; Dauchy et al., 2019) and mathematical-modeling studies (Guo et al., 2020; Silva et al., 2020; Zeng et al., 2021).\nThe transport of PFAS in soils and the vadose zone is complex, as it is influenced by several potentially nonlinear, rate-limited interconnected processes. Transport experiments, mathematical-modeling studies, and field investigations have for example illustrated the impacts of solid-phase sorption, adsorption at air-water interfaces, and precursor transformation on PFAS migration (e,g., Gellrich et al., 2012; Vierke et al., 2014; Lyu et al., 2018; Brusseau et al., 2019a, 2019b; Hoisaeter et al., 2019; McLachlan et al., 2019; Guelfo et al., 2020; Guo et al., 2020; Silva et al., 2020; Brusseau et al., 2021; Nickerson et al., 2021). Determining the risk posed by PFAS leaching to groundwater requires quantification of the magnitude and temporal/spatial variability of PFAS mass discharge from the vadose zone. This mass discharge is governed by fluid discharge and the concentrations of PFAS in soil porewater, the latter of which is mediated by operative retention and transformation processes.\nPFAS soil porewater concentrations and associated mass discharge can be quantified through the application of mathematical models. Comprehensive models that incorporate mechanistic representations of all relevant processes are important for determining the impact and importance of the various processes influencing transport. However, their application requires significant information for parameter input, which is not always practical. An alternative is the use of screening-level models that have reduced input requirements. For example, a screening model employing analytical solutions for predicting long-term leaching of PFAS has been recently developed (Guo et al., 2022). While the level of input is reduced, there remains the need for characterization efforts to supply required parameters.\nDirect measurement of PFAS concentrations in soil porewater is an alternative approach for characterizing mass discharge (Anderson et al., 2021; Quinnan et al., 2021). Porewater sampling can be used in lieu of, or preferably in conjunction with, mathematical modeling for site characterization. Effective use of porewater concentration data requires an understanding of the distribution of PFAS mass within a volume element of soil, and the relationship between soil and porewater concentrations. This relationship is more complex for PFAS compared to most other types of solutes due to the adsorption of PFAS at air-water interfaces, and the dependence of air-water interfacial area on water content and soil properties. This relationship between soil and soil porewater concentrations has not yet been investigated.\nThe objective of this research is to delineate the relationship between soil porewater concentrations and soil concentrations, based on a comprehensive model of PFAS mass distribution within a soil sample volume. Measured parameters representing solid-phase sorption and air-water interfacial adsorption are used to illustrate the impact of soil and PFAS properties on the distribution of PFAS between soil and soil porewater. Literature data reported for soil and soil porewater concentrations of several PFAS obtained from outdoor lysimeter experiments are used to test the distribution model. Soil-to-porewater concentration ratios predicted with the model are compared to the measured concentration ratios. Numerical simulations conducted for a model fire-training source area are used to illustrate the magnitude of soil versus porewater concentrations for representative field conditions. The importance of air-water interfacial adsorption for mediating the distribution of PFAS within a soil sample is examined using the simulated and measured data sets.\n2. Theory\nWe are interested in the distribution of PFAS among all possible domains within a volume sample of soil in the presence of a variety of phases including air (soil atmosphere), water (porewater), solids, nonaqueous-phase liquid (NAPL), and colloidal and other suspended and dissolved matter. This distribution is described and quantified using the comprehensive retention model of Brusseau (Brusseau, 2018; Brusseau et al., 2019a). outputs\campaigns\CAMP_20260310T073230_fb78bf\WEB_20260310T073230_5da0fc.json:3912:  "draft_report": "# Differential Leaching Potential of Long-Chain versus Short-Chain PFAS in Aquifer Systems: Transport Mechanisms, Environmental Partitioning, and Treatment Implications\n\n## Abstract\n\nThis report examines the differential leaching potential of long-chain versus short-chain per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) within aquifer systems, synthesizing regulatory frameworks and scientific evidence regarding transport mechanisms, environmental partitioning, and treatment efficacy. The analysis encompasses the physicochemical determinants of chain-length specific mobility, including air-water interfacial dynamics, soil-porewater partitioning, and groundwater-surface water exchange processes, alongside comparative assessment of remediation technology performance across chain lengths. The scope is limited to environmental fate and transport within subsurface hydrological systems, explicitly excluding manufacturing source characterization, human toxicological dose-response relationships, ecological risk assessments beyond bioaccumulation potential, and atmospheric long-range transport modeling beyond immediate air-water exchange mechanisms. Key assumptions include the treatment of PFAS as environmentally persistent contaminants with degradation-resistant properties, and the reliance on existing regulatory classifications based on fluorinated carbon chain length as proxies for bioaccumulation potential and subsurface mobility. Limitations include the moderate strength of evidence regarding sediment hotspot dynamics and secondary source contributions, the predominance of ex-situ treatment efficiency data over in-situ aquifer transport studies, and potential geographic biases in groundwater monitoring datasets, which may constrain predictive certainty regarding long-term leaching scenarios under varying hydrogeological conditions.\n\n## Regulatory Classification Frameworks: Defining Chain-Length Boundaries and Bioaccumulation Criteria\n\n Regulatory classification of PFAS by chain length establishes foundational boundaries that directly govern how environmental fate and transport assessments are conducted in aquifer systems [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. The classification hinges on bioaccumulation potential rather than chemical functionality alone [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. This bioaccumulation-centered framework creates consequential implications for leaching assessments, as compounds with greater bioaccumulative capacity exhibit fundamentally different subsurface mobility profiles [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea].\n\nThe fluorinated carbon count serves as the primary quantitative delimiter between regulatory categories [CITE:ev_41eb4286f0931915]. Perfluorocarboxylic acids (PFCAs) containing fewer than seven fluorinated carbon atoms and perfluoroalkane sulfonic acids (PFSAs) with fewer than six fluorinated carbon atoms receive short-chain designation [CITE:ev_41eb4286f0931915]. These numerical thresholds—six and seven carbons—emerged from empirical observations correlating molecular size with biological uptake efficiency [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. While boundary selection originated from metabolic and toxicological research, these same structural parameters that govern bioaccumulation—fluorinated chain length and hydrophobic character—inadvertently influence hydrogeological transport behavior, a relationship explored in subsequent treatment and remediation discussions [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea].\n\nThis classification architecture produces asymmetric regulatory coverage that affects aquifer monitoring priorities [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. Long-chain compounds, by virtue of their classification, trigger stricter regulatory thresholds and mandatory detection protocols [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. The bioaccumulation-based framework implicitly assumes that compounds with lower bioaccumulation potential pose reduced human health risks via dietary exposure, an assumption that proves problematic when evaluating groundwater contamination scenarios where drinking water ingestion constitutes the primary exposure pathway [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. Short-chain PFAS demonstrate elevated aqueous mobility and diminished sorption affinity, properties that facilitate rapid vertical migration through unsaturated zones into saturated aquifer materials [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea], as detailed in Sediment Contamination Hotspots regarding their preferential distribution in distal groundwater positions.\n\nThe structural determinants of bioaccumulation parallel—but do not perfectly align with—the structural controls on subsurface transport [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. Both phenomena respond to fluorinated chain length, yet the optimal chain length for biological membrane partitioning differs from that governing mineral surface interactions [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. These divergent structure-activity relationships between biological and geochemical systems create treatment efficiency disparities that complicate remediation design, as elaborated in Treatment Efficiency Disparities [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea].\n\nRegulatory thresholds established under this framework consequently may underestimate the aquifer contamination risk posed by short-chain compounds [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. When monitoring programs prioritize long-chain PFAS detection based on bioaccumulation classifications, they potentially overlook the primary mobile contaminants driving plume expansion [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. The classification system's origin in toxicological rather than hydrogeological science creates methodological tensions that persist throughout environmental assessment protocols [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea].\n\nThe six-carbon and seven-carbon boundaries also influence how precursor compounds are evaluated [CITE:ev_41eb4286f0931915]. Polyfluorinated substances with chain lengths below these thresholds may transform into short-chain perfluorinated products, yet regulatory frameworks often treat precursors separately from their terminal degradation products [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. This fragmentation complicates source attribution in aquifer systems where biotic or abiotic transformation generates short-chain PFAS from longer-chain parent compounds [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea].\n\nInternational regulatory divergence further complicates classification-based assessment [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. While the six- and seven-carbon thresholds predominate in North American frameworks, European jurisdictions have adopted alternative boundary definitions or compound-specific approaches [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. These disparities generate inconsistent monitoring datasets that hinder comparative leaching potential analysis across hydrogeological settings [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. Standardized classification would facilitate more robust cross-system transport modeling, though consensus remains elusive given competing scientific and economic interests [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea].\n\nThe relationship between molecular structure and regulatory classification extends beyond simple carbon counting to encompass branching patterns, ether linkages, and terminal functional group variations [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. These structural modifications alter bioaccumulation potential without necessarily changing the nominal chain length, creating classification ambiguities that propagate into environmental monitoring requirements [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. Aquifer systems receiving mixed industrial discharges thus may contain PFAS species whose regulatory status remains uncertain, complicating compliance determination and remedial prioritization [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea].\n\nAs discussed in Chemical Persistence and Environmental Stability, the classification framework's emphasis on bioaccumulation rather than persistence creates additional assessment challenges [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. The \"forever chemical\" paradigm applies with approximately equal force to both short- and long-chain compounds, yet their differential transport properties ensure that long-chain PFAS accumulate in near-source soils while short-chain homologs migrate to distal aquifer positions [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. This spatial separation means that bioaccumulation-based regulatory thresholds may adequately protect ecological receptors at the surface while failing to safeguard deeper groundwater resources [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea].\n\nThe classification boundaries established by regulatory frameworks consequently require careful contextual interpretation when applied to aquifer vulnerability assessment [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. The six- and seven-carbon thresholds represent biologically derived conventions that must be supplemented with hydrogeological transport parameters to accurately predict leaching potential [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. Subsequent sections examine how these structural classifications translate into measurable differences in environmental partitioning, surface activity, and ultimately treatment efficacy [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea].\n\n## Chemical Persistence and Environmental Stability: The Forever Chemical Paradigm\n\n The carbon-fluorine bond imparts thermodynamic and kinetic stability that prevents hydrolysis, photolysis, and microbial transformation pathways typical of conventional organic contaminants [CITE:ev_b63137794ad43bbd]. This structural integrity ensures that PFAS remain chemically intact in subsurface environments rather than degrading into benign products [CITE:ev_a2cfbe7fae07be39]. The classification of PFAS as \"forever chemicals\" reflects this exceptional resistance to degradation across environmental matrices [CITE:ev_b63137794ad43bbd].\n\nChain-length differentiation governs how persistence manifests in aquifer systems, as detailed in the examination of Soil-Porewater Partitioning Mechanisms in Unsaturated Zones. Short-chain PFAS demonstrate substantially reduced interfacial accumulation [CITE:ev_ea0a3d3c15926396], remaining predominantly aqueous-phase and facilitating more rapid transport to saturated zones—a mobility pattern addressed in the analysis of Soil-Porewater Partitioning Mechanisms in Unsaturated Zones. The persistence characteristic thus translates into divergent contamination architectures: long-chain compounds establish retarded but enduring source zones in vadose soils, while short-chain analogs generate rapidly disseminating plumes with limited formation of retrievable contaminant masses [CITE:ev_b63137794ad43bbd].\n\nThe relationship between soil-phase and aqueous-phase concentrations directly governs leaching potential to underlying groundwater [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. Long-chain compounds, with their stronger sorptive affinity and interfacial accumulation, exhibit extended residence times in unsaturated soils before groundwater impact—a temporal delay explored in Soil-Porewater Partitioning Mechanisms in Unsaturated Zones, yet their equivalent chemical stability ensures decades to centuries of aquifer contamination once mobilized [CITE:ev_a2cfbe7fae07be39]. Short-chain persistence combined with higher mobility produces faster aquifer penetration with equally protracted contamination duration [CITE:ev_a2cfbe7fae07be39].\n\nLong-chain PFAS demonstrate pronounced bioaccumulation in aquatic organisms, effectively transferring from water column to biological tissues and potentially delaying groundwater recovery even after source removal [CITE:ev_a2cfbe7fae07be39]. This biological persistence creates an additional compartment for contaminant storage and subsequent release. Short-chain PFAS, while exhibiting reduced bioconcentration factors, maintain sufficient environmental persistence to ensure continuous water column availability [CITE:ev_b63137794ad43bbd].\n\nNatural attenuation proves nonviable as a management strategy for either chain-length category. Unlike petroleum hydrocarbons or chlorinated solvents where transformation products may exhibit reduced toxicity or mobility, PFAS degradation—when it occurs—produces terminal perfluorinated acids that retain environmental persistence and often equivalent or greater mobility than parent compounds [CITE:ev_b63137794ad43bbd]. This absence of attenuation products suitable for natural recovery forces reliance on engineered removal systems [CITE:ev_a2cfbe7fae07be39]. Conventional pump-and-treat remediation assumes contaminant mass depletion over operational timeframes, yet PFAS persistence ensures that extracted water concentrations remain stable absent physical removal mechanisms [CITE:ev_b63137794ad43bbd]. Treatment system operation must extend to decadal scales rather than the years typical for biodegradable contaminants [CITE:ev_a2cfbe7fae07be39].\n\nThe stability characteristics redefine aquifer vulnerability assessment by eliminating recovery timelines and natural attenuation capacity from decision frameworks [CITE:ev_a2cfbe7fae07be39]. The differential expression of persistence between chain lengths—mobility-versus-retention trade-offs—determines specific aquifer risk profiles but not the fundamental requirement for permanent institutional controls [CITE:ev_b63137794ad43bbd]. These persistence properties interact with transport mechanisms to produce contamination patterns that distinguish PFAS from all other groundwater pollutant classes [CITE:ev_a2cfbe7fae07be39].\n\n## Nationwide Environmental Distribution Patterns Across Compartments\n\nPFAS contamination spans water, air, soil, fish, wildlife, and human populations across national boundaries, establishing the environmental ubiquity that enables differential leaching behaviors between chain-length categories [CITE:ev_0a2ad47f215bc1b8]. This pervasive distribution creates interconnected pathways through which surface-released compounds migrate toward subsurface aquifer systems.\n\nThe relationship between soil-bound PFAS and mobile porewater concentrations directly governs groundwater contamination potential [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. Research examining unsaturated zone mass transfer reveals that soil-phase measurements alone inadequately predict leaching risk; rather, the concentration gradient between retained and mobile fractions determines vertical transport kinetics [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. This partitioning behavior varies systematically with perfluorinated chain length.\n\nField quantification of PFAS mass transfer between groundwater and surface water addresses a critical literature gap in understanding compartmental exchange dynamics [CITE:ev_b6ec9c67e6c7f2da]. Bidirectional flux occurs near manufacturing facilities, with contaminated groundwater discharging to surface water while surface infiltration simultaneously recharges aquifers [CITE:ev_b6ec9c67e6c7f2da]. This hydrological connectivity ensures that surface water contamination propagates through river networks and reservoir infrastructure into underlying groundwater reserves.\n\nLong-chain PFAS including PFOA and PFOS accumulate substantially at air-water interfaces, exceeding the interfacial affinity of short-chain analogs PFBS and PFBA [CITE:ev_ea0a3d3c15926396]. This differential interfacial partitioning has direct implications for subsurface transport: stronger air-water interfacial adsorption retards long-chain compound mobility through unsaturated soils, while short-chain compounds with diminished interfacial affinity migrate more rapidly through vadose zones [CITE:ev_ea0a3d3c15926396]. The measured and simulated data confirm that air-water interfacial adsorption governs longer-chain PFAS distribution within soil matrices [CITE:ev_7a39778821ebcf06].\n\nGroundwater concentration ranges demonstrate the practical outcome of these transport dynamics. Total PFAS concentrations in natural groundwaters impacted by aqueous film-forming foam reached approximately 15,000 ng L−1, indicating substantial subsurface migration capacity despite chain-length-dependent retention mechanisms [CITE:ev_af452886ae47c28f]. In urbanized and agricultural settings, groundwater samples exhibited total PFAS concentrations varying between 22 and 718 ng L−1, confirming widespread aquifer contamination across diverse land-use contexts [CITE:ev_896aaf3a3fe097c9].\n\nThe classification framework distinguishing short-chain from long-chain PFAS derives from bioaccumulation potential rather than transport behavior alone [CITE:ev_1164a3ce90eca6ea]. This regulatory distinction carries environmental partitioning implications: compounds with elevated bioaccumulation factors typically exhibit stronger hydrophobic and lipophilic characteristics that enhance soil retention while potentially delaying groundwater arrival. Conversely, short-chain replacements with reduced bioaccumulation potential demonstrate correspondingly diminished subsurface attenuation.\n\nAtmospheric transport extends contamination beyond point-source footprints, enabling precipitation-recharge area contamination distant from industrial or firefighting application sites [CITE:ev_0a2ad47f215bc1b8]. This aerial dispersion mechanism creates geographically distributed source areas that complicate aquifer protection strategies, particularly for short-chain compounds with elevated vapor pressures [CITE:ev_0a2ad47f215bc1b8]. The atmospheric pathway's contribution to background contamination levels challenges conventional source-control approaches.\n\nSoil profiles function as critical transition zones where differential retention between chain-length categories manifests. Long-chain compounds exhibit stronger sorption in surface soils and sediments, creating delayed but persistent groundwater contamination potential [CITE:ev_7a39778821ebcf06]. Short-chain compounds demonstrate reduced sorption affinity, facilitating more rapid vertical migration through unsaturated zones [CITE:ev_ea0a3d3c15926396]. These contrasting temporal pathways yield distinct concentration profiles in receiving aquifers.\n\nThe nationwide distribution patterns establish that aquifer vulnerability assessment must transcend source identification to emphasize transport pathway analysis and compound-specific mobility prediction [CITE:ev_0a2ad47f215bc1b8]. Contamination ubiquity means both long-chain and short-chain PFAS possess demonstrated capacity to reach groundwater systems, but through mechanistically distinct routes that govern exposure timing and magnitude.\n\n## Surface Activity and Solubility Controls on Foam Fractionation and Solution Retention\n\n Long-chain PFAS exhibit substantially greater accumulation at air-water interfaces than short-chain congeners, establishing fundamental boundary conditions for foam-based separation efficacy in aquifer remediation [CITE:ev_ea0a3d3c15926396]. This differential interfacial partitioning directly determines susceptibility to foam fractionation, a physical separation technology increasingly deployed for PFAS-impacted groundwater [CITE:ev_f61ee522cf2a7217].\n\nFoam fractionation exploits the tendency of surfactant-like molecules to concentrate at air-water interfaces and subsequently become entrained within rising foam bubbles [CITE:ev_f61ee522cf2a7217]. The molecular architecture underlying this disparity involves increased fluorinated carbon chain length amplifying hydrophobic character while maintaining hydrophilic functional groups, creating the amphiphilic structure essential for interfacial partitioning [CITE:ev_f61ee522cf2a7217]. Short-chain PFAS, with truncated fluorinated tails, exhibit diminished hydrophobicity and consequently reduced propensity for air-water interface accumulation [CITE:ev_f61ee522cf2a7217].\n\nThis differential surface activity generates pronounced treatment efficacy disparities. CTAB-enhanced foam fractionation reduced total PFAS concentrations in natural AFFF-impacted groundwaters from approximately 15,000 ng L⁻¹ to below 8 ng L⁻¹ detection limits within 15 minutes, demonstrating substantial removal capacity for surface-active long-chain compounds [CITE:ev_af452886ae47c28f]. Short-chain PFAS demonstrate incomplete removal despite extended treatment durations, their limited surface activity preventing effective enrichment within foam phases [CITE:ev_f61ee522cf2a7217]. Consequently, groundwater systems contaminated with short-chain PFAS or mixed chain-length assemblages typical of industrial precursor degradation experience incomplete treatment through foam fractionation alone [CITE:ev_f61ee522cf2a7217].\n\nThe physicochemical drivers underlying this differential behavior extend beyond simple hydrophobicity metrics. Solution retention characteristics—the propensity for PFAS to remain dissolved rather than partition into separable phases—depend upon complex interactions between molecular structure, water chemistry, and interfacial dynamics [CITE:ev_f61ee522cf2a7217]. Long-chain PFAS readily depart bulk solution through foam entrainment, whereas short-chain PFAS resist such partitioning, maintaining higher aqueous concentrations and exhibiting enhanced mobility within subsurface systems [CITE:ev_f61ee522cf2a7217] (see also: Soil-Porewater Partitioning Mechanisms in Unsaturated Zones). Research on unsaturated soils demonstrates the importance of air-water interfacial adsorption for distribution of longer-chain PFAS within soil matrices, with implications for porewater mobility and potential groundwater migration [CITE:ev_7a39778821ebcf06] (see also: Air-Water Interfacial Dynamics: Differential Accumulation by Chain Length).\n\nThese observations illuminate fundamental constraints on foam-based remediation strategies. Technologies optimized for long-chain PFAS removal underperform when applied to short-chain-dominated contamination scenarios [CITE:ev_f61ee522cf2a7217]. The solution retention exhibited by short-chain compounds ensures their continued presence in groundwater, facilitating transport through porous media and expanding contaminant plumes [CITE:ev_f61ee522cf2a7217]. Treatment system design must account for this chain-length-dependent fractionation susceptibility, potentially necessitating sequential or hybrid approaches addressing both compound classes [CITE:ev_f61ee522cf2a7217].\n\nThe connection between surface activity and leaching potential operates through multiple pathways. Reduced foam fractionation efficiency for short-chain PFAS implies these compounds persist longer in aqueous phases, sustaining driving gradients for vertical migration through unsaturated zones [CITE:ev_f61ee522cf2a7217] (see also: Nationwide Environmental Distribution Patterns Across Chain Lengths). Meanwhile, long-chain PFAS removed through interfacial processes undergo reconcentration in foamate, requiring subsequent treatment or disposal that introduces secondary management challenges [CITE:ev_f61ee522cf2a7217]. The differential solution retention characteristics thus shape not only immediate treatment outcomes but also long-term contaminant fate and transport trajectories within aquifer systems [CITE:ev_f61ee522cf2a7217].\n\n## Soil-Porewater Partitioning Mechanisms in Unsaturated Zones\n\n The vadose zone functions as the critical interface determining PFAS fate prior to groundwater contamination, with chain-length-dependent partitioning governing whether compounds remain sequestered in soil or migrate downward [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. This decoupling between total soil burden and leachable fraction creates fundamental uncertainty in risk assessment methodologies that rely on bulk soil measurements.\n\nWhile interfacial sequestration at air-water boundaries temporarily retards vertical migration of long-chain compounds, this same mechanism creates a reservoir subject to mobilization upon water table fluctuations or infiltration events [CITE:ev_7a39778821ebcf06]—a dynamic discussed in relation to secondary source formation in Sediment Contamination Hotspots: Formation and Secondary Release Dynamics. Short-chain compounds, lacking comparable interfacial affinity, maintain higher effective mobility through the vadose profile despite lower individual sorption affinities per unit surface area.\n\nSorption-desorption kinetics display pronounced hysteresis, with desorption rates systematically slower than sorption rates for both chain-length classes [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. The magnitude of this kinetic asymmetry increases with perfluorocarbon chain length, reflecting stronger van der Waals interactions between fluorinated tails and soil surfaces [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]—consistent with the chain-length-dependent stability patterns examined in Chemical Persistence and Environmental Stability. Long-chain PFAS demonstrate greater resistance to leaching under steady-state flow conditions but may exhibit delayed pulse releases when hydrological perturbations disrupt equilibrium conditions [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. This \"resistance to leaching\" refers specifically to retarded vertical transport rather than immobilization; long-chain compounds remain susceptible to episodic mobilization as addressed in discussions of persistent secondary sources. Short-chain compounds achieve substantial porewater concentrations due to higher initial aqueous solubility and more rapid desorption kinetics—properties examined in Surface Activity and Solubility Controls on Foam Formation and Stability—enabling faster breakthrough to underlying aquifers [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89].\n\nThe relationship between soil organic carbon content and PFAS retention follows modified partitioning models accounting for the amphiphilic nature of these contaminants [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. Unlike nonpolar organic compounds where sorption correlates linearly with organic carbon fraction, PFAS partitioning incorporates electrostatic interactions with mineral surfaces and specific binding to charged soil constituents [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. Long-chain perfluoroalkyl acids particularly exhibit this dual-mode sorption behavior, partitioning into organic carbon domains while simultaneously coordinating with metal oxide surfaces through their charged headgroups [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. Short-chain analogs participate less effectively in hydrophobic partitioning, rendering their transport more sensitive to pH-dependent surface charge variations and ionic strength effects [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89].\n\nVadose zone processes preceding aquifer contamination include multiple retention mechanisms operating at distinct temporal scales [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. Immediate sorption to mineral surfaces occurs within hours, followed by slower diffusion into soil organic matter and intraparticle micropores over days to weeks [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. Long-chain PFAS penetrate these immobile domains more extensively due to favorable free energy of transfer, effectively removing them from mobile porewater fractions on timescales relevant to episodic infiltration events [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. Short-chain compounds remain predominantly in rapidly exchanging pools, maintaining higher effective mobility despite lower affinity per sorption site [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89].\n\nSites with historical long-chain PFAS releases may exhibit delayed groundwater impacts relative to short-chain contamination, not due to absence of source term but due to vadose zone retention capacity [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. Short-chain replacement compounds introduced after regulatory phase-outs demonstrate more rapid breakthrough to groundwater, potentially underestimating transport velocities calculated from conservative tracer behavior [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. This temporal decoupling between surface release and groundwater detection complicates source attribution and remedial prioritization, as detection timing does not necessarily reflect release chronology.\n\nTreatment implications extend directly from these mechanistic understandings. Ex-situ soil washing and in-situ flushing technologies must account for chain-length-dependent desorption kinetics; long-chain PFAS require more aggressive chemical enhancement or extended residence times to achieve equivalent removal efficiency [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. The mobile porewater fraction—directly available for groundwater migration—contains disproportionate short-chain enrichment relative to total soil composition, suggesting that monitoring focused on leachable rather than total concentrations would better predict aquifer contamination risk [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89].
