This mechanistic asymmetry necessitates distinct analytical frameworks for exposure assessment and remedial design, as uniform treatment approaches fail to account for the divergent fate pathways established through these coupled physicochemical processes.\n\n## Remediation Methodologies: Cationic Surfactants and Foam Fractionation Applications\n\nAdditionally, this differential surface activity originates from the pronounced hydrophobic character conferred by extended fluorinated chains, which drives preferential orientation and concentration at phase boundaries. The technological implementation of this principle has been systematically evaluated through examination of six distinct cationic surfactant formulations. For instance, these surfactants function by stabilizing foam structures while enhancing PFAS partitioning to the interface, with molecular design parameters—including hydrocarbon tail length, charge density, and counterion composition—modulating both foam stability and PFAS-enrichment capacity. Additionally, surfactant selection becomes particularly consequential when addressing mixed-chain-length contamination, as competitive interfacial partitioning may favor long-chain species and leave short-chain counterparts inadequately extracted in treated effluent —a limitation examined in detail in the Treatment Efficiency Disparities section regarding short-chain remediation challenges. Empirical validation demonstrates substantial removal capabilities under optimized conditions, though with important chain-length-dependent qualifications. However, this aggregate performance metric masks substantial disparities in individual species removal: while long-chain PFAS achieve near-complete extraction, short-chain analogs demonstrate markedly diminished removal efficiencies, reflecting the underlying surface-activity differential. The optimistic framing of total PFAS reduction must be tempered by recognition that short-chain species—increasingly prevalent in contemporary AFFF formulations and environmental degradation products—may persist at 50% or greater residual concentrations despite aggressive treatment parameters. Operational constraints fundamentally shape aquifer remediation applicability. In addition, the technology requires ex situ implementation, necessitating groundwater extraction and above-ground treatment before potential reinjection or discharge. This engineering configuration limits deployment for deep confined aquifers or regions where water table depression is environmentally or economically prohibitive, constraints that intersect with groundwater-surface water exchange dynamics discussed in the corresponding hydrological section. Moreover, for accessible unconfined aquifers, the method offers distinct waste management advantages over activated carbon or ion exchange: concentrated PFAS-laden foam fractions amenable to subsequent destruction technologies replace spent solid waste requiring landfill disposal —a characteristic relevant to sediment contamination hotspot management where concentrated waste streams require controlled destruction. Treatment system design must accommodate chain-length-specific performance characteristics. Long-chain PFAS removal achieves satisfactory efficiencies with standard cationic surfactant formulations and conventional bubble generation parameters. Indeed, short-chain PFAS remediation demands substantially modified approaches—increased surfactant concentrations, reduced bubble sizes to enhance interfacial area, or sequential treatment stages—that escalate operational costs and energy requirements. These economic and engineering trade-offs reflect a broader technological limitation: surface-activity-based separation methods face fundamental constraints with minimally hydrophobic short-chain perfluorinated compounds. Furthermore, the environmental significance of chain-length transformations—whether through natural precursor degradation or engineered treatment—warrants careful consideration. Conversion of more extractable long-chain species to recalcitrant short-chain forms may inadvertently degrade remediation effectiveness, as the latter demonstrate markedly diminished foam fractionation amenability. This risk reinforces the necessity for treatment trains that address both chain length categories rather than relying on single-technology solutions. Furthermore, analytical considerations extend to surfactant residual management. Cationic surfactants introduced to enhance PFAS removal may persist in treated water, potentially affecting downstream biological processes or requiring additional treatment stages. In addition, the environmental fate of these auxiliary chemicals in aquifer systems—should treated water be reinjected—must be evaluated against PFAS concentration reduction benefits. Source zone characteristics further modulate implementation: high-concentration zones may overwhelm surfactant binding capacity, necessitating pretreatment or staged application, while dilute plumes permit reduced surfactant loading with improved process economics. Current technological development reflects recognition that conventional PFAS remediation approaches exhibit chain-length-dependent performance limitations. In addition, the six-surfactant evaluation establishes foundational understanding toward expanding effective treatment range, though substantial performance gaps remain for short-chain species. Future advancement toward multifunctional formulations capable of engaging short-chain PFAS through alternative binding mechanisms beyond hydrophobic interaction alone would address this fundamental constraint and support more comprehensive aquifer restoration objectives. Foam fractionation operational parameters exhibit pronounced sensitivity to groundwater geochemistry, with ionic strength and dissolved organic matter content modulating surfactant-PFAS complex stability and interfacial partitioning dynamics. Elevated concentrations of competing anions, particularly sulfate and bicarbonate, can destabilize cationic surfactant foams through charge screening effects, necessitating formulation adjustments that increase surfactant dosage requirements and associated treatment costs. In addition, the six-surfactant evaluation systematically examined these matrix effects across representative groundwater compositions, establishing operational envelopes within which chain-length-dependent removal efficiencies remain achievable. Temperature fluctuations present additional engineering considerations. Reduced groundwater temperatures, characteristic of deeper aquifer systems, diminish surfactant solubility and increase solution viscosity, impairing bubble rise velocities and foam drainage rates. Nevertheless, elevated temperatures enhance surfactant mobility but accelerate foam collapse, reducing PFAS enrichment factors. The optimal temperature window for cationic surfactant foam fractionation spans approximately, conditions most readily achievable in temperate unconfined aquifer applications where groundwater extraction temperatures approximate ambient surface conditions. In addition, the six distinct surfactant formulations evaluated encompassed structural variations designed to probe structure-performance relationships: alkyltrimethylammonium species with C12–C18 hydrocarbon tails, dialkyldimethylammonium compounds, and benzalkonium-derived structures incorporating aromatic moieties. These molecular architectures generate divergent interfacial film properties—elasticity, surface viscosity, and electrostatic characteristics—that differentially accommodate PFAS partitioning. Notably, surfactants with intermediate tail lengths (C14–C16) demonstrated superior performance across the chain-length spectrum, achieving the most favorable compromise between foam stability and short-chain PFAS accessibility to the air-water interface. Comparative assessment against alternative surfactant classes—anionic, nonionic, and zwitterionic—establishes cationic formulations as uniquely advantageous for PFAS foam fractionation. Anionic surfactants generate electrostatic repulsion with anionic PFAS species, severely limiting interfacial co-adsorption. Furthermore, nonionic surfactants lack the electrostatic attraction mechanism that enhances PFAS partitioning in cationic systems. Zwitterionic compounds exhibit pH-dependent charge characteristics that introduce operational complexity inconsistent with robust field deployment. Process intensification strategies examined in conjunction with the six-surfactant evaluation include foam reflux operation, wherein collapsed foam liquid is recycled to the feed stream to enhance PFAS concentration factors. Nevertheless, this modification improves overall system efficiency but introduces surfactant accumulation risks requiring careful mass balance management. The concentrated foamate produced—containing PFAS enriched by factors of 10²–10³ relative to influent concentrations—demands subsequent treatment, with thermal destruction or supercritical water oxidation representing viable destruction pathways that complete the remediation chain. In addition, scale-up considerations from laboratory evaluation to field implementation necessitate recognition of hydrodynamic non-idealities. Column diameter effects on bubble size distribution, gas distributor fouling under prolonged operation, and surfactant degradation through microbial or oxidative pathways in recycled systems all introduce performance uncertainties not fully captured in controlled experimental conditions. Nevertheless, the systematic six-surfactant evaluation provides essential foundational data for predictive model development and process optimization, establishing quantitative relationships between surfactant molecular parameters and PFAS removal outcomes that inform technology selection for site-specific aquifer remediation challenges. The interfacial partitioning behavior of PFAS species exhibits direct correspondence with their environmental mobility profiles in aquifer systems. Long-chain PFAS such as PFOA and PFOS demonstrate substantially greater accumulation at the air-water interface compared to short-chain analogs including PFBS and PFBA, a differential that simultaneously enables efficient foam fractionation extraction while indicating their relative retention in vadose zone environments through capillary-facilitated interfacial adsorption. Indeed, this surface-activity gradient carries significant implications for leaching potential assessment: compounds most amenable to surfactant-enhanced removal are conversely those most prone to attenuation in unsaturated soil profiles through air-water interfacial trapping mechanisms. The measured and simulated data sets demonstrate that longer-chain PFAS distribution within soil samples depends critically on air-water interfacial adsorption processes, suggesting that high foam fractionation efficiency for these species correlates with reduced vertical migration risk in typical aquifer overburden materials. Short-chain PFAS present inverse characteristics—diminished foam fractionation amenability yet enhanced subsurface mobility due to weaker interfacial partitioning and greater aqueous solubility. In addition, this creates a fundamental remediation paradox: the species most likely to reach groundwater through rapid leaching are precisely those most resistant to surfactant-based extraction technologies. The classification of short- and long-chain PFAS based on bioaccumulation potential further complicates risk prioritization, as regulatory frameworks emphasizing bioaccumulation may underweight the hydrological hazards posed by mobile short-chain compounds that bypass natural attenuation mechanisms. In addition, empirical validation of cationic surfactant foam fractionation under realistic groundwater conditions demonstrates substantial treatment capabilities that must be interpreted through chain-length-specific lenses. However, this aggregate performance obscures species-dependent extraction efficiencies: the near-complete elimination of total PFAS concentrations masks persistent short-chain residuals that may constitute environmentally significant fractions when initial plume compositions reflect contemporary AFFF formulations or precursor degradation products. The six-surfactant evaluation framework provides systematic comparison of molecular design parameters, yet acknowledges that short-chain PFAS removal requires substantially modified operational approaches that escalate energy and chemical inputs. Groundwater-surface water exchange dynamics introduce additional complexity for aquifer remediation design. Field-based quantification of PFAS mass transfer between groundwater and surface water reveals transport pathways that bypass conventional extraction well capture zones, suggesting that foam fractionation implementation must account for hydrological connectivity that may deliver short-chain PFAS to receiving waters before treatment system intervention. Additionally, the persistence characteristics of PFAS as \"forever chemicals\" that resist environmental degradation amplify the significance of chain-length-dependent removal efficiency gaps. Unlike biodegradable contaminants where partial extraction may suffice for natural attenuation completion, PFAS species escaping foam fractionation retain indefinite aquifer residence potential. This necessitates treatment train approaches that combine surfactant-enhanced extraction with destructive technologies capable of addressing concentrated short-chain residuals in foamate streams, rather than reliance on single-technology solutions with inherent chain-length selectivity limitations. Specifically, the six-surfactant evaluation framework specifically targeted cationic formulations including CTAB (cetyltrimethylammonium bromide) to probe structure-performance relationships across chain-length variants. CTAB-enhanced systems demonstrated exceptional aggregate performance, reducing total PFAS concentrations from approximately 15,000 ng L⁻¹ to below 8 ng L⁻¹ detection limits within 15 minutes under optimized conditions. However, the substantial aggregate removal efficiency must be interpreted cautiously: the detection limit threshold masks differential species persistence, with short-chain residuals potentially representing environmentally significant fractions when contemporary AFFF formulations dominate source composition. The air-water interfacial accumulation differential between chain-length categories carries direct implications for vadose zone attenuation mechanisms. Long-chain PFAS including PFOA and PFOS demonstrate markedly greater interfacial accumulation than short-chain analogs PFBS and PFBA, a characteristic that simultaneously enables efficient foam fractionation while indicating enhanced retention in unsaturated soil profiles through capillary-facilitated interfacial trapping. However, research examining PFAS distribution in unsaturated soils confirms that air-water interfacial adsorption governs longer-chain PFAS retention within soil samples, establishing an inverse relationship between foam fractionation amenability and leaching potential. This correlation between surface activity and subsurface mobility generates divergent risk profiles: compounds most resistant to groundwater penetration are paradoxically those most amenable to surfactant-based extraction. Furthermore, research exploring soil-porewater concentration relationships demonstrates that short-chain species maintain elevated mobility in porewater fractions capable of migrating to underlying aquifers. This creates a fundamental treatment paradox where the PFAS most likely to reach groundwater through rapid leaching are precisely those most resistant to cationic surfactant extraction technologies. The environmental persistence of PFAS as \"forever chemicals\" that resist natural degradation amplifies the significance of chain-length-dependent removal gaps. Moreover, unlike biodegradable contaminants where partial extraction suffice for attenuation completion, PFAS species escaping foam fractionation retain indefinite aquifer residence potential. This necessitates treatment train approaches combining surfactant-enhanced extraction with destructive technologies rather than reliance on single-technology solutions with inherent chain-length selectivity limitations.\n\n## Treatment Efficiency Disparities: The Short-Chain Removal Challenge\n\nIndeed, removal efficiencies for long-chain PFAS typically exceed 90%, whereas short-chain PFAS removal reaches only 50% or less under comparable operational conditions [11]. Furthermore, this differential treatment response amplifies the environmental mobility challenges identified throughout this analysis: compounds exhibiting greater subsurface mobility simultaneously resist capture by conventional and advanced treatment technologies, creating a compounding effect with the transport dynamics discussed in Section 'Sediment Contamination Hotspots: Formation and Secondary Release' [11]. The technological gap means that short-chain PFAS, already predisposed to extensive aquifer transport due to weaker soil sorption characteristics as detailed in that same section, encounter fewer effective barriers to long-range migration. The mechanistic basis for this disparity in unenhanced systems resides in surface-active properties governing foam fractionation efficacy. Additionally, as established in Section 'Air-Water Interfacial Dynamics: Different', long-chain PFAS exhibit substantially greater accumulation at air-water interfaces than short-chain analogs, a differential that directly translates to separation performance. Short-chain compounds exhibit markedly diminished surface activity, limiting their transfer to foam fractions and necessitating substantially longer residence times or modified operational parameters to achieve comparable removal [11]. Furthermore, this physicochemical constraint represents a fundamental technological boundary for conventional foam fractionation rather than an engineering optimization challenge. Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) enhancement demonstrates potential pathways for addressing this disparity, as examined in Section 'Remediation Methodologies: Cationic Surfactants and PFAS'. The cationic surfactant operates through dual mechanisms: direct competition for air-water interface occupancy that displaces PFAS molecules, and complexation with anionic PFAS species that modifies their partitioning behavior. Furthermore, this approach acknowledges that short-chain removal requires fundamental modification of separation chemistry rather than incremental parameter adjustment. Field validation in AFFF-impacted natural groundwaters illustrates the performance characteristics of enhanced treatment. In addition, this exceptional bulk reduction performance demonstrates the efficacy of surfactant enhancement for achieving low total concentrations, though compound-specific removal kinetics may vary. The 15-minute timeframe represents optimal laboratory conditions; practical full-scale applications face constraints from groundwater geochemistry variability, competing organic matter, and surfactant dosage optimization that may differentially affect short-chain removal sustainability. Treatment efficiency disparities carry profound implications for aquifer management strategies. Moreover, remediation systems designed around long-chain PFAS behavior will systematically underperform for short-chain contamination, potentially creating false confidence in protective efficacy while mobile short-chain plumes continue expanding. This technological blind spot necessitates compound-specific performance validation rather than reliance on bulk PFAS metrics that obscure differential removal. Additionally, monitoring frameworks must explicitly quantify short-chain species to verify treatment effectiveness, as total PFAS reduction may primarily reflect long-chain capture while short-chain concentrations persist above regulatory thresholds. The convergence of transport and treatment disadvantages for short-chain PFAS demands integrated assessment of leaching potential. Compounds exhibiting 50% or lower removal efficiency enter aquifer systems with reduced attenuation capacity [11], while their intrinsic mobility characteristics enable faster and more extensive plume migration. In addition, this dual disadvantage suggests that short-chain PFAS contamination may represent the dominant long-term groundwater quality challenge even where total contamination burdens are lower than historical long-chain releases. Notably, primary treatment targeting long-chain PFAS removal should be coupled with secondary processes specifically engineered for short-chain capture, potentially including advanced oxidation, ion exchange resins with tailored selectivity, or membrane separations with appropriate molecular weight cutoffs. The economic and operational complexity of such multi-barrier systems underscores the prevention imperative: controlling short-chain PFAS source terms offers greater protective efficacy than reliance on attenuation technologies with inherent performance limitations for mobile compounds. The differential treatment response ultimately reinforces the central thesis regarding differential leaching potential. Moreover, short-chain PFAS combine environmental persistence, subsurface mobility, and technological resistance in a manner that challenges conventional groundwater protection paradigms developed around less mobile contaminants. Effective aquifer management requires explicit recognition that the compounds most difficult to remove from water are simultaneously those most likely to migrate extensively within aquifer systems, necessitating protective strategies that prioritize source control and early interception over reliance on in situ or ex situ treatment efficacy. Moreover, the CTAB enhancement mechanism operates through rapid kinetic partitioning that overcomes the surface activity limitations constraining unenhanced systems. This performance validates that surfactant enhancement fundamentally alters separation chemistry rather than merely optimizing existing parameters. Moreover, the dual mechanism—interface competition and anionic complexation—enables simultaneous capture of both long- and short-chain species that unenhanced systems cannot achieve. However, the 15-minute treatment timeframe reflects controlled laboratory conditions with optimized CTAB dosages and minimal competing matrix constituents. Practical deployment faces substantial constraints from variable groundwater geochemistry, including dissolved organic carbon interference, ionic strength fluctuations, and pH-dependent surfactant speciation that may differentially compromise short-chain removal sustainability. The concentration reduction from 15,000 ng L⁻¹ to <8 ng L⁻¹, while demonstrating exceptional bulk performance, does not guarantee equivalent removal efficiency for individual short-chain compounds whose partitioning behavior varies with perfluorinated chain length and headgroup chemistry. The technological implications extend beyond single-process optimization to systemic treatment architecture. Significantly, the 50% or lower unenhanced removal efficiency for short-chain PFAS establishes a performance floor that conventional engineering cannot breach without chemical enhancement. This constraint positions CTAB-enhanced foam fractionation as a potential primary treatment component, though its effectiveness as a standalone solution remains contingent on sustained surfactant dosing and matrix-specific optimization. In addition, the rapid kinetic achievement of bulk reduction suggests that enhanced foam fractionation may serve effectively as a pretreatment stage concentrating PFAS for subsequent destruction technologies, thereby addressing the volume reduction challenge that limits advanced oxidation process economic viability. Compound-specific removal validation becomes critical given that bulk metrics may obscure persistent short-chain contamination. However, the reduction to <8 ng L⁻¹ total PFAS does not preclude residual short-chain concentrations above regulatory thresholds, as these compounds contribute disproportionately less to total mass while presenting disproportionately greater mobility risk. Additionally, monitoring frameworks must therefore implement speciated analysis rather than total PFAS quantification to verify treatment effectiveness against compound-specific standards. The convergence of rapid enhanced treatment kinetics with inherent unenhanced system limitations underscores the compound-specific nature of technological solutions. Furthermore, the transition from long-chain to short-chain PFAS in industrial formulations, driven by bioaccumulation-based regulatory pressure, has transferred environmental risk to compounds that combine maximum subsurface mobility with minimum technological capturability—characteristics that challenge legacy groundwater protection frameworks developed around less mobile, more treatable contaminants. The evaluation of six cationic surfactants for PFAS removal from groundwater demonstrates that CTAB represents one of multiple chemical enhancement pathways, though its specific performance characteristics relative to alternative surfactants remain subject to comparative validation. This broader surfactant screening suggests that the enhancement approach itself—rather than any single compound—constitutes the critical innovation for addressing short-chain removal limitations. The mechanistic versatility of cationic surfactants, operating through both interface competition and electrostatic complexation, provides a template for future optimization that may yield superior cost-effectiveness or reduced environmental persistence compared to CTAB specifically. The relationship between soil-phase PFAS and mobile porewater concentrations directly informs treatment efficiency disparities at the source-pathway interface. Additionally, research quantifying PFAS mass transfer between soil and porewater reveals that unsaturated zone processes govern the contaminant loading delivered to groundwater systems. Treatment technologies encounter not merely dissolved-phase contamination but dynamically equilibrating systems where soil reservoirs continuously replenish groundwater concentrations. Additionally, this has particular significance for short-chain PFAS: their weaker soil sorption drives higher porewater fractions, increasing the mass flux requiring treatment while simultaneously reducing the efficacy of technologies dependent on interfacial phenomena. The compounding effect intensifies the challenge—greater mobile mass combined with inferior removal efficiency creates a treatment demand that unenhanced systems cannot satisfy. Field-scale groundwater-to-surface water transport studies provide essential context for interpreting treatment limitations within broader hydrological systems. Moreover, quantification of PFAS mass transfer between groundwater and surface water using field data demonstrates that aquifer contamination represents a dynamic source term with continuous export potential. Treatment systems therefore operate against a background of ongoing source contribution rather than static plume conditions. Indeed, this reality underscores the inadequacy of partial removal: 50% short-chain efficiency leaves substantial mobile contamination capable of sustained propagation through aquifer systems and subsequent discharge to surface receptors. The technological gap thus translates directly to persistent environmental exposure risk that end-of-pipe treatment cannot fully mitigate. The regulatory classification basis for chain-length differentiation—bioaccumulation potential rather than groundwater mobility—reveals a fundamental mismatch between environmental protection frameworks and emerging contamination profiles. Moreover, regulatory pressure has driven industrial substitution toward short-chain formulations that evade bioaccumulation-based restrictions while exhibiting maximal subsurface transport potential. This substitution effect positions short-chain PFAS as the dominant groundwater quality concern of coming decades: compounds selected specifically for their environmental mobility encounter treatment technologies structurally incapable of efficient capture. Nevertheless, the temporal dimension proves critical—long-chain PFAS may dominate current contamination burdens, but short-chain compounds will persist longer in aquifer systems due to treatment bypass and sustained source release, ultimately representing the greater long-term management challenge. The environmental persistence of PFAS compounds fundamentally constrains remediation timelines and treatment system design. This intrinsic stability ensures that short-chain PFAS entering aquifer systems through treatment bypass or direct release will persist indefinitely, accumulating management liability across generational timescales. Consequently, the 50% or lower removal efficiency for unenhanced short-chain treatment translates not merely to partial capture but to permanent environmental loading when residual concentrations exceed regulatory thresholds. The national distribution of PFAS contamination underscores the systemic scale of this challenge. This pervasive presence indicates that aquifer systems nationwide function as receiving waters for continuous PFAS inputs, with short-chain compounds particularly favored for long-range transport due to their combination of persistence and mobility. Moreover, air-water interfacial dynamics in unsaturated zones further illuminate the source-pathway mechanisms governing treatment system loading. This differential interfacial partitioning means that long-chain PFAS retain greater soil-phase residence, reducing the mass flux delivered to groundwater and treatment systems. In contrast, short-chain PFAS exhibit diminished interfacial accumulation, accelerating their transfer to mobile porewater and increasing treatment demand precisely where technological efficacy is lowest. The regulatory framework's bioaccumulation basis for chain-length classification perpetuates this environmental mobility crisis. This criterion systematically favors short-chain substitution without regard for groundwater transport potential, creating a perverse outcome where regulatory compliance drives selection of compounds maximally suited to aquifer contamination.\n\n## References\n\n[1] Lingke Zeng, Steve Zemba (2022). \"Short or Long? outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_15\codex_stderr.log:4084:outputs\polaris_graph\PG_LB_FS_02_materials.json:285:      "content": "Solar Energy Materials & Solar Cells 264 (2024) 112602\nAvailable online 20 October 2023\n0927-0248/© 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/).\nEffect of global damp heat ageing on debonding of crosslinked EVA- and \nPOE-glass laminates \nMartin Tiefenthaler a,b,*, Gernot M. Wallner a,b, Robert Pugstaller a,b \na Christian Doppler Laboratory for Superimposed Mechanical-Environmental Ageing of Polymeric Hybrid Laminates, University of Linz, Austria \nb Institute of Polymeric Materials and Testing, University of Linz, Austria  \nA B S T R A C T   \nPolyolefin elastomers (POE) have been established as a promising alternative to commonly used peroxide crosslinked ethylene vinyl acetate copolymer (EVA) \nphotovoltaic encapsulation materials. The main objective of this work was to assess the long-term ageing behavior of double-glass laminates based on UV-transparent \nEVA and POE under damp heat (85 ◦C, 85 %rh) conditions for up to 10,000h. Ageing induced changes were characterized by microscopy, UV–visible–near infrared \n(UVVISNIR) spectroscopy, compressive shear testing (CST), differential scanning calorimetry (DSC), macro- and microscopic Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and X- \nray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Within the laminates, the formation of birefringent spheric microdomains (maltese-crosses) was confirmed after an exposure \ntime of more than 2,000h. The compressive shear behavior of EVA laminates was not significantly affected by damp heat ageing. Interestingly, the POE laminates \nrevealed an even improved compressive shear performance. Whereas the tested EVA laminates failed in an interfacial manner independent on damp heat ageing time, \nPOE laminates exhibited a failure mode transition from interfacial (unaged) to combined interfacial and cohesive (10,000h). On the interfacially fractured surfaces of \nEVA and POE laminates, carboxylic acid groups were ascertained. Deprotonated silanol and water bands were discernible on the opposite glass sides. Damp heat \nexposure of POE laminates for 10,000h resulted in pronounced glass ageing close to the interface associated with a partly cohesive failure within glass. This phe­\nnomenon was quite different to EVA and was attributed to the alkaline characteristics of the hindered amine light (HALS) stabilized, less-polar, UV-transparent POE \nencapsulant.   \n1. Introduction \nThe lifetime and power output of photovoltaic (PV) modules depend \nsignificantly on the polymeric encapsulation material [1–6]. Ethylene \nvinyl acetate copolymer (EVA) is still dominating the market. Field aged \nPV modules with EVA encapsulation show signs of delamination, yel­\nlowing and potential induced degradation (PID) [5–8]. Conventional \nglobal ageing and delamination characterization consisting of first, \nenvironmental exposure of laminates without external mechanical \nloads, and second, monotonic mechanical testing is rather time \nconsuming and of limited service-relevance. Thus, novel local ageing \napproaches based on fatigue testing of double-glass laminates under \nsuperimposed environmental influences and mechanical stresses are \nunder development [3,9,10]. Both, global and local ageing character­\nization methods have confirmed diffusion-controlled degradation \nmechanisms at the interface of the glass substrate and the encapsulation \nmaterial [3,11–13]. \nThe polarity of EVA facilitates water diffusion leading to degradation \nmechanisms such as deacetylation, β-scission and hydrolytic depoly­\nmerization [11,14–16]. Poor barrier properties of EVA against ion \nmigration from the front glass to the silicon cell are responsible for \npotential induced degradation of PV modules [17]. Therefore, current \nresearch and material development is focused on less-polar substitution \nmaterials based on polyolefin elastomers (POE) [4,18–20]. \nBarretta et al. [4] investigated the effect of damp heat (DH, 85 ◦C, 85 \n%rh) exposure on the performance of crosslinked EVA and POE films. No \nsignificant changes of optical transmittance as well as surface and bulk \nchemistry were detected after damp heat exposure for up to 3,000h. \nOreski et al. [18] examined the influence of DH on the degradation and \npower output of 6-cell PV modules with EVA or POE encapsulants. \nAcetic acid induced degradation of the cells encapsulated with EVA was \nconfirmed after 3,000h, whereas no significant changes were observed \nin POE-based modules. \nThe exposure of glass substrates to DH conditions triggers ageing \nmechanisms such as leaching of network cations (e.g., Na, Ca), hydro­\nlysis of the Si–O network, glass corrosion and carbonate deposition on \nthe surfaces [21]. Hence, global ageing of glass substrates and its \ninteraction with the encapsulant has also an impact on the lifetime of PV \nmodules [21–24]. Silane based coupling agents improve the adhesion \nand the resistance against DH induced degradation of EVA-glass \n* Corresponding author. Christian Doppler Laboratory for Superimposed Mechanical-Environmental Ageing of Polymeric Hybrid Laminates, University of Linz, \nAustria. \nE-mail address: martin.tiefenthaler@jku.at (M. Tiefenthaler).  \nContents lists available at ScienceDirect \nSolar Energy Materials and Solar Cells \njournal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/solmat \nhttps://doi.org/10.1016/j.solmat.2023.112602 \nReceived 10 May 2023; Received in revised form 26 September 2023; Accepted 16 October 2023   \n\n\nSolar Energy Materials and Solar Cells 264 (2024) 112602\n2\nlaminates \n[13,25]. \nThe \nlong-term \nbehavior \nof \ncovalent \nencapsulant-Si-O-Si-glass bonds is crucial for the long-term delamina­\ntion resistance of PV modules [13,25]. The EVA-glass interface is more \nprone to ageing induced delamination than the EVA-silicon cell interface \n[11]. In the early stage, the degradation mechanism of the EVA-glass \ninterface is characterized by deacetylation and β-scission reactions of \nEVA and later on by hydrolytic depolymerization [11]. Furthermore, \nsodium ion migration plays a major role associated with a significantly \nenhanced sodium content on the fractured interface [11]. \nIn a recent paper, it was clearly shown that the degradation mech­\nanisms within double-glass modules are highly dependent on the \nformulation of the encapsulation material [12]. A variety of commer­\ncially relevant and experimental encapsulation formulations has been \ninvestigated without clear differentiation and description of the stabi­\nlizer package used. Moreover, compressive shear testing subsequent to \nenvironmental exposure of double-glass laminates is gaining importance \nfor delamination characterization [26–30]. \nHence, the main objective of this paper was to evaluate and compare \nthe damp heat ageing behavior of double-glass laminates based on UV- \ntransparent EVA and POE encapsulants. The UV-transparency is ach­\nieved by adding just hindered amine light stabilizers (HALS) which gives \nthe encapsulation material more alkaline characteristics. Phenolic an­\ntioxidants, which are prone to the formation of acidic low molar mass \ndegradation products due to the ester-based linkage groups, were \navoided. Quadratic compressive shear double-glass specimens were \nlaminated and exposed for up to 10,000 h to damp heat. Subsequently \nand prior to mechanical testing, non-destructive microscopic and \nUVVISNIR spectroscopic analysis was carried out. Then, the mechanical \nbehavior was examined by compressive shear testing. Finally, the frac­\ntured surfaces and the remaining encapsulant were analyzed by differ­\nential scanning calorimetry (DSC), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) \nspectroscopy/microscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). \n2. Experimental \n2.1. Materials and laminates \nTwo peroxide crosslinking UV-transparent encapsulants based on \nethylene vinyl acetate copolymer (EVA) and polyolefin elastomer (POE) \nwere investigated. While the EVA film revealed a non-periodic topog­\nraphy, the POE had a periodic pyramidal topography. A non-thermally \ntoughened solar glass allowing for water jet cutting after lamination \nwas used in this study. By XPS, the following composition of the glass \nsurface was determined (in at%): 21.1 Si, 57.5 O, 11.1 C, 2.6 N, 5.7 Na, \n1.3 Ca and 0.7 K. Details on the chemical composition and the ther­\nmomechanical properties of the encapsulants are given in Ref. [19]. \nTwo EVA or POE films were used to prepare double-glass laminates \n(300 × 200 × 3.1 mm3) at 155 ◦C, 800 mbar for 20 min. Double-glass \nspecimens with dimensions of 25 × 25 × 7 mm3 were prepared by \nwater jet cutting. Specimens were damp heat exposed (85 ◦C, 85 %rh) in \nan environmental chamber WK3-340/40 (Weiss Umwelttechnik GmbH, \nGermany) for 0, 1,000, 2,000, 5,000 and 10,000h. Whereas 3 specimens \nwere examined for each ageing interval, only 1 specimen was available \nafter 10,000h of damp heat exposure. \n2.2. Characterization methods \nPrior to compressive shear testing, optical and structural changes \nwere assessed by direct and polarized light microscopy (DLM and PLM) \nusing a VHX 7000 (Keyence, Japan). While DLM measurements were \nperformed using full ring incident light, PLM measurements were car­\nried out in transmittance mode with the specimen placed between two \nfully crossed (0/90◦) polarizers. DLM images were taken from the \nspecimens immediately after removal from the damp heat chamber. \nThen, the glass surfaces of the specimen were polished using an auto­\nmated wet-polishing unit with fine sandpapers. Hemispheric and diffuse \ntransmittance spectra in the range from 250 to 2500 nm were recorded \nin normal transmittance mode in 5 nm steps on a Lamda 950 UVVISNIR \nspectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA) equipped with a 150 mm integrating \nsphere. The UV cut-off lengths were defined by a 10% drop in trans­\nmittance compared to the reference at 800 nm [31]. To deduce integral \nnormal-hemispheric and -diffuse transmittance values, the spectral data \nwere weighted with an AM1.5 source function [31,32]. The trans­\nmittance data were integrated in the solar (300–2000 nm) and UV \n(300–400 nm) range. Moreover, solar haze values were evaluated by \nnormalization of the diffuse with the hemispheric transmittance [33]. In \naddition to the laminates, the used glass substrate was characterized in \nthe unaged, reference state. \nShear failure is a common phenomenon related to interfacial ageing. \nWhile interlaminar shear testing methods are often applied for charac­\nterization of composites [34], in this study focus was given to \ncompressive shear testing as developed by Chapuis et al. [26] for double \nglass laminates. Compressive shear tests were carried out in tensile \nmode on an universal tensile testing machine Z020 (ZwickRoell, Ger­\nmany) equipped with a temperature chamber (see Fig. 1). The \ndisplacement rate was 1 mm/min. To prevent fracture of the glass \nsubstrate and to mimic critical service-relevant conditions, the test \ntemperature was set to 60 ◦C [27,29]. Shear loads detected by \ncompressive shear testing of double-glass laminates are at least 50 times \nhigher than service-relevant shear loads in a PV module [35]. Hence, the \nmain purpose of monotonic shear testing was to deduce ultimate me­\nchanical values allowing for quantitative comparison of the damp heat \nageing behavior of glass laminates based on the investigated \nUV-transparent EVA and POE encapsulants. Moreover, it should be \nevaluated if damp heat ageing for up to 10,000 h is associated with a \ncritical drop in ultimate shear loads below service-relevant shear \nloading conditions. \nAs illustrated in Fig. 2, the failure modes were classified as interfa­\ncial, cohesive in the encapsulant or glass and combined interfacial and \ncohesive. Remaining encapsulants on the fractured double-glass lami­\nnates were investigated by direct light microscopy (DLM). Moreover, the \ncorresponding glass sides were examined with a 3D laser confocal mi­\ncroscope LEXT OLS4000 (Olympus, Japan). Stitched 10x images of the \nentire glass surface (25 × 25 mm2) were generated. Details of the frac­\ntured surfaces and height profiles were deduced from 50x images. \nFurthermore, encapsulant samples were taken with a scalpel and \ncharacterized by differential scanning calorimetry (DSC 8500, Perki­\nnElmer, USA). Samples (< 5 mg) were placed in a perforated 3 μL \naluminum pan. Thermograms were recorded with a heating/cooling rate \nof 20 K/min under nitrogen atmosphere. The experiments consisted of a \nfirst heating step (0–200 ◦C) followed by a cooling step (200-0 ◦C) and a \nsubsequent second heating step (0–200 ◦C). During damp heat ageing, \nthe edges of the encapsulant were directly exposed to water vapor. In \nFig. 1. Compressive shear test (CST) setup: a) schematic illustration according \nto Ref. [26]; b) image of setup implemented at an universal mechanical testing \nsystem equipped with a temperature chamber. \nM. Tiefenthaler et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                           \n\n\nSolar Energy Materials and Solar Cells 264 (2024) 112602\n3\ncontrast, the ingress to the center of the laminate was diffusion \ncontrolled. To assess potential structural differences within the encap­\nsulant layer, DSC samples were taken from the edge and the center. \nFTIR spectroscopy was carried out in attenuated total reflection \n(ATR) mode using a Spectrum 100 (PerkinElmer, USA) equipped with a \ndiamond/ZnSe ATR unit. The spectra were obtained by averaging 4 \nscans ranging from 650 to 4000 cm−1 with a spectral resolution of 4 \ncm−1. For each encapsulant and exposure time, global-ATR spectra were \ncollected from the remaining encapsulant layer. Moreover, spatially \nresolved, local FTIR-ATR microscopic images with dimensions of 300 ×\n300 μm2 were recorded using a Spectrum 100 and a Spotlight 400 im­\naging system (PerkinElmer, USA) equipped with a Ge-crystal. Spectra \nwere run in the range from 720 to 4000 cm−1 with a spatial resolution of \n1.56 μm and a spectral resolution of 16 cm−1. The IR-ATR spectra were \nbaseline corrected. \nX-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) measurements were per­\nformed using a Theta Probe device and an Avantage data system \n(Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The chemical composition of fractured \nsurfaces was deduced from XPS survey scans with an Al-Kα mono­\nchromatic X-ray source (1486.6 eV) and a spot size diameter of 300 or \n400 μm. The pass energy of the hemispherical analyzer was set to 50 eV. \nFurther details as to XPS analysis are given in Ref. [36]. Special focus \nwas given to the analysis and changes of the sodium and calcium con­\ntents within the bulk encapsulant and at surfaces and interfaces of EVA \nand POE based double-glass laminates. To improve the comparability \nand decrease the effect of contaminations, measured sodium and cal­\ncium fractions were normalized by the corresponding silicon content. \nFTIR and XPS are differing significantly in their depth resolution. \nWhile a sample layer with a thickness of about 5 μm is assessed by FTIR \nin ATR-mode, XPS is a highly surface-sensitive characterization method \nwith a penetration depth of about 10 nm [37]. Hence, FTIR-ATR and XPS \nmeasurements were carried out to differentiate between ageing phe­\nnomena close to the fractured surface (by XPS) and within the usually \nplastically deformed damage zone within a few μm-distance to fractured \nsurface (by FTIR-ATR). \n3. Results and discussion \n3.1. Visual appearance and optical properties \nA silicate glass network contains bridging oxygens (BO, i.e., Si–O–Si) \nand non-bridging oxygens (NBO, e.g., Si–O-). The latter are charge \ncompensated by glass network modifier cations (e.g., Na, Ca). As well \ndiscussed in literature [21,22,24], DH exposure of silicate glass leads to \nthe formation of a surface water film and to diffusion of molecular water \ninto the bulk. The diffused water reacts with both, BO and NBO, asso­\nciated with 1) the formation of silanol groups, 2) the release of (glass) \nnetwork modifier cations, and 3) the formation of more NBO (Si–O-) \ngroups [21,22,24]. These three mechanisms are weakening the glass \nnetwork [21,24]. The network modifier cations migrate to the water \nfilm and cause a raise of the pH value by changing the electrochemical \npotential between the glass surface and the bulk. At a pH value of 9, the \nmigrated ions form water-insoluble carbonate (e.g., Na2CO3, CaCO3) \nclusters associated with permanent whitening of the surface [21]. In \nFig. 3, DLM images of non-surface polished EVA and POE laminates are \nillustrated after 0, 5,000 and 10,000h of DH exposure. Leaching induced \nsurface whitening was clearly discernible after 5,000h. The entire glass \nsurfaces of both laminates revealed a homogenous white appearance \nafter 10,000h. The surface whitening effect was not dependent on the \nencapsulant material (as shown in Fig. 3). \nMicroscopic images of surface polished EVA and POE laminates ob­\ntained by DLM (1st row) and PLM (2nd and 3rd row) after 0, 5,000, and \n10,000h of DH exposure are depicted and compared in Fig. 4. DLM \nimages revealed an increased whitening for both encapsulant types after \nFig. 2. Failure modes of compressive shear tested double-glass laminates: 1) interfacial failure at the encapsulant-glass interface, 2) cohesive failure: a) within the \nencapsulant, b) within the glass substrate. \nFig. 3. DLM images of non-surface polished EVA and POE-glass laminates \nexposed to DH for 0, 5,000 and 10,000h. \nM. Tiefenthaler et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                           \n\n\nSolar Energy Materials and Solar Cells 264 (2024) 112602\n4\n5,000h of DH exposure. The edge clearing effect was more pronounced \nfor EVA laminates. In contrast, the edges of the POE laminates appeared \nfully opaque after 10,000h (see Fig. 4). After 5,000h of DH exposure, \nspheric microdomains (maltese-crosses, d < 100 μm) were clearly \ndiscernible in the polarized light microscopic (PLM) images of both \nlaminate types (see Fig. 4). The microdomains differed in appearance, \nsize and number. Compared to POE, EVA laminates exhibited less, but \nlarger microdomains, which were presumably related to water uptake \nand clustering. The edge clearing effect of EVA laminates was dependent \non conditioning time at 23 ◦C and 50 %rh, subsequent to DH exposure. \nThe microdomains within the POE laminates were smaller and more \nhomogenous (see Fig. 4). Harris et al. [38] reported the formation of \ncalcite (CaCO3) crystals and spherolithic superstructures resulting in \nmaltese-crosses in PLM images. Most likely, the finer microdomains in \nPOE laminates could be attributed to glass ageing induced formation of \nCaCO3 and Na2CO3 crystals at the POE-glass interface [21]. Interest­\ningly, these microdomains were nucleated along the edge lines of the \npyramidal topography of the extruded POE film. In addition to the \nmaltese-cross microdomains, distinct black spots with a diameter up to \n2 mm were also discernible in POE laminates after 5,000 and 10,000h of \nDH exposure. \nIn contrast to POE, which was reported to prevent effectively ion \nmigration [17], the higher polarity of EVA favors ion diffusion into the \nbulk of the encapsulant [3,11,21]. Moreover, the damp heat induced \nhydrolysis of EVA could facilitate the formation of acetic acid, which \nwould reduce the alkalinity of the pH at the EVA-glass interface. These \ntwo mechanisms were ascribed to hinder the formation of sodium or \ncalcium carbonates at EVA-glass interfaces by keeping the pH value well \nbelow 9. \nSolar transmittance spectra of a single glass substrate and the lami­\nnates are depicted in Fig. 5. The hemispheric transmittance spectrum of \nthe glass substrate revealed an UV cut-off at 330 nm. The UV cut-off of \nEVA laminates shifted from 345 to 385 nm after 10,000h of damp heat \nexposure. A cut-off shift from 355 to 400 nm was also confirmed for POE \nlaminates. The results of the double-glass EVA laminates are in agree­\nment with findings in Refs. [35,39]. Morse et al. [39] did not detect any \nsignificant changes of the UV cut-off for POE laminates exposed to 1.5 \nMJ/m2 of solar radiation at 85 ◦C for 4,000h. The deviations within this \nwork and the findings in Ref. [39] were attributed to different ageing \nconditions. Barret et al. [4] investigated the effect of damp heat expo­\nsure on free standing crosslinked POE films and reported a decrease of \nthe hemispheric transmittance with increasing ageing durations, which \nis also in agreement with findings of this study (see Figs. 5 and 6b)). The \nlower hemispheric transmittance values of POE-based glass laminates in \nthe unaged reference state is presumably related to a stronger index of \nrefraction difference of glass and POE associated with higher reflectivity \nat the interfaces. The diffuse transmittance was significantly higher for \nthe unaged POE laminate. More pronounced scattering of POE is pre­\nsumably related to a higher degree of crystallinity and a lower cross­\nlinking density [19]. The increase in scattering at lower wavelength \nvalues is a clear indicator for scattering domains such as crystal lamellae \nwith a thickness well below 300 nm. While the lamellae thickness of PE \ncrystals is below 10 nm, the lateral dimensions of crystal lamellae are \nseveral 100 nm and hence, contributing to scattering [31]. Both lami­\nnates revealed hydrocarbon (CH) related absorption bands at 2500 to \n2200, 1800 to 1650, 1400 and 1200 nm and a small carbonyl (C=O) \nFig. 4. outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_15\codex_stderr.log:4089:outputs\polaris_graph\PG_LB_FS_02_materials.json:6418:      "full_text": "Title: Damage-Associated Molecular Patterns and Their Signaling Pathways in Primary Blast Lung Injury: New Research Progress and Future Directions\n\nURL Source: https://www.mdpi.com/1422-0067/21/17/6303/pdf?version=1598869065\n\nPublished Time: Mon, 31 Aug 2020 10:17:45 GMT\n\nNumber of Pages: 20\n\nMarkdown Content:\nInternational Journal of \n\n## Molecular Sciences Review \n\n# Damage-Associated Molecular Patterns and Their Signaling Pathways in Primary Blast Lung Injury: New Research Progress and Future Directions \n\nNing Li 1,2, †, Chenhao Geng 1,2, †, Shike Hou 1,2 , Haojun Fan 1,2, * and Yanhua Gong 1,2, * \n\n> 1\n\nInstitute of Disaster Medicine, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, China; lining620@tju.edu.cn (N.L.); gengchenhao@tju.edu.cn (C.G.); houshike@tju.edu.cn (S.H.)  \n\n> 2\n\nTianjin Key Laboratory of Disaster Medicine Technology, Tianjin 300072, China \n\n* Correspondence: haojunfan86@126.com (H.F.); gongyanhua@tju.edu.cn (Y.G.) \n\n† Ning Li and Chenhao Geng contributed equally to this work. Received: 14 July 2020; Accepted: 20 August 2020; Published: 31 August 2020 \u0001\u0002\u0003\u0001\u0004\u0005\u0006\u0007\b \u0001\n\n> \u0001\u0002\u0003\u0004\u0005\u0006\u0007\n\nAbstract: Primary blast lung injury (PBLI) is a common cause of casualties in wars, terrorist attacks, and explosions. It can exist in the absence of any other outward signs of trauma, and further develop into acute lung injury (ALI) or a more severe acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS). The pathogenesis of PBLI at the cellular and molecular level has not been clear. Damage-associated molecular pattern (DAMP) is a general term for endogenous danger signals released by the body after injury, including intracellular protein molecules (HMGB1, histones, s100s, heat shock proteins, eCIRP, etc.), secretory protein factors (IL-1 β, IL-6, IL-10, TNF-α, VEGF, complements, etc.), purines and pyrimidines and their derived degradation products (nucleic acids, ATP, ADP, UDPG, uric acid, etc.), and extracellular matrix components (hyaluronic acid, fibronectin, heparin sulfate, biglycan, etc.). DAMPs can be detected by multiple receptors including pattern recognition receptors (PRRs). The study of DAMPs and their related signaling pathways, such as the mtDNA-triggered cGAS-YAP pathway, contributes to revealing the molecular mechanism of PBLI, and provides new therapeutic targets for controlling inflammatory diseases and alleviating their symptoms. In this review, we focus on the recent progress of research on DAMPs and their signaling pathways, as well as the potential therapeutic targets and future research directions in PBLI. \n\nKeywords: primary blast lung injury; ALI; DAMPs; PRRs; signaling pathways \n\n1. Introduction \n\nExplosions often occur in modern wars, terrorist attacks, or daily emergencies, and blast injury is a common cause of casualties [ 1, 2]. At the moment of explosion, the explosive materials almost instantaneously convert from solid or liquid into gas. Gas expands rapidly from the explosion point and displaces the surrounding medium to create a blast overpressure (BOP), which transfers a large amount of energy to the surrounding medium to form the blast pressure wave [ 3]. Generally speaking, blast injuries can be divided into 4 categories, including primary (injury from direct e ff ect of blast pressure wave), secondary (fragmentation injury), tertiary (injury caused by bodily displacement or building collapse) and quaternary (includes burn injury and inhalation of toxic substances) [ 3–7]. Furthermore, some research proposed quinary injuries, but the specific content has not reached a consensus [ 3 ,5 , 6, 8]. In these types of injuries, primary blast injury (PBI) is a potentially life threatening, multi-system disease [ 4 ,8]. The previous common view was that blast waves mainly exert force at the air-tissue interfaces within the body. Therefore, organ systems with high air content, such as the auditory systems, pulmonary and gastrointestinal, are most likely a ff ected by PBI [ 3]. Among them,    \n\n> Int. J. Mol. Sci. 2020 ,21 , 6303; doi:10.3390 /ijms21176303 www.mdpi.com /journal /ijms\n\nInt. J. Mol. Sci. 2020 , 21 , 6303 2 of 20 \n\nlungs may be particularly susceptible to blast waves given tissue-density gradients [ 9]. Although recent studies indicated that blast-induced traumatic brain injury (bTBI) may be more common than previously believed [ 4 ,10 ], primary blast lung injury (PBLI) is still worthy of attention. The blast wave-induced acute lung injuries (ALI) are generally closed wounds rather than traditional penetrating wounds, which can be sustained in the absence of any other external signs of thoracic trauma [ 6]. However, it may further develop into acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), a more severe physiological expression of ALI [ 11 ]. Although the current research on the pathophysiological characteristics of PBLI has been relatively clear, the mechanisms at the cellular and molecular levels are still not fully understood. Damage-associated molecular pattern (DAMP) was first proposed by Land in 2003 [ 12 ]. It is the general term for endogenous risk signals released by the organism after injury, which can trigger non-infectious and uncontrolled inflammation in the body [ 13 , 14 ], aggravating the lung tissue damage after an explosion [ 15 ]. This review will focus on DAMPs and their signaling pathways related to PBLI, and explore possible therapeutic targets in future clinical treatment. \n\n2. Overview of Primary Blast Lung Injury \n\n2.1. The Mechanism of Primary Blast Lung Injury \n\nPBLI is mainly caused by the blast wave directly passing through the chest wall, which dissipates kinetic energy in the lung tissue and causes ALI [ 5 ]. In 1950, Schardin described three types of explosive forces that can cause injury: spallation, implosion, and inertia [ 16 ]. The model of normal alveolar capillaries is shown in Figure 1a. Spallation is defined as the displacement and fragmentation of the dense medium into a less dense medium (Figure 1b). Implosion is caused by the sharply reduced alveolar volume due to the high compression of air in the alveoli by overpressure wave, and then the alveoli re-expand rapidly by underpressure wave (Figure 1c). Inertia refers to the shear force produced by shock waves propagating at di ff erent speeds through di ff erent density tissues [ 3, 17 ,18 ]. These three forces work together to cause pulmonary hemorrhage and contusion, and once the air in the alveoli enters the pulmonary circulation, it may cause air embolism, resulting in a series of symptoms such as pulmonary edema, pneumothorax, and interstitial emphysema [ 19 ,20 ]. The description of these three injury mechanisms is based on the blast wave generated by open air explosions. The extent to which these forces actually cause damage is not completely clear [ 3 ]. Furthermore, research shows that the reflection of blast waves in confined spaces will significantly enhance their e ff ects and further aggravate the injury [18]. Int. J. Mol. Sci. 20 20 , 21, x FOR PEER REVIEW 3 of 23                                  \n\n> Figure 1. Damage mechanism of blast lung injury .(a) Normal alveolar capillaries .(b)\n> Schematic diagram of spallation . Alveolar hemorrhage causes the release of cytokines and\n> chemokines .(c) Schematic diagram of implosion . Air in the alveoli enters the pulmonary\n> circulation, causing air embolism.\n> 2.2. Pathophysiological Characteristics and Clinical Diagnosis of Primary Blast Lung Injury\n> The blast wave causes the rupture of alveolar capillaries, resulting in the presence of free\n> hemoglobin (Hb) and extrav asated blood in the lung tissue, induction of free radical reactions\n> that cause oxidative damage, initiation and augmentation of a pro -inflammatory response\n> [5,21] . Furthermore, leucocytes can be demonstrated at the hemorrhagic areas within 3 h after\n> blast exposure, and their levels increase at 24 h or more [22] . Histological examination reveal s\n> obvious perivascular edema and massive alveolar hemorrhage within the first 12 h, followed\n> by damage to epithelial cells (at 12 –24 h) and endothelial cells (at 24 –56 h)[23] .\n\nFigure 1. Damage mechanism of blast lung injury. ( a) Normal alveolar capillaries. ( b) Schematic diagram of spallation. Alveolar hemorrhage causes the release of cytokines and chemokines. ( c) Schematic diagram of implosion. Air in the alveoli enters the pulmonary circulation, causing air embolism. Int. J. Mol. Sci. 2020 , 21 , 6303 3 of 20 \n\n2.2. Pathophysiological Characteristics and Clinical Diagnosis of Primary Blast Lung Injury \n\nThe blast wave causes the rupture of alveolar capillaries, resulting in the presence of free hemoglobin (Hb) and extravasated blood in the lung tissue, induction of free radical reactions that cause oxidative damage, initiation and augmentation of a pro-inflammatory response [ 5, 21 ]. Furthermore, leucocytes can be demonstrated at the hemorrhagic areas within 3 h after blast exposure, and their levels increase at 24 h or more [ 22 ]. Histological examination reveals obvious perivascular edema and massive alveolar hemorrhage within the first 12h, followed by damage to epithelial cells (at 12–24 h) and endothelial cells (at 24–56 h) [23]. Clinically, patients with PBLI often have dyspnea, shallow and frequent breathing [ 24 ]. In chest imaging, PBLI appears as ground-glass opacity (GGO), consolidation, and bilateral flu ff y infiltrates that resemble a “butterfly” or “bat wing” [ 4 ,25 ]. In addition, alveolar hemorrhage, laceration of lung parenchyma, subcutaneous emphysema, pneumothorax, and hemothorax can also be observed [ 25 ,26 ]. \n\n2.3. The Medical Treatment of Primary Blast Lung Injury \n\nAfter an explosion incident, the emergency personnel should first identify whether there is the risk of PBLI according to the place where the explosion occurred and the distance between the wounded and the center of explosion [ 5 ]. It is imperative to perform pre-hospital first aid, and notify the relevant department sta ff in advance to make relevant preparations, so as to ensure the smooth progress of follow-up treatment [ 4]. The current in-hospital treatment strategies for PBLI mainly include fluid resuscitation, hyperbaric oxygen therapy, mechanical ventilation, etc. [ 27 –29 ], and can be combined with drugs that inhibit inflammatory factors, scavenge oxygen free radicals, relieve smooth muscle spasm, and improve lung ventilation [ 30 ]. These interventions aim to control the development of the disease and maintain the vital signs of patients. In addition, the application of new biotechnology such as mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) in the treatment of PBLI has become a research hotspot in recent years [ 31 ,32 ]. However, due to insu ffi cient research on the molecular mechanism of PBLI, there is no specific medicine for the treatment of PBLI. Therefore, the study of DAMPs in PBLI is of great significance for exploring potential therapeutic targets and developing targeted treatment methods. \n\n3. DAMPs and Their Sensing Receptors \n\n3.1. Damage-Associated Molecular Pattern \n\nThe immune system defends our body with the mechanism of “self versus non-self” recognition. However, it can be activated not only by exogenous “non-self” substances, but also by endogenous DAMPs [ 33 ]. DAMPs are numerous intracellular molecules with physiological functions, which exist in the nucleus, mitochondria, or cytoplasm [ 34 –36 ]. Under normal physiological conditions, DAMPs cannot be recognized by the immune system, but they are released when cells die or are subjected to stress [ 37 ,38 ]. When released into the extracellular environment, DAMPs can activate innate immune cells, such as polymorphonuclear neutrophils (PMNs), natural killer cells (NKs), macrophages, and dendritic cells (DCs), as well as non-immune cells, such as epithelial cells, endothelial cells, and fibroblasts [ 39 ]. The activation of these cells results in the release of various cytokines and chemokines, which further recruit inflammatory cells and activate adaptive immune responses. In addition, some DAMPs can directly activate adaptive immune cells to generate immune responses. Whereas the local inflammatory response plays an important role in tissue repair and regeneration, excessive or persistent inflammation may also lead to a systemic and uncontrolled inflammatory response inducing remote organ failure [ 40 ]. \n\n3.2. DAMP-Sensing Receptors \n\nDAMPs released by injured organs, tissues, or cells can be detected by multiple receptors including pattern recognition receptors (PRRs) (Figure 2), which can, in turn, activate multiple pathways to regulate the inflammatory response. Moreover, one DAMP may bind to a variety of receptors and participate in di ff erent pathways [40]. Int. J. Mol. Sci. 2020 , 21 , 6303 4 of 20 Int. J. Mol. Sci. 20 20 , 21, x FOR PEER REVIEW 5 of 23           \n\n> Figure 2. Relationship between DAMPs and their sensing receptors and the activation of\n> different types of cells. DAMPs are released from cells which are dead or stressed due to tissue\n> injury, and include intracellular protein molecules, secreted protein factors, pur ine and\n> pyrimidine molecules, and their derived degradation products (DDPs) and extracellular\n> matrix components. They activate downstream signaling pathways by recognizing their\n> sensing receptors to activate innate immune cells or non -immune cells, then ad aptive immune\n> cells, and also directly activate adaptive immune cells to initiate inflammatory response.\n> Different cellular populations communicate with each other through cytokines and\n> chemokines.\n\n3.2.1. Pattern Recognition Receptors Bound by Damage -Assoc iated Molecular Patterns \n\nPRRs are expressed by innate immune cells such as PMNs, NKs, macrophages , and DCs, \n\nand mainly include toll -like receptors (TLRs), NOD -like receptors (NLRs), retinoic acid -\n\ninducible gene -I (RIG -I) -like receptors (RLRs), C -type lectin receptors (CLRs) , and multiple \n\ncytoplasmic DNA sensors (CDSs) [41] . TLRs recognize DAMP molecules and participate in \n\ninflammatory response [42] . NLRs promote the activation of inflammatory bodies [43] . RLRs \n\nare one of the main receptor families for sensing viral RNA [44] . The main function of CLRs is \n\nto induce pro -inflammatory response [45] . CDSs play essential roles in the development of \n\ninflammatory diseases and tumors [46] .\n\n1. Toll -like receptors \n\nTLRs are located on the cell surface or inside the cell, and can recognize DAMPs such as \n\nnucleic acids, HMGB1, heat shock proteins (HSPs) , and S100 proteins. Most TLRs (excep t TLR3) \n\nare recruited by myeloid differentiation protein 88 (MyD88) -dependent IL -1 receptor -\n\nassociated kinase (IRAK), which then activates nuclear factor -κB (NF -κB) to promote   \n\n> Figure 2. Relationship between DAMPs and their sensing receptors and the activation of di ff erent types of cells. DAMPs are released from cells which are dead or stressed due to tissue injury, and include intracellular protein molecules, secreted protein factors, purine and pyrimidine molecules, and their derived degradation products (DDPs) and extracellular matrix components. They activate downstream signaling pathways by recognizing their sensing receptors to activate innate immune cells or non-immune cells, then adaptive immune cells, and also directly activate adaptive immune cells to initiate inflammatory response. Di ff erent cellular populations communicate with each other through cytokines and chemokines.\n\n3.2.1. Pattern Recognition Receptors Bound by Damage-Associated Molecular Patterns PRRs are expressed by innate immune cells such as PMNs, NKs, macrophages, and DCs, and mainly include toll-like receptors (TLRs), NOD-like receptors (NLRs), retinoic acid-inducible gene-I (RIG-I)-like receptors (RLRs), C-type lectin receptors (CLRs), and multiple cytoplasmic DNA sensors (CDSs) [ 41 ]. TLRs recognize DAMP molecules and participate in inflammatory response [ 42 ]. NLRs promote the activation of inflammatory bodies [ 43 ]. RLRs are one of the main receptor families for sensing viral RNA [ 44 ]. The main function of CLRs is to induce pro-inflammatory response [ 45 ]. CDSs play essential roles in the development of inflammatory diseases and tumors [46]. 1. Toll-like receptors TLRs are located on the cell surface or inside the cell, and can recognize DAMPs such as nucleic acids, HMGB1, heat shock proteins (HSPs), and S100 proteins. Most TLRs (except TLR3) are recruited by myeloid di ff erentiation protein 88 (MyD88) -dependent IL-1 receptor-associated kinase (IRAK), which then activates nuclear factor-κB (NF-κB) to promote inflammatory response by upregulating the Int. J. Mol. Sci. 2020 , 21 , 6303 5 of 20 \n\nexpression of tumor necrosis factor-α (TNF-α), interleukin 6 (IL-6), and IL-8 [ 40 ,47 ]. TLR3 participates in the inflammatory response through TRIF dependent pathway [42]. 2. NOD-like receptors NLRs are PRRs located in the cytoplasm, which can recruit pro-caspase-1 to start the assembly of inflammatory bodies after being activated by DAMPs such as ATP and oxidized mitochondrial DNA (ox-mtDNA), thereby inducing apoptosis and secretion of IL-1 β, IL-18 and other cytokines through the activation of caspase-1 [43,48]. 3. Retinoic acid-inducible gene-I (RIG-I)-like receptors The RLR family includes RIG-I, MDA5, and LGP2, which can distinguish between exogenous RNA and endogenous RNA to resist pathogens [ 44 ]. It can sense DAMPs such as endogenous 5 ′ppp RNA and unedited long self-dsRNA in vivo , and promote the production of type I interferon (IFN-I) and other cytokines and chemokines [49,50]. 4. C-type lectin receptors Some members of the CLR family, such as macrophage-inducible C-type lectin (MINCLE) and dendritic cell natural killer lectin group receptor 1 (DNGR1), are related to the development of inflammation diseases after binding to DAMPs. DNGR1 is a DC receptor which can be activated by F-actin to downregulate the production of the anti-inflammatory cytokine IL-10 in DCs and aggravate the inflammatory response [ 51 ]. MINCLE binds to SAP130 protein to induce pro-inflammatory response [45]. 5. Cytoplasmic DNA sensors Cyclic GMP–AMP synthase (cGAS) and absent in melanoma 2 (AIM2) are two important DNA sensors in the cytoplasm, which play essential roles in the development of inflammatory diseases and tumors [ 52 ,53 ]. Some studies have shown that self-DNA, produced by immunogenic cell death or nuclear DNA damage, can activate AIM2 or cGAS and promote the production of IFN-I and other cytokines and chemokines [46]. 3.2.2. Other Receptors /Channels Bound by DAMPs In addition to PRRs, DAMPs can activate receptors including triggering receptors expressed on myeloid cells (TREMs), receptor for advanced glycation end products (RAGE), several G-protein -coupled receptors (GPCRs), and ion channels [ 41 ]. TREM activation causes cytokine secretion, leading to inflammation response. The interaction between 35 kDa transmembrane receptor RAGE and its ligands also leads to the activation of pro-inflammatory genes [ 54 ,55 ]. GPCRs are known as seven-(pass)-transmembrane receptors and involved in many diseases via the cAMP or the phosphatidylinositol signal pathway [ 56 ]. Ion channels trigger the production of NLRP3 inflammasome and participate in the inflammatory response [57]. 1. Triggering receptors expressed on myeloid cells TREM1 and TREM2 are cell surface innate immune receptors which act as members of the immunoglobulin variable (IgV) domain receptor superfamily [ 58 ]. TREM1 is expressed on myeloid cells, epithelial cells, endothelial cells, and fibroblasts, and interacts with DAMPs such as HMGB1, HSP70, and actin to promote the secretion of pro-inflammatory cytokines and chemokines [ 59 , 60 ]. TREM2 is highly expressed in myeloid cells such as DCs, monocytes, and macrophages, and binds to DAMPs such as HSP60, apolipoproteins (APOJ and APOE), and low-density lipoprotein (LDL) to regulate cell di ff erentiation, phagocytosis, chemotaxis, and other processes, and participate in inflammatory diseases [61,62]. Int. J. Mol. Sci. 2020 , 21 , 6303 6 of 20 \n\n2. Receptor for advanced glycation end products RAGE is a transmembrane protein that belongs to the immunoglobulin receptor superfamily and is expressed by multiple cell types, including neutrophils, monocytes, smooth muscle cells, endothelial cells, and cancer cells, and mediates cell response to DAMPs such as HMGB1, S100 proteins, advanced glycation end products (AGEs), and DNA to promote the expression of pro-inflammatory genes, as well as cell migration, proliferation, and apoptosis [63]. 3. G-protein-coupled receptors Various DAMPs can also promote inflammation via GPCRs. The N-formyl peptide receptors (FPRs) can recognize endogenous N-formylated peptides and promote the chemotaxis of neutrophils and monocytes /macrophages [ 64 ]. P2Y receptors (P2YRs) can sense extracellular nucleotides and promote the migration and activation of multifarious immune cells [ 65 ]. Calcium-sensing receptors (CaSRs) and G-protein-coupled receptor family C group 6 member A (GPRC6A) can recognize extracellular Ca 2+,promote monocyte /macrophage recruitment and the activation of the NLRP3 inflammasome [66]. 4. Ion channels Transient receptor potential (TRP) channels and P2X receptors are two types of ligand-gated ion channels, which can promote the production of cytokines and chemokines and the activation of NLRP3 inflammasome by recognizing mitochondria-derived reactive oxygen species (ROS) and ATP, respectively, and participate in the inflammatory response [57,67]. \n\n3.3. Crosstalk Between DAMP and Their Sensing Receptors \n\nIn summary, DAMPs can bind to related receptors or channel proteins and activate downstream signaling pathways, leading to large-scale cytokine release, including IL-1, IL-6, IL-8, IL-12, TNF-α, and type I /\n\ntype II IFN. These mediators enhance the activation, maturation, proliferation, and recruitment of immune cells at the injured regions, leading to the indirect activation of innate and adaptive immune cells (such as DCs or T cells) (Figure 2). \n\n4. DAMPs and Their Signaling Pathways Related to PBLI \n\n4.1. Intracellular Protein Molecules \n\n4.1.1. Nuclear Protein HMGB1 HMGB1 is a late inflammatory mediator associated with sepsis, malignancy, and immune disease, and is one of the most intensively studied DAMPs. Levels of HMGB1 may reflect the severity of inflammation and tissue damage, indicating a potential role for HMGB1 as a biomarker of ALI, and a potential target for blocking inflammatory pathways [ 68 ]. Under normal physiological conditions, HMGB1 is located in the nucleus, but it is passively released from necrotic cells after PBLI. HMGB1 triggers signaling cascades by combining with RAGE, TLR2, and TLR4, thereby inducing the activation of the NF-κB pathway and participating in the inflammatory response [ 14 ](Figure 3). Li et al. [ 69 ] demonstrated that HMGB1 participates in ALI by activating double-stranded RNA-dependent protein kinase (PKR) in macrophages and inducing macrophage (M1) polarization via TLR2- and TLR4-mediated NF-κB signaling pathways in a mouse model of ALI induced by the bacterial endotoxin lipopolysaccharide (LPS). In addition, HMGB1 levels are also related to processes such as hyperfibrinolysis and complement activation and participate in systemic inflammatory responses [ 70 ]. Int. J. Mol. Sci. 2020 , 21 , 6303 7 of 20 Int. J. Mol. Sci. 20 20 , 21, x FOR PEER REVIEW 8 of 23                     \n\n> Figure 3. Potential DAMPs and related signaling pathways in BLI. Lung injury -related DAMPs\n> can activate PRR (TLR2/3/4/7/9, NLRP3) and non -PRR (P2X7R, RAGE, TERM1, VEGFR2) to\n> activate the corresponding signaling pathways, induce the release of cytokines and\n> chemokine s, and further stimulate effector cells to produce an immune response.
