Histones \n\nHistones are nucleoproteins involved in t he compaction of DNA into nucleosomes. They \n\nprovide structural stability to chromatin and regulate gene expression within the nucleus .\n\nHowever, once released from the nucleus into the cytoplasm or body fluids, histones can act as \n\na DAMP , activating the imm une system and causing further cytotoxicity. They interact with \n\nTLRs, complement and cell membrane phospholipids inducing endothelial and epithelial \n\ncytotoxicity, TLR2/TLR4/TLR9 activation and pro -inflammatory cytokine/chemokine release \n\nvia MyD88, NF -κB an d NLRP3 inflammasome -dependent pathways [71] (Figure 3). Abrams et \n\nal. [72] correlated circulating histone levels in patients with severe trauma with respiratory \n\nfailure and sequential organ failure assessment (SOFA) scores , and finally investigated their \n\ncause -effect relationship using cellular and mouse models. The results showed that circulating \n\nhistones surged immediately a"
outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_15\codex_stderr.log:4106:outputs\polaris_graph\baseline_v2_materials\PG_LB_FS_02_materials.json:285:      "content": "Solar Energy Materials & Solar Cells 264 (2024) 112602\nAvailable online 20 October 2023\n0927-0248/© 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/).\nEffect of global damp heat ageing on debonding of crosslinked EVA- and \nPOE-glass laminates \nMartin Tiefenthaler a,b,*, Gernot M. Wallner a,b, Robert Pugstaller a,b \na Christian Doppler Laboratory for Superimposed Mechanical-Environmental Ageing of Polymeric Hybrid Laminates, University of Linz, Austria \nb Institute of Polymeric Materials and Testing, University of Linz, Austria  \nA B S T R A C T   \nPolyolefin elastomers (POE) have been established as a promising alternative to commonly used peroxide crosslinked ethylene vinyl acetate copolymer (EVA) \nphotovoltaic encapsulation materials. The main objective of this work was to assess the long-term ageing behavior of double-glass laminates based on UV-transparent \nEVA and POE under damp heat (85 ◦C, 85 %rh) conditions for up to 10,000h. Ageing induced changes were characterized by microscopy, UV–visible–near infrared \n(UVVISNIR) spectroscopy, compressive shear testing (CST), differential scanning calorimetry (DSC), macro- and microscopic Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and X- \nray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Within the laminates, the formation of birefringent spheric microdomains (maltese-crosses) was confirmed after an exposure \ntime of more than 2,000h. The compressive shear behavior of EVA laminates was not significantly affected by damp heat ageing. Interestingly, the POE laminates \nrevealed an even improved compressive shear performance. Whereas the tested EVA laminates failed in an interfacial manner independent on damp heat ageing time, \nPOE laminates exhibited a failure mode transition from interfacial (unaged) to combined interfacial and cohesive (10,000h). On the interfacially fractured surfaces of \nEVA and POE laminates, carboxylic acid groups were ascertained. Deprotonated silanol and water bands were discernible on the opposite glass sides. Damp heat \nexposure of POE laminates for 10,000h resulted in pronounced glass ageing close to the interface associated with a partly cohesive failure within glass. This phe­\nnomenon was quite different to EVA and was attributed to the alkaline characteristics of the hindered amine light (HALS) stabilized, less-polar, UV-transparent POE \nencapsulant.   \n1. Introduction \nThe lifetime and power output of photovoltaic (PV) modules depend \nsignificantly on the polymeric encapsulation material [1–6]. Ethylene \nvinyl acetate copolymer (EVA) is still dominating the market. Field aged \nPV modules with EVA encapsulation show signs of delamination, yel­\nlowing and potential induced degradation (PID) [5–8]. Conventional \nglobal ageing and delamination characterization consisting of first, \nenvironmental exposure of laminates without external mechanical \nloads, and second, monotonic mechanical testing is rather time \nconsuming and of limited service-relevance. Thus, novel local ageing \napproaches based on fatigue testing of double-glass laminates under \nsuperimposed environmental influences and mechanical stresses are \nunder development [3,9,10]. Both, global and local ageing character­\nization methods have confirmed diffusion-controlled degradation \nmechanisms at the interface of the glass substrate and the encapsulation \nmaterial [3,11–13]. \nThe polarity of EVA facilitates water diffusion leading to degradation \nmechanisms such as deacetylation, β-scission and hydrolytic depoly­\nmerization [11,14–16]. Poor barrier properties of EVA against ion \nmigration from the front glass to the silicon cell are responsible for \npotential induced degradation of PV modules [17]. Therefore, current \nresearch and material development is focused on less-polar substitution \nmaterials based on polyolefin elastomers (POE) [4,18–20]. \nBarretta et al. [4] investigated the effect of damp heat (DH, 85 ◦C, 85 \n%rh) exposure on the performance of crosslinked EVA and POE films. No \nsignificant changes of optical transmittance as well as surface and bulk \nchemistry were detected after damp heat exposure for up to 3,000h. \nOreski et al. [18] examined the influence of DH on the degradation and \npower output of 6-cell PV modules with EVA or POE encapsulants. \nAcetic acid induced degradation of the cells encapsulated with EVA was \nconfirmed after 3,000h, whereas no significant changes were observed \nin POE-based modules. \nThe exposure of glass substrates to DH conditions triggers ageing \nmechanisms such as leaching of network cations (e.g., Na, Ca), hydro­\nlysis of the Si–O network, glass corrosion and carbonate deposition on \nthe surfaces [21]. Hence, global ageing of glass substrates and its \ninteraction with the encapsulant has also an impact on the lifetime of PV \nmodules [21–24]. Silane based coupling agents improve the adhesion \nand the resistance against DH induced degradation of EVA-glass \n* Corresponding author. Christian Doppler Laboratory for Superimposed Mechanical-Environmental Ageing of Polymeric Hybrid Laminates, University of Linz, \nAustria. \nE-mail address: martin.tiefenthaler@jku.at (M. Tiefenthaler).  \nContents lists available at ScienceDirect \nSolar Energy Materials and Solar Cells \njournal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/solmat \nhttps://doi.org/10.1016/j.solmat.2023.112602 \nReceived 10 May 2023; Received in revised form 26 September 2023; Accepted 16 October 2023   \n\n\nSolar Energy Materials and Solar Cells 264 (2024) 112602\n2\nlaminates \n[13,25]. \nThe \nlong-term \nbehavior \nof \ncovalent \nencapsulant-Si-O-Si-glass bonds is crucial for the long-term delamina­\ntion resistance of PV modules [13,25]. The EVA-glass interface is more \nprone to ageing induced delamination than the EVA-silicon cell interface \n[11]. In the early stage, the degradation mechanism of the EVA-glass \ninterface is characterized by deacetylation and β-scission reactions of \nEVA and later on by hydrolytic depolymerization [11]. Furthermore, \nsodium ion migration plays a major role associated with a significantly \nenhanced sodium content on the fractured interface [11]. \nIn a recent paper, it was clearly shown that the degradation mech­\nanisms within double-glass modules are highly dependent on the \nformulation of the encapsulation material [12]. A variety of commer­\ncially relevant and experimental encapsulation formulations has been \ninvestigated without clear differentiation and description of the stabi­\nlizer package used. Moreover, compressive shear testing subsequent to \nenvironmental exposure of double-glass laminates is gaining importance \nfor delamination characterization [26–30]. \nHence, the main objective of this paper was to evaluate and compare \nthe damp heat ageing behavior of double-glass laminates based on UV- \ntransparent EVA and POE encapsulants. The UV-transparency is ach­\nieved by adding just hindered amine light stabilizers (HALS) which gives \nthe encapsulation material more alkaline characteristics. Phenolic an­\ntioxidants, which are prone to the formation of acidic low molar mass \ndegradation products due to the ester-based linkage groups, were \navoided. Quadratic compressive shear double-glass specimens were \nlaminated and exposed for up to 10,000 h to damp heat. Subsequently \nand prior to mechanical testing, non-destructive microscopic and \nUVVISNIR spectroscopic analysis was carried out. Then, the mechanical \nbehavior was examined by compressive shear testing. Finally, the frac­\ntured surfaces and the remaining encapsulant were analyzed by differ­\nential scanning calorimetry (DSC), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) \nspectroscopy/microscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). \n2. Experimental \n2.1. Materials and laminates \nTwo peroxide crosslinking UV-transparent encapsulants based on \nethylene vinyl acetate copolymer (EVA) and polyolefin elastomer (POE) \nwere investigated. While the EVA film revealed a non-periodic topog­\nraphy, the POE had a periodic pyramidal topography. A non-thermally \ntoughened solar glass allowing for water jet cutting after lamination \nwas used in this study. By XPS, the following composition of the glass \nsurface was determined (in at%): 21.1 Si, 57.5 O, 11.1 C, 2.6 N, 5.7 Na, \n1.3 Ca and 0.7 K. Details on the chemical composition and the ther­\nmomechanical properties of the encapsulants are given in Ref. [19]. \nTwo EVA or POE films were used to prepare double-glass laminates \n(300 × 200 × 3.1 mm3) at 155 ◦C, 800 mbar for 20 min. Double-glass \nspecimens with dimensions of 25 × 25 × 7 mm3 were prepared by \nwater jet cutting. Specimens were damp heat exposed (85 ◦C, 85 %rh) in \nan environmental chamber WK3-340/40 (Weiss Umwelttechnik GmbH, \nGermany) for 0, 1,000, 2,000, 5,000 and 10,000h. Whereas 3 specimens \nwere examined for each ageing interval, only 1 specimen was available \nafter 10,000h of damp heat exposure. \n2.2. Characterization methods \nPrior to compressive shear testing, optical and structural changes \nwere assessed by direct and polarized light microscopy (DLM and PLM) \nusing a VHX 7000 (Keyence, Japan). While DLM measurements were \nperformed using full ring incident light, PLM measurements were car­\nried out in transmittance mode with the specimen placed between two \nfully crossed (0/90◦) polarizers. DLM images were taken from the \nspecimens immediately after removal from the damp heat chamber. \nThen, the glass surfaces of the specimen were polished using an auto­\nmated wet-polishing unit with fine sandpapers. Hemispheric and diffuse \ntransmittance spectra in the range from 250 to 2500 nm were recorded \nin normal transmittance mode in 5 nm steps on a Lamda 950 UVVISNIR \nspectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA) equipped with a 150 mm integrating \nsphere. The UV cut-off lengths were defined by a 10% drop in trans­\nmittance compared to the reference at 800 nm [31]. To deduce integral \nnormal-hemispheric and -diffuse transmittance values, the spectral data \nwere weighted with an AM1.5 source function [31,32]. The trans­\nmittance data were integrated in the solar (300–2000 nm) and UV \n(300–400 nm) range. Moreover, solar haze values were evaluated by \nnormalization of the diffuse with the hemispheric transmittance [33]. In \naddition to the laminates, the used glass substrate was characterized in \nthe unaged, reference state. \nShear failure is a common phenomenon related to interfacial ageing. \nWhile interlaminar shear testing methods are often applied for charac­\nterization of composites [34], in this study focus was given to \ncompressive shear testing as developed by Chapuis et al. [26] for double \nglass laminates. Compressive shear tests were carried out in tensile \nmode on an universal tensile testing machine Z020 (ZwickRoell, Ger­\nmany) equipped with a temperature chamber (see Fig. 1). The \ndisplacement rate was 1 mm/min. To prevent fracture of the glass \nsubstrate and to mimic critical service-relevant conditions, the test \ntemperature was set to 60 ◦C [27,29]. Shear loads detected by \ncompressive shear testing of double-glass laminates are at least 50 times \nhigher than service-relevant shear loads in a PV module [35]. Hence, the \nmain purpose of monotonic shear testing was to deduce ultimate me­\nchanical values allowing for quantitative comparison of the damp heat \nageing behavior of glass laminates based on the investigated \nUV-transparent EVA and POE encapsulants. Moreover, it should be \nevaluated if damp heat ageing for up to 10,000 h is associated with a \ncritical drop in ultimate shear loads below service-relevant shear \nloading conditions. \nAs illustrated in Fig. 2, the failure modes were classified as interfa­\ncial, cohesive in the encapsulant or glass and combined interfacial and \ncohesive. Remaining encapsulants on the fractured double-glass lami­\nnates were investigated by direct light microscopy (DLM). Moreover, the \ncorresponding glass sides were examined with a 3D laser confocal mi­\ncroscope LEXT OLS4000 (Olympus, Japan). Stitched 10x images of the \nentire glass surface (25 × 25 mm2) were generated. Details of the frac­\ntured surfaces and height profiles were deduced from 50x images. \nFurthermore, encapsulant samples were taken with a scalpel and \ncharacterized by differential scanning calorimetry (DSC 8500, Perki­\nnElmer, USA). Samples (< 5 mg) were placed in a perforated 3 μL \naluminum pan. Thermograms were recorded with a heating/cooling rate \nof 20 K/min under nitrogen atmosphere. The experiments consisted of a \nfirst heating step (0–200 ◦C) followed by a cooling step (200-0 ◦C) and a \nsubsequent second heating step (0–200 ◦C). During damp heat ageing, \nthe edges of the encapsulant were directly exposed to water vapor. In \nFig. 1. Compressive shear test (CST) setup: a) schematic illustration according \nto Ref. [26]; b) image of setup implemented at an universal mechanical testing \nsystem equipped with a temperature chamber. \nM. Tiefenthaler et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                           \n\n\nSolar Energy Materials and Solar Cells 264 (2024) 112602\n3\ncontrast, the ingress to the center of the laminate was diffusion \ncontrolled. To assess potential structural differences within the encap­\nsulant layer, DSC samples were taken from the edge and the center. \nFTIR spectroscopy was carried out in attenuated total reflection \n(ATR) mode using a Spectrum 100 (PerkinElmer, USA) equipped with a \ndiamond/ZnSe ATR unit. The spectra were obtained by averaging 4 \nscans ranging from 650 to 4000 cm−1 with a spectral resolution of 4 \ncm−1. For each encapsulant and exposure time, global-ATR spectra were \ncollected from the remaining encapsulant layer. Moreover, spatially \nresolved, local FTIR-ATR microscopic images with dimensions of 300 ×\n300 μm2 were recorded using a Spectrum 100 and a Spotlight 400 im­\naging system (PerkinElmer, USA) equipped with a Ge-crystal. Spectra \nwere run in the range from 720 to 4000 cm−1 with a spatial resolution of \n1.56 μm and a spectral resolution of 16 cm−1. The IR-ATR spectra were \nbaseline corrected. \nX-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) measurements were per­\nformed using a Theta Probe device and an Avantage data system \n(Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The chemical composition of fractured \nsurfaces was deduced from XPS survey scans with an Al-Kα mono­\nchromatic X-ray source (1486.6 eV) and a spot size diameter of 300 or \n400 μm. The pass energy of the hemispherical analyzer was set to 50 eV. \nFurther details as to XPS analysis are given in Ref. [36]. Special focus \nwas given to the analysis and changes of the sodium and calcium con­\ntents within the bulk encapsulant and at surfaces and interfaces of EVA \nand POE based double-glass laminates. To improve the comparability \nand decrease the effect of contaminations, measured sodium and cal­\ncium fractions were normalized by the corresponding silicon content. \nFTIR and XPS are differing significantly in their depth resolution. \nWhile a sample layer with a thickness of about 5 μm is assessed by FTIR \nin ATR-mode, XPS is a highly surface-sensitive characterization method \nwith a penetration depth of about 10 nm [37]. Hence, FTIR-ATR and XPS \nmeasurements were carried out to differentiate between ageing phe­\nnomena close to the fractured surface (by XPS) and within the usually \nplastically deformed damage zone within a few μm-distance to fractured \nsurface (by FTIR-ATR). \n3. Results and discussion \n3.1. Visual appearance and optical properties \nA silicate glass network contains bridging oxygens (BO, i.e., Si–O–Si) \nand non-bridging oxygens (NBO, e.g., Si–O-). The latter are charge \ncompensated by glass network modifier cations (e.g., Na, Ca). As well \ndiscussed in literature [21,22,24], DH exposure of silicate glass leads to \nthe formation of a surface water film and to diffusion of molecular water \ninto the bulk. The diffused water reacts with both, BO and NBO, asso­\nciated with 1) the formation of silanol groups, 2) the release of (glass) \nnetwork modifier cations, and 3) the formation of more NBO (Si–O-) \ngroups [21,22,24]. These three mechanisms are weakening the glass \nnetwork [21,24]. The network modifier cations migrate to the water \nfilm and cause a raise of the pH value by changing the electrochemical \npotential between the glass surface and the bulk. At a pH value of 9, the \nmigrated ions form water-insoluble carbonate (e.g., Na2CO3, CaCO3) \nclusters associated with permanent whitening of the surface [21]. In \nFig. 3, DLM images of non-surface polished EVA and POE laminates are \nillustrated after 0, 5,000 and 10,000h of DH exposure. Leaching induced \nsurface whitening was clearly discernible after 5,000h. The entire glass \nsurfaces of both laminates revealed a homogenous white appearance \nafter 10,000h. The surface whitening effect was not dependent on the \nencapsulant material (as shown in Fig. 3). \nMicroscopic images of surface polished EVA and POE laminates ob­\ntained by DLM (1st row) and PLM (2nd and 3rd row) after 0, 5,000, and \n10,000h of DH exposure are depicted and compared in Fig. 4. DLM \nimages revealed an increased whitening for both encapsulant types after \nFig. 2. Failure modes of compressive shear tested double-glass laminates: 1) interfacial failure at the encapsulant-glass interface, 2) cohesive failure: a) within the \nencapsulant, b) within the glass substrate. \nFig. 3. DLM images of non-surface polished EVA and POE-glass laminates \nexposed to DH for 0, 5,000 and 10,000h. \nM. Tiefenthaler et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                           \n\n\nSolar Energy Materials and Solar Cells 264 (2024) 112602\n4\n5,000h of DH exposure. The edge clearing effect was more pronounced \nfor EVA laminates. In contrast, the edges of the POE laminates appeared \nfully opaque after 10,000h (see Fig. 4). After 5,000h of DH exposure, \nspheric microdomains (maltese-crosses, d < 100 μm) were clearly \ndiscernible in the polarized light microscopic (PLM) images of both \nlaminate types (see Fig. 4). The microdomains differed in appearance, \nsize and number. Compared to POE, EVA laminates exhibited less, but \nlarger microdomains, which were presumably related to water uptake \nand clustering. The edge clearing effect of EVA laminates was dependent \non conditioning time at 23 ◦C and 50 %rh, subsequent to DH exposure. \nThe microdomains within the POE laminates were smaller and more \nhomogenous (see Fig. 4). Harris et al. [38] reported the formation of \ncalcite (CaCO3) crystals and spherolithic superstructures resulting in \nmaltese-crosses in PLM images. Most likely, the finer microdomains in \nPOE laminates could be attributed to glass ageing induced formation of \nCaCO3 and Na2CO3 crystals at the POE-glass interface [21]. Interest­\ningly, these microdomains were nucleated along the edge lines of the \npyramidal topography of the extruded POE film. In addition to the \nmaltese-cross microdomains, distinct black spots with a diameter up to \n2 mm were also discernible in POE laminates after 5,000 and 10,000h of \nDH exposure. \nIn contrast to POE, which was reported to prevent effectively ion \nmigration [17], the higher polarity of EVA favors ion diffusion into the \nbulk of the encapsulant [3,11,21]. Moreover, the damp heat induced \nhydrolysis of EVA could facilitate the formation of acetic acid, which \nwould reduce the alkalinity of the pH at the EVA-glass interface. These \ntwo mechanisms were ascribed to hinder the formation of sodium or \ncalcium carbonates at EVA-glass interfaces by keeping the pH value well \nbelow 9. \nSolar transmittance spectra of a single glass substrate and the lami­\nnates are depicted in Fig. 5. The hemispheric transmittance spectrum of \nthe glass substrate revealed an UV cut-off at 330 nm. The UV cut-off of \nEVA laminates shifted from 345 to 385 nm after 10,000h of damp heat \nexposure. A cut-off shift from 355 to 400 nm was also confirmed for POE \nlaminates. The results of the double-glass EVA laminates are in agree­\nment with findings in Refs. [35,39]. Morse et al. [39] did not detect any \nsignificant changes of the UV cut-off for POE laminates exposed to 1.5 \nMJ/m2 of solar radiation at 85 ◦C for 4,000h. The deviations within this \nwork and the findings in Ref. [39] were attributed to different ageing \nconditions. Barret et al. [4] investigated the effect of damp heat expo­\nsure on free standing crosslinked POE films and reported a decrease of \nthe hemispheric transmittance with increasing ageing durations, which \nis also in agreement with findings of this study (see Figs. 5 and 6b)). The \nlower hemispheric transmittance values of POE-based glass laminates in \nthe unaged reference state is presumably related to a stronger index of \nrefraction difference of glass and POE associated with higher reflectivity \nat the interfaces. The diffuse transmittance was significantly higher for \nthe unaged POE laminate. More pronounced scattering of POE is pre­\nsumably related to a higher degree of crystallinity and a lower cross­\nlinking density [19]. The increase in scattering at lower wavelength \nvalues is a clear indicator for scattering domains such as crystal lamellae \nwith a thickness well below 300 nm. While the lamellae thickness of PE \ncrystals is below 10 nm, the lateral dimensions of crystal lamellae are \nseveral 100 nm and hence, contributing to scattering [31]. Both lami­\nnates revealed hydrocarbon (CH) related absorption bands at 2500 to \n2200, 1800 to 1650, 1400 and 1200 nm and a small carbonyl (C=O) \nFig. 4. Direct (1st row) and polarized (2nd and 3rd row) light microscopic images of glass surface polished EVA (left) and POE (right) laminates exposed to DH for 0, \n5,000, and 10,000h. \nFig. 5. Effect of damp heat exposure (10,000h) on the hemispheric and diffuse transmittance (Th and Td) of EVA- and POE-glass laminates.  \nM. Tiefenthaler et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                           \n\n\nSolar Energy Materials and Solar Cells 264 (2024) 112602\n5\npeak at 2100 nm [31]. Moreover, damp heat aged laminates (for 10, \n000h) revealed an additional band attributed to molecular water in the \nrange from 1950 to 1900 nm [31]. Interestingly, this band was more \npronounced for POE than for EVA laminates. Presumably, a water rich \nlayer was formed in the near interface glass regions of POE laminates \ndue to excellent bulk barrier properties of POE. However, water diffu­\nsion rates were reported to be at least two magnitudes higher at the \ninterface of metal oxides and polymeric adhesives, such as epoxies \n[40–42]. Presumably, the edges of the periodic, pyramidic topography \nresulted in imperfections at the interface allowing for fast ingress of \nwater. \nNormalized hemispheric transmittance values within the solar \n(300–2000 nm) and the UV (300–400 nm) range of EVA and POE lam­\ninates are illustrated in Fig. 6a). Unaged EVA laminates revealed slightly \nhigher (+1.5%) hemispheric solar and UV transmittance values than \nPOE. Compared to unaged laminates, damp heat exposed (1,000h) EVA \nand POE specimens exhibited a decrease in the hemispheric trans­\nmittance by −3.6 and −1.1%, respectively. After 10,000h of damp heat \nageing, the hemispheric solar transmittance was significantly higher for \nEVA laminates. A pronounced drop in solar transmittance (by −5.8%) \nwas ascertained just for the POE laminate. Morse et al. [39] reported a \ndecrease of hemispheric transmittance of double-glass EVA laminates by \n−3% due to global ageing at 80 ◦C, 30 %rh for 180 days. In contrast, no \nchanges were observed for POE laminates [39]. The poorer performance \nof POE laminates in this study was presumably attributable to damp heat \ninduced glass and interface ageing effects. A decrease of the hemispheric \ntransmittance was also described for free standing and damp heat \nexposed EVA and POE films [4]. \nUnexposed EVA laminates revealed about 4.0% higher hemispheric \nUV transmittance values than POE specimens (see Fig. 6a)). Damp heat \nexposure for 10,000h caused a significant decrease (−19.0%) of the UV \ntransmittance for both, EVA and POE laminates. \nThe solar haze increased by a factor of 9 after already 1,000h of \ndamp heat exposure of EVA laminates. No significant further changes \nwere discernible at longer damp heat exposure up to 10,000h. Compared \nto unaged specimens, POE laminates exhibited 2x (1,000h) and 3x \n(10,000h) higher haze values after damp heat exposure. However, the \nincrease in haze of POE-glass was much less compared to EVA-glass. The \nageing induced haze of the laminates wa"
outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_15\codex_stderr.log:4110:outputs\polaris_graph\baseline_v2_materials\PG_LB_FS_02_materials.json:5329:  "draft_report": "# How does damp heat aging affect the adhesion strength of DVS-PEI encapsulants in flexible electronics?\n\n## Abstract\n\nThis systematic review synthesizes evidence from 19 sources examining how damp heat aging affects encapsulant adhesion strength in flexible electronics, with particular attention to the uncharacterized DVS-PEI material class. Under combined thermal-humid exposure at 95°C, adhesive joint tensile strength decreased by 54.4%, nearly double the 31.3% reduction from moisture absorption alone [6], following Arrhenius kinetics proportional to interfacial moisture concentration [3]. Thermal cycling alone (10,000 cycles, 5–50°C) produced no statistically significant effect on interfacial mechanics (p > 0.05) [4], confirming moisture as the primary degradation driver. EVA at the glass interface degrades preferentially, reaching levels equivalent to 15+ years of field exposure after 10,000 hours of accelerated aging [13]. Industry data reveal that only 69% of module configurations pass damp heat qualification [16], with 15 manufacturers experiencing failures. Formulation optimization identifies 24% VA content in PEVA [15] and 84–90% gel content in EVA [10] as durability optima, while POE demonstrates superior adhesion stability over EVA [5]. A threshold debonding energy of 160 J/m² is proposed for encapsulant interfaces [10]. The most critical gap identified is the near-complete absence of DVS-PEI-specific damp heat aging data across all dimensions—kinetics, failure modes, formulation sensitivity, and field correlation.\n\n## Encapsulant Materials and Damp Heat Test Standards\n\nInterface degradation and bulk encapsulant integrity do not necessarily track together under environmental stress. Thermal degradation at the encapsulant-substrate interface can reach significant levels even when the bulk encapsulant material itself shows negligible degradation in the absence of UV radiation and elevated humidity [REF:1]. This decoupling between interfacial and bulk failure modes underscores the need for adhesion-specific characterization rather than reliance on bulk polymer metrics alone.\n\nAmong encapsulant systems evaluated under accelerated aging conditions, cross-linking encapsulants such as ethylene vinyl acetate (EVA) and polyolefin elastomer (POE) demonstrate sensitivity to processing parameters. Higher lamination temperature proved beneficial for both peel force and gel content, with improvements observed before and after damp-heat aging [REF:2]. Adhesion between encapsulant and glass was characterized through peel tests, while gel content and yellowness index served as complementary aging indicators [REF:2]. The co-measurement of mechanical adhesion alongside chemical aging markers enables a more complete picture of encapsulant performance under stress [REF:2].\n\nThe observation that lamination temperature influences post-aging adhesion retention [REF:2] while interface degradation can proceed independently of bulk degradation [REF:1] points to a complex failure landscape. Process optimization at the lamination stage may mitigate adhesion loss, yet the interfacial degradation pathway operates through mechanisms not fully captured by bulk indicators. Current evidence is limited to EVA and POE systems; the adhesion behavior of other encapsulant families under equivalent damp-heat protocols remains an open question requiring dedicated investigation.\n\n**Key Findings**\n\n- Thermal degradation at the encapsulant-substrate interface can be significant even when the bulk encapsulant experiences negligible degradation without UV and humidity exposure [REF:1].\n- Higher lamination temperature improves both peel force and gel content for cross-linking encapsulants (EVA, POE) before and after damp-heat aging [REF:2].\n- Peel tests, gel content, and yellowness index together provide a multi-indicator assessment of adhesion and encapsulant aging [REF:2].\n- Evidence is currently limited to EVA and POE; other encapsulant families lack equivalent damp-heat adhesion characterization.\n\n## Adhesion Degradation Kinetics Under Damp Heat\n\nAdhesion energy between cell, encapsulant, and glass layers decreases under both hot-dry and hot-humid accelerated aging conditions. Coupon-level testing after 5000 h of exposure at 90°C under ~1% RH (hot-dry) and 60% RH (hot-humid) demonstrated measurable decreases in adhesion energy Gc [REF:5]. The presence of moisture amplifies the degradation pathway: tensile strength of adhesive joints decreased by approximately 31.3% after moisture absorption alone, while combined exposure to high temperature (95°C) and humidity drove losses to 54.4% [REF:6]. These quantitative results converge with kinetic modeling that describes the adhesion degradation rate as proportional to the moisture concentration at the interface, governed by an Arrhenius temperature dependence [REF:3]. Higher temperature increases both the rate of chemical reaction and the diffusion coefficient, promoting greater water penetration and further degradation of adhesive joints [REF:6].\n\nThe mechanistic picture emerging from these data distinguishes between reversible and irreversible damage modes. Interface strength reduction, the appearance of microstructure defects, and hydrolysis of the polymer matrix are all irreversible consequences of damp-heat aging [REF:6]. This irreversibility contrasts with the behavior observed under purely thermal cycling. Thermocycling over 10,000 cycles between 5°C and 50°C had no statistically significant effect (p > 0.05) on the interfacial mechanics and fracture mode of either experimental or commercial adhesives [REF:4]. The divergence between moisture-driven and thermally driven outcomes reinforces the kinetic model's emphasis on moisture concentration as the rate-controlling variable at the interface [REF:3][REF:4].\n\n| Aging Condition | Degradation Observed | Source |\n|---|---|---|\n| Hot-dry, 90°C, ~1% RH, 5000 h | Gc decreased | [REF:5] |\n| Hot-humid, 90°C, 60% RH, 5000 h | Gc decreased | [REF:5] |\n| Moisture absorption only | Tensile strength −31.3% | [REF:6] |\n| Combined 95°C + humidity | Tensile strength −54.4% | [REF:6] |\n| Thermocycling, 10,000 cycles, 5–50°C | No significant effect (p > 0.05) | [REF:4] |\n\nComparative performance across encapsulant chemistries reveals material-dependent degradation rates under damp heat. EVA laminates showed less degradation in peel force after damp heat than POE samples across the tested lamination temperatures [REF:2]. POE laminates, by contrast, revealed improved compressive shear performance after damp-heat aging compared to their unaged state [REF:7]. This apparent divergence—EVA outperforming POE in peel force retention while POE improves in shear—indicates that adhesion degradation kinetics are both material-specific and test-mode dependent [REF:2][REF:7]. Peel strength and shear strength capture different interfacial failure mechanisms, and ranking encapsulants by one metric alone risks mischaracterizing their damp-heat durability.\n\nThe kinetic framework linking degradation rate to interfacial moisture concentration and Arrhenius-type temperature dependence [REF:3] provides a unifying model, yet its applicability across all encapsulant families remains unverified. Current quantitative data concentrate on EVA and POE systems [REF:2][REF:7], with adhesion energy measurements on cell/encapsulant/glass configurations [REF:5] and tensile testing on adhesive joints exposed to combined thermo-humid conditions [REF:6]. The absence of equivalent fracture energy (kJ/m²) measurements for TPO or other polyolefin encapsulants under standardized damp-heat protocols represents a significant evidence gap. Similarly, the irreversible nature of hydrolysis-driven degradation [REF:6] raises durability questions that Arrhenius extrapolation from accelerated tests may not fully capture, particularly when the dominant failure mechanism transitions from interfacial debonding to bulk polymer degradation at longer exposure durations.\n\n**Key Findings**\n\n- Tensile strength of adhesive joints decreased by 31.3% under moisture absorption alone and by 54.4% under combined high temperature (95°C) and humidity, confirming moisture as the primary degradation driver [REF:6].\n- Adhesion energy Gc decreased after 5000 h of both hot-dry (90°C, ~1% RH) and hot-humid (90°C, 60% RH) aging on cell/encapsulant/glass coupons [REF:5].\n- Thermocycling alone (10,000 cycles, 5–50°C) had no statistically significant effect (p > 0.05) on interfacial mechanics, isolating moisture as the critical variable [REF:4].\n- EVA laminates retained peel force better than POE under damp heat [REF:2], while POE showed improved compressive shear after aging [REF:7], indicating test-mode-dependent degradation ranking.\n- Interface strength reduction, microstructure defects, and polymer matrix hydrolysis are irreversible effects of damp-heat exposure [REF:6].\n\n## Failure Mode Transitions and Interfacial Mechanisms\n\nSubstrate type and aging method exert statistically significant effects on bond strength, while the choice of adhesive approach does not. Three-way ANOVA demonstrated that substrate (p = 0.00) and aging method (p = 0.00) were significant factors, whereas adhesive approach had no significant influence (p = 0.84) [REF:8]. This finding reframes the failure mode question: degradation outcomes depend more on the interface being stressed and the environmental protocol applied than on the adhesive formulation itself [REF:8]. The resistance of resin-dentin bonds to thermal-aging degradation was material dependent [REF:11], and among the adhesive systems tested, one-step self-etch adhesives obtained the lowest microtensile bond strength (MTBS) results after both aging treatments, with significantly reduced adhesive capacity following thermocycling [REF:11]. Not all adhesive chemistries, however, succumb to aging: the experimental non-methacrylate adhesive EA 5 experienced no changes (p > 0.05) in bonding performance following the entire 1-year duration of biochemical aging [REF:4].\n\n| Factor | p-value | Significance | Source |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| Substrate | 0.00 | Significant | [REF:8] |\n| Aging method | 0.00 | Significant | [REF:8] |\n| Adhesive approach | 0.84 | Not significant | [REF:8] |\n\nThe chemical degradation pathway in EVA encapsulants under damp heat proceeds through decomposition into acetic acid, which in turn reacts with perovskite layers and causes yellow PbI2 degradation [REF:12]. This acetic acid release represents a failure mechanism specific to EVA chemistry and distinguishes it from non-acetate-containing encapsulants. At the interfacial level, EVA at the glass interface degrades more than at the cell interface, explaining why this initially stronger interface becomes the preferential site for delamination [REF:14]. The differential degradation across the two EVA interfaces [REF:14] aligns with the ANOVA finding that substrate type drives bond strength outcomes [REF:8], as the glass and cell surfaces present fundamentally different chemical environments for moisture interaction and adhesive breakdown.\n\nMaterial properties beyond chemistry further constrain encapsulant durability. PEVA18 showed inadequate transmittance and relatively high stiffness after 2000 h of damp-heat aging, discouraging its use as an encapsulant [REF:9]. Elevated stiffness after aging increases the mechanical stress at the interface during thermal cycling, potentially lowering the delamination threshold. This stiffness-mediated pathway operates alongside the chemical degradation routes documented for EVA [REF:12][REF:14] and represents an independent contributor to failure mode transitions in polyethylene-vinyl acetate variants [REF:9].\n\nThe long-term trajectory of adhesion loss provides a bridge between accelerated laboratory aging and field performance. At 10,000 hours of accelerated aging, EVA adhesion falls to a level generally seen after long-term field exposures of more than 15 years [REF:13]. The EVA manufacturer STR has suggested a minimum of 53 N/cm peel strength after lamination (measured by 180° peel) as sufficient to ensure a 20-year lifetime [REF:10]. These two data points—the 10,000-hour laboratory equivalence to >15 years of field exposure [REF:13] and the 53 N/cm durability threshold [REF:10]—together frame the practical boundary conditions. When accelerated aging drives peel strength below this manufacturer-specified floor, the encapsulant system transitions from a regime of gradual interfacial weakening to one where delamination becomes the dominant failure mode.\n\nSeveral methodological limitations constrain these findings. The statistical independence of adhesive approach from bond strength [REF:8] was demonstrated in a specific substrate-adhesive matrix and may not generalize to all encapsulant-substrate combinations encountered in flexible electronics. The EA 5 result showing zero degradation over one year of biochemical aging [REF:4] applies to a non-methacrylate formulation that differs fundamentally from the EVA and POE systems central to photovoltaic encapsulation. The EVA deacetylation pathway producing acetic acid [REF:12] is chemistry-specific and does not extend to polyolefin or thermoplastic encapsulants. Quantitative fracture energy measurements that would enable direct comparison of failure mode transitions across encapsulant families remain sparse in the available evidence.\n\n**Key Findings**\n\n- Substrate type and aging method significantly affect bond strength (p = 0.00 for both), while adhesive approach does not (p = 0.84), indicating that interfacial conditions dominate over adhesive formulation [REF:8].\n- EVA decomposes into acetic acid under damp heat, reacting with perovskite and causing PbI2 degradation, a failure mechanism specific to acetate-containing encapsulants [REF:12].\n- EVA at the glass interface degrades more than at the cell interface, making the initially stronger glass interface the preferential delamination site [REF:14].\n- At 10,000 hours of accelerated aging, EVA adhesion reaches levels equivalent to >15 years of field exposure, against a manufacturer-recommended minimum of 53 N/cm for 20-year lifetime [REF:13][REF:10].\n- The experimental non-methacrylate adhesive EA 5 showed no degradation (p > 0.05) over 1 year of biochemical aging, suggesting alternative chemistries may resist aging-induced failure modes [REF:4].\n\n## Formulation and Processing Parameters Affecting Durability\n\nVinyl acetate (VA) content exerts a decisive influence on PEVA encapsulant durability under damp-heat conditions. PEVA films with 18, 24, 33, and 40% VA content were cured at 150°C under vacuum and subjected to damp-heat aging at 85°C and 85% RH per IEC 61215 for 1000 and 2000 h [REF:9][REF:15]. PEVA with higher VA content (33% and 40%) degraded significantly after 2000 hours of damp-heat aging, resulting in significant loss of transmittance and mechanical integrity [REF:15]. Tensile strength, thermal stability, and degree of crosslinking were greater for PEVA containing lower VA content (18% and 24%) and diminished for higher VA (33% and 40%) after damp-heat aging [REF:15]. The pattern is consistent across multiple performance axes: lower VA content preserves both mechanical and thermal properties more effectively under prolonged moisture-heat exposure.\n\n| VA Content | Tensile Strength After DH | Thermal Stability After DH | Crosslinking After DH | Encapsulant Recommendation | Source |\n|---|---|---|---|---|---|\n| 18% (PEVA18) | Higher | Higher | Higher | Discouraged (stiffness, transmittance) | [REF:15][REF:9] |\n| 24% | Higher | Higher | Higher | Recommended | [REF:9][REF:15] |\n| 33% | Diminished | Diminished | Diminished | Not recommended | [REF:15] |\n| 40% | Diminished | Diminished | Diminished | Not recommended | [REF:15] |\n\nThe lowest VA formulation, however, does not represent the optimal choice despite its superior mechanical retention. PEVA18 (18% VA) showed inadequate percent transmittance and relatively high stiffness after 2000 h of damp-heat aging, discouraging its use as an encapsulant [REF:15]. This creates a formulation window: while higher VA content (33–40%) compromises durability and lower VA content (18%) compromises optical and mechanical functionality, the intermediate 24% VA formulation balances these competing requirements. Both independent investigations converge on the same recommendation—reducing VA content in PEVA to 24% for better encapsulation response against damp-heat aging [REF:9][REF:15].\n\nCrosslink density, quantified through gel content, provides a complementary parameter governing damp-heat resistance. The optimal gel content range for the EVA encapsulant materials tested was 84–90%, where laminates showed the best combined chemical, optical, and adhesion stability [REF:10]. Samples below 70% gel content showed low chemical and optical stability and weak adhesion [REF:10]. Gel content thus serves as a process-quality indicator: encapsulants laminated below the 70% threshold enter service with insufficient crosslink density to withstand hygrothermal stress, while those within the 84–90% range retain interfacial integrity through the aging protocol.\n\nThe interplay between VA content and crosslinking density defines a two-dimensional formulation space for damp-heat durability. Lower VA content (18–24%) preserves crosslinking capacity after aging [REF:15], and higher gel content (84–90%) ensures adhesion retention [REF:10]. The PEVA18 case illustrates that maximizing one parameter without considering the other leads to suboptimal outcomes: despite superior crosslinking retention at low VA, the 18% formulation fails on transmittance and stiffness criteria [REF:15]. The 24% VA target [REF:9][REF:15] combined with the 84–90% gel content window [REF:10] together define the processing envelope most likely to deliver long-term encapsulant performance.\n\nSeveral gaps limit the generalizability of these formulation guidelines. The VA content studies examined PEVA specifically [REF:9][REF:15], and the gel content optimization was conducted on a particular EVA material [REF:10]; whether the 24% VA and 84–90% gel content optima transfer to other polyolefin or thermoplastic encapsulant families remains uncharacterized. The aging protocol followed IEC 61215 at 85°C/85% RH [REF:9], a standardized but singular condition that does not capture the full range of field exposure profiles, including cyclic humidity or UV co-exposure. Dose-response data between VA content and specific degradation products (such as acetic acid yield) are absent from the available claims, limiting mechanistic interpretation of why the 24–33% VA boundary marks the transition from acceptable to unacceptable degradation.\n\n**Key Findings**\n\n- PEVA with higher VA content (33% and 40%) degraded significantly after 2000 h of damp-heat aging (85°C/85% RH), with loss of transmittance, tensile strength, thermal stability, and crosslinking [REF:15].\n- PEVA18 (18% VA) retained superior mechanical properties but showed inadequate transmittance and high stiffness after aging, ruling it out as an encapsulant [REF:15].\n- Two independent sources recommend reducing VA content to 24% for optimal damp-heat resistance [REF:9][REF:15].\n- The optimal gel content range for EVA is 84–90%, with samples below 70% showing low chemical/optical stability and weak adhesion [REF:10].\n- The intersection of 24% VA content and 84–90% gel content defines the formulation window most protective against damp-heat degradation [REF:9][REF:10][REF:15].\n\n## Field Reliability and Industry Quality Trends\n\nIndustry-scale reliability testing reveals that damp-heat performance remains a significant quality challenge across the photovoltaic module supply chain. In the 2024 PVEL scorecard, only 69% of bills of materials (BOMs) tested degraded by less than 6% power loss after damp-heat testing [REF:16]. Fifteen manufacturers experienced at least one failure during damp-heat testing in the 2024 reporting period [REF:16]. The involvement of multiple manufacturers in these failure events indicates that the problem is distributed across the supply base rather than concentrated in isolated producers.\n\n| Metric | Value | Source |\n|---|---|---|\n| BOMs with <6% power loss after DH | 69% | [REF:16] |\n| Manufacturers with at least one DH failure | 15 | [REF:16] |\n\nThe temporal dynamics of adhesion degradation under damp-heat conditions follow a non-linear trajectory that complicates durability predictions. Both field exposure and accelerated chamber aging reveal a distinct adhesion plateau that emerges during intermediate exposure periods—after 1 year in the field and after 1,000 hours in a chamber [REF:13]. This plateau behavior means that short-duration accelerated tests may overestimate the rate of long-term degradation by extrapolating from the initial steep decline phase, while the plateau phase itself does not guarantee permanent stabilization at acceptable adhesion levels.\n\nA parallel non-monotonic pattern has been observed in single lap joint strength under damp-heat aging: the strength first decreased and then rose with aging time, a trend attributed to post-curing of the adhesive making it harder [REF:6]. This recovery phenomenon contrasts with the monotonic degradation typically assumed in accelerated aging models and has implications for interpreting short-duration test results. The adhesion plateau observed in field and chamber studies [REF:13] and the post-cure-driven strength recovery in lap joints [REF:6] together indicate that adhesion evolution under damp heat is not a simple monotonic decline—transient recovery or stabilization phases intervene, potentially masking underlying progressive degradation.\n\n| Aging Stage | Adhesion Trend | Mechanism | Source |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| Initial (<1 year field / <1000 h chamber) | Declining | Moisture ingress, interfacial degradation | [REF:13] |\n| Intermediate plateau | Stabilized | Plateau formation | [REF:13] |\n| Non-monotonic (lap joints) | Decrease then rise | Post-curing hardening | [REF:6] |\n\nThe 69% pass rate in industry testing [REF:16] must be interpreted against the non-linear adhesion trajectories documented in controlled studies [REF:6][REF:13]. The plateau observed after 1,000 hours of chamber aging [REF:13] aligns with standard qualification test durations (IEC 61215 specifies 1,000 h), raising the possibility that modules passing the 1,000-hour checkpoint benefit from plateau-phase stabilization without being tested through the subsequent degradation phase that may follow at longer exposures. The post-curing recovery in lap joints [REF:6] further complicates pass/fail interpretation: a module that appears to have stabilized may simply be in a transient hardening phase.\n\nThe available evidence base for field reliability assessment carries several methodological limitations. The PVEL scorecard data [REF:16] report power degradation thresholds rather than direct adhesion measurements, requiring inference about the relationship between power loss and encapsulant adhesion failure. The adhesion plateau observation [REF:13] was derived from specific module configurations and aging protocols, and its universality across encapsulant chemistries and substrate combinations has not been established. The non-monotonic strength trend in single lap joints [REF:6] involves a post-curing mechanism that is adhesive-specific and may not apply to all encapsulant systems. Longer-duration accelerated aging studies exceeding the plateau phase, combined with direct adhesion measurements at the module level, would strengthen the link between laboratory findings and field failure prediction.\n\n**Key Findings**\n\n- Only 69% of BOMs tested in the 2024 PVEL scorecard degraded by less than 6% power loss after damp-heat testing, with 15 manufacturers experiencing at least one failure [REF:16].\n- A distinct adhesion plateau emerges after 1 year of field exposure and 1,000 hours of chamber aging, indicating non-linear degradation kinetics [REF:13].\n- Single lap joint strength follows a non-monotonic trajectory under damp-heat aging—first decreasing, then rising due to post-curing of the adhesive [REF:6].\n- The coincidence of the adhesion plateau with standard 1,000-hour qualification test durations raises questions about whether current pass/fail criteria capture long-term degradation behavior [REF:13][REF:16].\n\n## Design Implications and Research Gaps\n\nEncapsulant selection for damp-heat resistance benefits from comparative adhesion stability data across polymer families. POE coupons demonstrated the best adhesion stability, followed by EPE composites and then EVA [REF:5]. FTIR-ATR analysis showed insignificant bulk degradation in these systems, confirming the critical role of UV and moisture in accelerating encapsulant degradation at interfaces rather than in the polymer bulk [REF:5]. The POE > EPE > EVA stability ranking [REF:5], combined with the threshold debonding energy framework proposing 160 J/m² for encapsulant interfaces and 10 J/m² for backsheet interfaces to avoid delamination [REF:10], provides a quantitative basis for material selection decisions.\n\n| Encapsulant | Adhesion Stability Ranking | Debonding Energy Threshold | Source |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| POE | Best | 160 J/m² (encapsulant interface) | [REF:5][REF:10] |\n| EPE | Intermediate | 160 J/m² (encapsulant interface) | [REF:5][REF:10] |\n| EVA | Lowest | 160 J/m² (encapsulant interface) | [REF:5][REF:10] |\n| Backsheet interface | — | 10 J/m² | [REF:10] |\n\nMoisture barrier design at the module edge represents a complementary strategy to encapsulant chemistry optimization. Butyl rubber edge seal achieves two orders of magnitude lower Water Vapor Transmission Rate (WVTR) compared with UV-curable epoxies and polymeric encapsulants [REF:12]. A bond width of 1.25 cm for butyl rubber with added desiccant was modeled to be adequate for a 25-year lifetime against moisture ingress [REF:12]. The dramatic WVTR differential between butyl rubber and alternative sealants [REF:12] positions edge-seal engineering as a high-leverage intervention point: reducing moisture flux at the module perimeter directly limits the interfacial moisture concentration that kinetic models identify as the primary degradation driver.\n\nProcessing conditions prior to bonding also modulate adhesive performance. Adhesive effectiveness may increase if preheated at 36°C or above before use rather than applied at refrigerator or room temperature [REF:17]. This temperature sensitivity at the application stage implies that thermal conditioning protocols during manufacturing could influence the initial bond quality that subsequently determines damp-heat durability. Separately, dentin bond strengths of adhesive systems decreased with increasing relative humidity across six tested conditions [REF:18]. The humidity sensitivity during bond formation [REF:18] and the preheating benefit [REF:17] together highlight that the environmental conditions at the moment of encapsulant application—not only during subsequent service life—affect long-term adhesion performance.\n\nThe convergence of encapsulant stability rankings [REF:5], debonding energy thresholds [REF:10], and edge-seal moisture barriers [REF:12] defines a multi-layer design framework for damp-heat resistance. Selecting a POE-based encapsulant provides the highest adhesion stability [REF:5], ensuring that the encapsulant interface remains above the 160 J/m² delamination threshold [REF:10] for longer exposure durations. Pairing this with a butyl rubber edge seal at 1.25 cm bond width [REF:12] addresses the moisture ingress pathway from the module perimeter. Process-level controls—including preheating adhesives above 36°C [REF:17] and managing ambient humidity during lamination [REF:18]—add a third layer of protection by maximizing initial bond quality.\n\nSeveral critical research gaps limit the translation of these findings into comprehensive design guidelines for flexible electronics. DVS-PEI-specific aging data are absent from the available evidence base; the stability ranking of POE > EPE > EVA [REF:5] does not include PEI or DVS-modified formulations, leaving their damp-heat adhesion behavior uncharacterized. The debonding energy thresholds of 160 J/m² and 10 J/m² [REF:10] were proposed for specific interface configurations and have not been validated across the range of flexible substrates (polyimide, PET, PEN) used in flexible electronics. Combined stress testing—where damp heat, UV, and mechanical flexing act simultaneously—has not been systematically investigated, despite FTIR evidence that UV and moisture together are the critical accelerants [REF:5]. Long-duration field validation correlating the butyl rubber edge-seal model predictions [REF:12] with actual 25-year moisture ingress performance remains outstanding. The humidity and temperature sensitivity of initial bond formation [REF:17][REF:18] has not been quantified for encapsulant lamination processes specifically, creating uncertainty about how manufacturing environment control translates to field durability.\n\n**Key Findings**\n\n- POE demonstrates the best adhesion stability, followed by EPE and then EVA, with FTIR confirming that interface degradation rather than bulk polymer degradation drives failure under UV and moisture exposure [REF:5].\n- Threshold debonding energies of 160 J/m² for encapsulant interfaces and 10 J/m² for backsheet interfaces define quantitative delamination avoidance targets [REF:10].\n- Butyl rubber edge seal provides two orders of magnitude lower WVTR than UV-curable epoxies, with a modeled 1.25 cm bond width adequate for 25-year moisture protection [REF:12].\n- Adhesive effectiveness increases with preheating above 36°C [REF:17], and bond strength decreases with increasing relative humidity during application [REF:18], indicating manufacturing environment as a durability variable.\n- DVS-PEI-specific damp-heat aging data, combined stress testing, and long-duration field validation of edge-seal performance represent the most pressing research gaps for flexible electronics encapsulation.\n\n## References\n\n[1] Liu K, Miller D, Bosco N (2025). outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_15\codex_stderr.log:4111:outputs\polaris_graph\baseline_v2_materials\PG_LB_FS_02_materials.json:5330:  "final_report": "# How does damp heat aging affect the adhesion strength of DVS-PEI encapsulants in flexible electronics?\n\n## Abstract\n\nThis systematic review synthesizes evidence from 19 sources examining how damp heat aging affects encapsulant adhesion strength in flexible electronics, with particular attention to the uncharacterized DVS-PEI material class. Under combined thermal-humid exposure at 95°C, adhesive joint tensile strength decreased by 54.4%, nearly double the 31.3% reduction from moisture absorption alone [6], following Arrhenius kinetics proportional to interfacial moisture concentration [3]. Thermal cycling alone (10,000 cycles, 5–50°C) produced no statistically significant effect on interfacial mechanics (p > 0.05) [4], confirming moisture as the primary degradation driver. EVA at the glass interface degrades preferentially, reaching levels equivalent to 15+ years of field exposure after 10,000 hours of accelerated aging [13]. Industry data reveal that only 69% of module configurations pass damp heat qualification [16], with 15 manufacturers experiencing failures. Formulation optimization identifies 24% VA content in PEVA [15] and 84–90% gel content in EVA [10] as durability optima, while POE demonstrates superior adhesion stability over EVA [5]. A threshold debonding energy of 160 J/m² is proposed for encapsulant interfaces [10]. The most critical gap identified is the near-complete absence of DVS-PEI-specific damp heat aging data across all dimensions—kinetics, failure modes, formulation sensitivity, and field correlation.\n\n## Encapsulant Materials and Damp Heat Test Standards\n\nInterface degradation and bulk encapsulant integrity do not necessarily track together under environmental stress. Thermal degradation at the encapsulant-substrate interface can reach significant levels even when the bulk encapsulant material itself shows negligible degradation in the absence of UV radiation and elevated humidity [REF:1]. This decoupling between interfacial and bulk failure modes underscores the need for adhesion-specific characterization rather than reliance on bulk polymer metrics alone.\n\nAmong encapsulant systems evaluated under accelerated aging conditions, cross-linking encapsulants such as ethylene vinyl acetate (EVA) and polyolefin elastomer (POE) demonstrate sensitivity to processing parameters. Higher lamination temperature proved beneficial for both peel force and gel content, with improvements observed before and after damp-heat aging [REF:2]. Adhesion between encapsulant and glass was characterized through peel tests, while gel content and yellowness index served as complementary aging indicators [REF:2]. The co-measurement of mechanical adhesion alongside chemical aging markers enables a more complete picture of encapsulant performance under stress [REF:2].\n\nThe observation that lamination temperature influences post-aging adhesion retention [REF:2] while interface degradation can proceed independently of bulk degradation [REF:1] points to a complex failure landscape. Process optimization at the lamination stage may mitigate adhesion loss, yet the interfacial degradation pathway operates through mechanisms not fully captured by bulk indicators. Current evidence is limited to EVA and POE systems; the adhesion behavior of other encapsulant families under equivalent damp-heat protocols remains an open question requiring dedicated investigation.\n\n**Key Findings**\n\n- Thermal degradation at the encapsulant-substrate interface can be significant even when the bulk encapsulant experiences negligible degradation without UV and humidity exposure [REF:1].\n- Higher lamination temperature improves both peel force and gel content for cross-linking encapsulants (EVA, POE) before and after damp-heat aging [REF:2].\n- Peel tests, gel content, and yellowness index together provide a multi-indicator assessment of adhesion and encapsulant aging [REF:2].\n- Evidence is currently limited to EVA and POE; other encapsulant families lack equivalent damp-heat adhesion characterization.\n\n## Adhesion Degradation Kinetics Under Damp Heat\n\nAdhesion energy between cell, encapsulant, and glass layers decreases under both hot-dry and hot-humid accelerated aging conditions. Coupon-level testing after 5000 h of exposure at 90°C under ~1% RH (hot-dry) and 60% RH (hot-humid) demonstrated measurable decreases in adhesion energy Gc [REF:5]. The presence of moisture amplifies the degradation pathway: tensile strength of adhesive joints decreased by approximately 31.3% after moisture absorption alone, while combined exposure to high temperature (95°C) and humidity drove losses to 54.4% [REF:6]. These quantitative results converge with kinetic modeling that describes the adhesion degradation rate as proportional to the moisture concentration at the interface, governed by an Arrhenius temperature dependence [REF:3]. Higher temperature increases both the rate of chemical reaction and the diffusion coefficient, promoting greater water penetration and further degradation of adhesive joints [REF:6].\n\nThe mechanistic picture emerging from these data distinguishes between reversible and irreversible damage modes. Interface strength reduction, the appearance of microstructure defects, and hydrolysis of the polymer matrix are all irreversible consequences of damp-heat aging [REF:6]. This irreversibility contrasts with the behavior observed under purely thermal cycling. Thermocycling over 10,000 cycles between 5°C and 50°C had no statistically significant effect (p > 0.05) on the interfacial mechanics and fracture mode of either experimental or commercial adhesives [REF:4]. The divergence between moisture-driven and thermally driven outcomes reinforces the kinetic model's emphasis on moisture concentration as the rate-controlling variable at the interface [REF:3][REF:4].\n\n| Aging Condition | Degradation Observed | Source |\n|---|---|---|\n| Hot-dry, 90°C, ~1% RH, 5000 h | Gc decreased | [REF:5] |\n| Hot-humid, 90°C, 60% RH, 5000 h | Gc decreased | [REF:5] |\n| Moisture absorption only | Tensile strength −31.3% | [REF:6] |\n| Combined 95°C + humidity | Tensile strength −54.4% | [REF:6] |\n| Thermocycling, 10,000 cycles, 5–50°C | No significant effect (p > 0.05) | [REF:4] |\n\nComparative performance across encapsulant chemistries reveals material-dependent degradation rates under damp heat. EVA laminates showed less degradation in peel force after damp heat than POE samples across the tested lamination temperatures [REF:2]. POE laminates, by contrast, revealed improved compressive shear performance after damp-heat aging compared to their unaged state [REF:7]. This apparent divergence—EVA outperforming POE in peel force retention while POE improves in shear—indicates that adhesion degradation kinetics are both material-specific and test-mode dependent [REF:2][REF:7]. Peel strength and shear strength capture different interfacial failure mechanisms, and ranking encapsulants by one metric alone risks mischaracterizing their damp-heat durability.\n\nThe kinetic framework linking degradation rate to interfacial moisture concentration and Arrhenius-type temperature dependence [REF:3] provides a unifying model, yet its applicability across all encapsulant families remains unverified. Current quantitative data concentrate on EVA and POE systems [REF:2][REF:7], with adhesion energy measurements on cell/encapsulant/glass configurations [REF:5] and tensile testing on adhesive joints exposed to combined thermo-humid conditions [REF:6]. The absence of equivalent fracture energy (kJ/m²) measurements for TPO or other polyolefin encapsulants under standardized damp-heat protocols represents a significant evidence gap. Similarly, the irreversible nature of hydrolysis-driven degradation [REF:6] raises durability questions that Arrhenius extrapolation from accelerated tests may not fully capture, particularly when the dominant failure mechanism transitions from interfacial debonding to bulk polymer degradation at longer exposure durations.\n\n**Key Findings**\n\n- Tensile strength of adhesive joints decreased by 31.3% under moisture absorption alone and by 54.4% under combined high temperature (95°C) and humidity, confirming moisture as the primary degradation driver [REF:6].\n- Adhesion energy Gc decreased after 5000 h of both hot-dry (90°C, ~1% RH) and hot-humid (90°C, 60% RH) aging on cell/encapsulant/glass coupons [REF:5].\n- Thermocycling alone (10,000 cycles, 5–50°C) had no statistically significant effect (p > 0.05) on interfacial mechanics, isolating moisture as the critical variable [REF:4].\n- EVA laminates retained peel force better than POE under damp heat [REF:2], while POE showed improved compressive shear after aging [REF:7], indicating test-mode-dependent degradation ranking.\n- Interface strength reduction, microstructure defects, and polymer matrix hydrolysis are irreversible effects of damp-heat exposure [REF:6].\n\n## Failure Mode Transitions and Interfacial Mechanisms\n\nSubstrate type and aging method exert statistically significant effects on bond strength, while the choice of adhesive approach does not. Three-way ANOVA demonstrated that substrate (p = 0.00) and aging method (p = 0.00) were significant factors, whereas adhesive approach had no significant influence (p = 0.84) [REF:8]. This finding reframes the failure mode question: degradation outcomes depend more on the interface being stressed and the environmental protocol applied than on the adhesive formulation itself [REF:8]. The resistance of resin-dentin bonds to thermal-aging degradation was material dependent [REF:11], and among the adhesive systems tested, one-step self-etch adhesives obtained the lowest microtensile bond strength (MTBS) results after both aging treatments, with significantly reduced adhesive capacity following thermocycling [REF:11]. Not all adhesive chemistries, however, succumb to aging: the experimental non-methacrylate adhesive EA 5 experienced no changes (p > 0.05) in bonding performance following the entire 1-year duration of biochemical aging [REF:4].\n\n| Factor | p-value | Significance | Source |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| Substrate | 0.00 | Significant | [REF:8] |\n| Aging method | 0.00 | Significant | [REF:8] |\n| Adhesive approach | 0.84 | Not significant | [REF:8] |\n\nThe chemical degradation pathway in EVA encapsulants under damp heat proceeds through decomposition into acetic acid, which in turn reacts with perovskite layers and causes yellow PbI2 degradation [REF:12]. This acetic acid release represents a failure mechanism specific to EVA chemistry and distinguishes it from non-acetate-containing encapsulants. At the interfacial level, EVA at the glass interface degrades more than at the cell interface, explaining why this initially stronger interface becomes the preferential site for delamination [REF:14]. The differential degradation across the two EVA interfaces [REF:14] aligns with the ANOVA finding that substrate type drives bond strength outcomes [REF:8], as the glass and cell surfaces present fundamentally different chemical environments for moisture interaction and adhesive breakdown.\n\nMaterial properties beyond chemistry further constrain encapsulant durability. PEVA18 showed inadequate transmittance and relatively high stiffness after 2000 h of damp-heat aging, discouraging its use as an encapsulant [REF:9]. ```yaml
verdict: REQUEST_CHANGES
novel_p0: []
continuing_p0: []
p1:
  - id: P1-1
    finding: "The signed-bundle leg is mapped to the wrong artifact. `/runs/[runId]` and `/inspector/[runId]` export JSON via `downloadBundleAsJson`, while the actual signed bundle endpoint is `/api/v6/runs/{run_id}/bundle.tar.gz`. Unlinking `/generation` also removes the only current frontend signed-bundle affordance."
    required_change: "Add a canonical journey affordance for the signed `.tar.gz` bundle, preferably on run/inspector pages, using `/runs/{runId}/bundle.tar.gz`; keep JSON export clearly secondary if retained."

  - id: P1-2
    finding: "The brief claims the integrated report already renders on `/runs/[runId]`, but that page only shows run status, actions, and live SSE events. The report surface is currently `/inspector/[runId]`."
    required_change: "Either map integrated report to the inspector default view and make the journey/test assert that, or add a real report panel to the run page. Do not claim existing run-page report rendering."

  - id: P1-3
    finding: "The follow-up/compare leg is not acceptance-correct. Follow-up is explicitly in #510 and backend substrate exists at `POST /runs/{run_id}/followup`; compare substrate exists at `GET /runs/{left}/compare/{right}`. Mapping compare to `/benchmark` is semantically wrong; benchmark compares POLARIS vs external systems, not two runs."
    required_change: "Carve minimal product UI/navigation for actual follow-up and actual run compare, or explicitly remove those legs from #510 acceptance. As written, follow-up is not acceptable as out-of-scope."

  - id: P1-4
    finding: "The test deliverables only add a new demo spec, but existing e2e specs still assert the old `/intake` slice-era journey: `demo_walkthrough`, `landing_template_grid`, `command_palette`, and `command_palette_suggest` will fail after the proposed `/dashboard?template=` repair."
    required_change: "Update or supersede the stale specs in the same PR; do not leave the old slice-era walkthrough as an expected path."

p2:
  - "If `/runs/[runId]/graph` remains listed as a journey page, add a contextual inspector/run link to it; today it only has a back-link from graph to inspector."
  - "Mounting `GlobalNav` in layout will duplicate the landing `HomeKeyboardShell` header unless the home header is folded into the global nav or deliberately exempted."
  - "The new journey test should assert the rendered journey-page link surface, not just nav links, so regressions like `/benchmark → /generation` are caught."

convergence_call: continue
remaining_blockers_for_execution:
  - "Wire signed `.tar.gz` bundle export into the canonical run/inspector journey."
  - "Correct the integrated-report route mapping."
  - "Provide real follow-up and run-compare navigation, or change the issue scope."
  - "Update stale Playwright specs that still require `/intake`."
